# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**Define industrial ergonomics and list any four of its key sub-areas.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial ergonomics** is the branch of ergonomics that applies the principles of human anatomy, physiology, biomechanics, and anthropometry to the design of industrial workplaces, workstations, tools, equipment, and work methods — with the goal of maximizing worker safety, comfort, productivity, and overall system efficiency.

**Four key sub-areas of industrial ergonomics:**

1. **Workstation design** — sizing tables, chairs, consoles, and operator seats based on anthropometric percentiles.
2. **Manual material handling** — safe lifting, carrying, pushing, and pulling tasks evaluated using the NIOSH Lifting Equation.
3. **Tools and equipment design** — grip geometry, handle diameter, vibration-dampened tools to prevent Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome (HAVS).
4. **Work physiology** — managing fatigue, work-rest cycles, shift schedules, and metabolic energy expenditure.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for sub-areas)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) in the NIOSH Lifting Equation? State its value under ideal conditions.**

**Model Answer:**

The Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) is the **maximum weight that nearly all healthy industrial workers (approximately 75% of the female and 99% of the male working population) can lift safely** in a specific industrial task without an increased risk of lifting-related lower-back injury.

Under ideal lifting conditions (two-handed, symmetrical lift, no twisting, hands close to body, optimal vertical height of 75 cm, short vertical travel, low frequency, and good coupling), the **Load Constant (LC) = 23 kg**, and because all six multipliers equal 1.0, **RWL = 23 kg**.

*(3 marks: 2 marks for definition + 1 mark for LC value)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Every Part B question carries 14 marks, split into two 7-mark sub-parts (e.g., a & b). Both questions below represent an internal choice pair.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - June 2024 — Module 1]

**(a)** Explain the **various disciplines of ergonomics** as classified by the International Ergonomics Association (IEA). State at least **five disciplines** with one-line definitions each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A worker in a packaging industry lifts a carton weighing **L = 15 kg** from a pallet to a conveyor. The task parameters are: horizontal distance **H = 30 cm**, vertical height of hands at start **V = 50 cm**, vertical travel **D = 40 cm**, asymmetry angle **A = 45°**, frequency **F = 2 lifts/min** for duration **≤ 2 hours**, and coupling quality **fair (CM = 0.95)**. Compute the **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** and **Lifting Index (LI)** using the NIOSH 1991 equation, and recommend whether the task is safe. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The IEA classifies ergonomics into the following disciplines:

1. **Physical Ergonomics** — Concerned with human anatomical, anthropometric, physiological, and biomechanical characteristics as they relate to physical activity.
2. **Cognitive Ergonomics** — Concerned with mental processes such as perception, attention, cognition, memory, reasoning, and motor response, as they affect interactions among humans and other system elements.
3. **Organizational Ergonomics** — Concerned with the optimization of socio-technical systems, including organizational structures, policies, and processes (e.g., shift work, teamwork, virtual organizations).
4. **Environmental Ergonomics** — Concerned with the interaction between humans and the physical environment (heat, cold, noise, vibration, lighting, air quality).
5. **Industrial Ergonomics** — Concerned with the design of industrial workplaces, workstations, tools, equipment, and methods to match the worker, ensuring safety, health, and productivity.

*(7 marks: ~1.4 marks per discipline with one-line definition)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Step-by-Step NIOSH Computation

**Given:** LC = 23 kg, L = 15 kg, H = 30 cm, V = 50 cm, D = 40 cm, A = 45°, F = 2 lifts/min, CM = 0.95.

**Step 1: Horizontal Multiplier (HM)**

$$
HM = \frac{25}{H + 25} = \frac{25}{30 + 25} = \frac{25}{55} = 0.4545
$$

**Step 2: Vertical Multiplier (VM)**

$$
VM = 1 - \left(0.0075 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert\right) = 1 - (0.0075 \cdot \vert 50 - 75 \vert) = 1 - (0.0075 \cdot 25)
$$

$$
VM = 1 - 0.1875 = 0.8125
$$

**Step 3: Distance Multiplier (DM)**

$$
DM = 0.82 + \frac{1.8}{D} = 0.82 + \frac{1.8}{40} = 0.82 + 0.045 = 0.865
$$

**Step 4: Asymmetric Multiplier (AM)**

$$
AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A = 1 - (0.0032 \cdot 45) = 1 - 0.144 = 0.856
$$

**Step 5: Frequency Multiplier (FM)**

For F = 2 lifts/min, duration ≤ 2 h, V = 50 cm (moderate vertical range), FM from NIOSH table:

$$
FM = 0.91
$$

**Step 6: Coupling Multiplier (CM)**

$$
CM = 0.95 \text{ (given, fair coupling)}
$$

**Step 7: Compute RWL**

$$
RWL = 23 \times 0.4545 \times 0.8125 \times 0.865 \times 0.856 \times 0.91 \times 0.95
$$

Computing stepwise:

$$
23 \times 0.4545 = 10.4535 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
10.4535 \times 0.8125 = 8.4935 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
8.4935 \times 0.865 = 7.3469 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
7.3469 \times 0.856 = 6.289 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
6.289 \times 0.91 = 5.723 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
5.723 \times 0.95 = 5.437 \text{ kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{RWL \approx 5.44 \text{ kg}}
$$

**Step 8: Compute Lifting Index (LI)**

$$
LI = \frac{L}{RWL} = \frac{15}{5.44} = 2.76
$$

**Step 9: Engineering Recommendation**

| **LI** | **Risk Category** | **Action** |
|---|---|---|
| ≤ 1.0 | Low | Acceptable |
| 1.0 to 3.0 | Moderate | Engineering redesign recommended |
| > 3.0 | High | Immediate redesign mandatory |

Since **LI = 2.76** falls in the **Moderate Risk** range, the task is **unsafe for sustained industrial operation** and requires engineering controls such as reducing horizontal distance, providing better coupling, or eliminating the asymmetric twist.

*(7 marks: 5 marks for multiplier computation + 1 mark for RWL + 1 mark for recommendation)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 1]

**(a)** Discuss the role of **anthropometry** in industrial ergonomics. Explain the difference between **static and dynamic anthropometry** with two examples each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A forging industry worker performs an **8-hour shift** doing repetitive hammer operations. His resting heart rate is **70 bpm** and his working heart rate is **120 bpm**. The metabolic cost of the task is estimated at **2.8 METs** and his body mass is **70 kg**. Calculate the **cardiac cost** and the **total energy expenditure** for the shift. State whether the workload is acceptable per ISO 8996. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Role of Anthropometry in Industrial Ergonomics:**

Anthropometry is the science of measuring human body dimensions — weight, stature, limb lengths, joint ranges, and functional reach. In industrial ergonomics, anthropometric data is essential to ensure that workstations, machinery, tools, controls, and personal protective equipment are designed to fit the **diversity of the human workforce**, from the 5th percentile female to the 95th percentile male user. Without anthropometric data, designers would either over-fit the design to one specific body (excluding most workers) or under-fit it (causing discomfort, poor posture, and injury).

**Static Anthropometry:**
Measurements of the **stationary body** in fixed, standardized postures (e.g., standing height, sitting height, shoulder breadth, elbow rest height, popliteal height, buttock-knee length). These are measured with the subject motionless, typically using an anthropometer or caliper.

- *Example 1:* **Sitting height** — used to design the height of seats, eye-level for displays, and overhead clearance in operator cabins.
- *Example 2:* **Elbow height** — used to design the height of work surfaces, control consoles, and armrests.

**Dynamic Anthropometry:**
Measurements of the body **in motion or in functional working postures** (e.g., forward reach, overhead reach, functional leg length, grip diameter, arm swing, body rotation angle). These reflect what the body can actually do during work, not just in a static posture.

- *Example 1:* **Forward functional reach** — used to design the placement of frequently used controls, tools, and parts bins at the assembly line.
- *Example 2:* **Grip diameter inside the hand (functional grasp)** — used to design the optimal handle diameter of screwdrivers, hammers, and pliers.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for role + 3 marks for static vs dynamic distinction + 2 marks for examples)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Step-by-Step Physiological Computation

**Step 1: Compute the Cardiac Cost (CC)**

Cardiac cost is the difference between working and resting heart rate:

$$
CC = HR_{\text{work}} - HR_{\text{rest}} = 120 - 70
$$

$$
\boxed{CC = 50 \text{ bpm}}
$$

**Acceptable Limit:** The ISO 8996 / OCRA guideline is that **sustained CC should not exceed 30–35 bpm** for an 8-h shift without rest breaks. A CC of 50 bpm indicates **high cardiovascular strain**.

**Step 2: Compute the Basal Metabolic Energy (8 hours)**

For a 70 kg male, the resting metabolic rate is approximately:

$$
BMR \approx 1 \text{ kcal/kg/h} = 70 \text{ kcal/h}
$$

$$
\text{8-h BMR} = 70 \times 8 = 560 \text{ kcal}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Activity Energy Expenditure**

A MET (Metabolic Equivalent of Task) is defined as the energy cost of sitting quietly = approximately **1 kcal/kg/h**. For a 2.8 MET task:

$$
\text{Activity Rate} = 2.8 \times 70 = 196 \text{ kcal/h}
$$

$$
\text{8-h Activity Energy} = 196 \times 8 = 1568 \text{ kcal}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Total Daily Energy Expenditure**

$$
\text{Total} = \text{BMR} + \text{Activity} = 560 + 1568
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Energy} = 2128 \text{ kcal/day}}
$$

**Step 5: Acceptability Decision per ISO 8996**

The ISO 8996 upper limit for **sustained heavy industrial work over 8 hours is approximately 2000 kcal/day** (≈ 8.37 MJ/day).

Since **2128 kcal/day > 2000 kcal/day**, the workload is **NOT acceptable** for a continuous 8-hour shift without rest breaks.

**Recommendation:** Introduce **frequent short rest breaks (e.g., 5 min every 30 min)**, rotate the worker to a lighter task every 2 hours, or provide mechanical assist devices to reduce the metabolic cost below 2.0 METs.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for CC + 3 marks for total energy + 2 marks for decision and recommendation)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PBIET604 Module 1:**
> 1. **Using LC = 40 kg instead of 23 kg.** The 1981 original NIOSH equation used 40 kg, but the 1991 revised equation (which KTU accepts) uses **23 kg**. Writing 40 kg in a 2024 scheme exam will lose full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting one of the six multipliers** (HM, VM, DM, AM, FM, CM). KTU examiners specifically check that **all six** are present. Missing one = full marks lost.
> 3. **Confusing BMI and BMR.** BMR (Basal Metabolic Rate) is kcal/h; BMI (Body Mass Index) is kg/m². They are unrelated.
> 4. **Wrong unit conversion in energy:** 1 kcal = 4.184 kJ. Students often write "2000 kJ" instead of "2000 kcal/day". The ISO 8996 limit is **kcal/day**, not kJ/day.
> 5. **Cardiac Cost formula confusion:** CC is **working HR minus resting HR**, NOT working HR alone. Writing "120 bpm" without subtracting 70 will lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Anthropometric percentile misuse:** Industrial design for general population uses **5th percentile female** (for minimum reach/access) and **95th percentile male** (for maximum clearance). Mixing these up loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU PBIET604 — Module 1 (Industrial Ergonomics)**

- **Disciplines of Ergonomics (IEA):** Physical, Cognitive, Organizational, Environmental, Industrial — five major branches.
- **Industrial Ergonomics Definition:** Application of ergonomic principles to design of industrial workplaces, workstations, tools, and methods to fit the worker.
- **Anthropometry:** Scientific study of human body measurements; classified into **Static** (stationary) and **Dynamic** (in motion) anthropometry.
- **Design Percentiles:** Use **5th percentile female** for minimum reach and **95th percentile male** for clearance in industrial workstation design.
- **NIOSH Lifting Equation (1991 Revised):** RWL = 23 × HM × VM × DM × AM × FM × CM, all units in kg.
- **Load Constant (LC) = 23 kg**, NOT 40 kg.
- **Lifting Index (LI) = L / RWL.** LI ≤ 1.0 = safe; 1.0–3.0 = moderate risk; > 3.0 = high risk.
- **Horizontal Multiplier (HM) = 25 / (H + 25)**, where H is in cm.
- **Vertical Multiplier (VM) = 1 − 0.0075 · |V − 75|**, with optimal V = 75 cm.
- **Distance Multiplier (DM) = 0.82 + 1.8 / D**.
- **Asymmetric Multiplier (AM) = 1 − 0.0032 · A**, where A is in degrees.
- **Frequency Multiplier (FM):** Read from NIOSH table; reduces with higher lifting rates.
- **Coupling Multiplier (CM):** 0.90 (poor), 0.95 (fair), 1.00 (good).
- **Cardiac Cost (CC) = HR_work − HR_rest**, acceptable limit ≤ 30 bpm for 8 h.
- **Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR) ≈ 1 kcal/kg/h.** 8-h BMR for 70 kg = 560 kcal.
- **Total Daily Energy Limit:** ≤ 2000 kcal/day (≈ 8.37 MJ/day) per ISO 8996.
- **MET definition:** 1 MET = 1 kcal/kg/h (energy cost of quiet sitting).
- **Common Industrial Disorders:** CTS (Carpal Tunnel Syndrome), Tendinitis, Lower Back Pain (LBP), Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome (HAVS).
- **Indian Standards:** IS 7250 (anthropometric measurements), IS 14746 (workstation design).
- **Practical Use:** Always compute LI first before redesign; aim to bring LI ≤ 1.0 through engineering controls.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Visual ergonomics and organizational ergonomics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET604 - APPLIED ERGONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Visual ergonomics and organizational ergonomics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Visual Ergonomics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> **Visual Ergonomics** is the sub-discipline of ergonomics concerned with the interaction between the human visual system and the visual environment at the workplace. It encompasses the study of visual capabilities, limitations, and the design of lighting, displays, workstations, and signage to optimize visual performance, visual comfort, safety, and productivity while minimizing visual fatigue and ocular disorders.

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Ergonomics = "Fitting the LIGHT to the EYE and the EYE to the TASK."**

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Headlight Workshop" Picture

Imagine a mechanic working under a single fluorescent tube that flickers and casts harsh shadows across the engine. Within 30 minutes, his eyes burn, he squints, misreads the dial gauge, and makes an error. Now imagine the SAME mechanic under soft, glare-free, well-diffused lighting with the gauge backlit — he works comfortably for hours without strain.

The **task** (mechanic + engine) didn't change. Only the **visual environment** changed. That invisible upgrade is *Visual Ergonomics*. It is the "invisible hand" that decides whether a job is performed with **clarity** or **eye-strain**.

### Core Sub-Areas of Visual Ergonomics

1. **Anatomy & Physiology of the Eye** — cornea, iris, lens, retina, fovea, optic nerve.
2. **Visual Functions** — acuity, accommodation, convergence, adaptation, color vision, stereopsis.
3. **Illumination Engineering** — illuminance, luminance, contrast, glare, color rendering.
4. **Visual Display Design** — VDTs (monitors, tablets), fonts, symbols, screen ergonomics.
5. **Workplace Lighting Design** — task lighting, ambient lighting, emergency lighting.
6. **Visual Fatigue & Disorders** — asthenopia, computer vision syndrome, presbyopia.

---

## 1.2 Organizational Ergonomics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (IEA / KTU 2024 Aligned):**
> **Organizational Ergonomics** (also called *Macroergonomics*) is the branch of ergonomics concerned with the optimization of socio-technical systems — including organizational structures, policies, processes, communication networks, work schedules, job design, and corporate culture — to improve overall system performance, employee well-being, and quality of working life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Organizational Ergonomics = "Fitting the ORGANIZATION to the HUMAN, and the HUMAN to the ORGANIZATION."**

### Intuitive Analogy — The "Stadium Crowd" Picture

Think of a cricket stadium. The match fails not because of one bad player, but because:
- The **schedule** clashes with peak fatigue times.
- The **communication** between captain and bowlers is poor.
- The **roles** of each fielder are unclear.
- The **shift changes** (batting order) don't consider player stamina.

Now imagine if you re-designed the *rules, rotations, communication protocols, and rest cycles* so every player is at peak at the right moment. The whole TEAM performs better. That redesign is *Organizational Ergonomics*.

### Core Sub-Areas of Organizational Ergonomics

1. **Work Organization** — task allocation, division of labor, supervision.
2. **Work Schedules & Shift Work** — circadian rhythms, night-shift effects.
3. **Job Design & Rotation** — job enlargement, enrichment, rotation.
4. **Communication & Cooperation** — formal/informal networks, team dynamics.
5. **Organizational Culture & Quality** — TQM, participative ergonomics, safety culture.
6. **Socio-Technical Systems Design** — balancing the social and technical sub-systems.
7. **Work-Related Stress & Workload Management** — burnout, recovery, work-life balance.

### The Linkage Between the Two

| Aspect | Visual Ergonomics | Organizational Ergonomics |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus Level** | Individual operator + task | Whole organization + society |
| **Scale** | Micro-ergonomics | Macro-ergonomics |
| **Key Human Factor** | Eyes & visual perception | Mind, body, social systems |
| **Design Output** | Lighting layout, display spec | Shift roster, job description |
| **Time Horizon** | Seconds to hours | Days to years |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** When asked to "differentiate," always use a **tabular format** — it scores full marks under KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Visual Ergonomics — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 The Human Eye — Functional Anatomy

The eye behaves like a precision **optical instrument**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cornea} &\rightarrow \text{Provides } \sim 2/3 \text{ of total refractive power (} \approx +43 \text{ D)} \\
\text{Iris} &\rightarrow \text{Adjusts pupil diameter (2 mm to 8 mm)} \\
\text{Lens} &\rightarrow \text{Accommodation by changing curvature (} +15 \text{ D to } +20 \text{ D)} \\
\text{Retina} &\rightarrow \text{Photoreceptor layer (rods } \sim 120 \text{ million, cones } \sim 6 \text{ million)} \\
\text{Fovea} &\rightarrow \text{Highest acuity region, cone-only, } \sim 1.5^\circ \text{ angular size}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.1.2 Key Visual Functions

- **Visual Acuity** — Ability to discriminate fine detail.
- **Accommodation** — Change of lens power to focus on near/far objects.
- **Adaptation** — Adjustment of retinal sensitivity to light levels.
- **Accommodation Range** — Distance between *near point* (Punctum Proximum) and *far point* (Punctum Remotum).

### 2.1.3 The Aging Eye — Presbyopia

After ~40 years, lens elasticity decreases → near point recedes from ~25 cm to ~50 cm or more. This is why older workers need **task lighting**, **magnification**, and **adjustable workstations**.

### 2.1.4 Lighting Fundamentals

| Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Luminous Flux | $\Phi$ | lumen (lm) | Total light emitted by a source |
| Luminous Intensity | $I$ | candela (cd) | Flux per unit solid angle |
| **Illuminance** | $E$ | **lux (lx)** | **Flux incident per unit area** |
| **Luminance** | $L$ | **cd/m²** | **Brightness perceived by the eye** |
| Contrast | $C$ | dimensionless | Ratio of luminance difference |
| Visual Angle | $\theta$ | minutes of arc (arcmin) | Angle subtended at the eye |

### 2.1.5 Glare — The Visual Ergonomics Killer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Glare** is a visual sensation caused by **brightnesses within the visual field that are excessively greater than the luminance to which the eyes are adapted**, causing discomfort (Disability Glare) or temporary vision loss (Disability Glare).

**Types of Glare:**
- **Disability Glare** — reduces visibility (e.g., oncoming headlights).
- **Discomfort Glare** — causes annoyance & fatigue (e.g., unshaded ceiling lights).
- **Direct Glare** — source directly in line of sight.
- **Reflected Glare** — reflected off glossy surfaces (VDT screens!).
- **Cumulative Glare Effect** — repeated low-level exposure over the day.

### 2.1.6 Visual Fatigue (Asthenopia)

Symptoms: burning eyes, blurred vision, headache, double vision, dry eyes. Today grouped under **Computer Vision Syndrome (CVS)** or **Digital Eye Strain**.

Causes:
- Inadequate lighting.
- Poor contrast on screens.
- Improper viewing distance (< 40 cm or > 70 cm).
- No anti-glare measures.
- Continuous screen time without micro-breaks.

### 2.1.7 Recommended Illuminance Levels (IS/ISO/CIE)

| Task Category | Illuminance (lux) | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Casual | 100 – 200 | Corridors, lobbies |
| Ordinary | 200 – 500 | Offices, classrooms |
| Detailed | 500 – 1000 | Drawing offices, inspection |
| Very Detailed | 1000 – 2000 | Fine assembly, electronics |
| Extremely Detailed | > 2000 | Watch-making, surgery |

---

## 2.2 Organizational Ergonomics — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Socio-Technical Systems (STS) Theory

Proposed by **Trist & Bamforth (1951)** at the Tavistock Institute (UK) — based on coal-mining studies. The organization has TWO interlocking sub-systems:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Social Sub-system} &\rightarrow \text{People, skills, attitudes, values, rewards} \\
\text{Technical Sub-system} &\rightarrow \text{Tools, machines, procedures, environment}
\end{aligned}
$$

**STS Principle of Joint Optimization:** An organization is optimized when BOTH sub-systems are designed together — not sequentially.

### 2.2.2 Work Schedules & Circadian Rhythms

The human **circadian rhythm** has a natural period of approximately **24.2 hours**, regulated by the **suprachiasmatic nucleus (SCN)** in the hypothalamus, synchronized by light (via the **retino-hypothalamic tract**) and melatonin secretion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Night-shift workers experience a circadian desynchronization** of 2–4 hours, leading to:
> - Sleep disorders
> - Reduced cognitive performance
> - Increased accident risk (especially between 02:00 – 05:00)
> - Long-term health effects (cardiovascular, metabolic)

### 2.2.3 Shift Rotation Principles (KTU Board Favorite)

| Principle | Recommendation |
|---|---|
| Rotation Direction | **Forward (day → evening → night)**, never backward |
| Rotation Speed | **Fast rotation (every 2–3 days)** for health; **slow rotation (weekly)** for adjustment |
| Shift Length | Maximum **8 hours**; 12-hour shifts allowed only with adequate rest |
| Recovery Time | Minimum **11 hours** between shifts (EU Directive 2003/88/EC) |
| Weekly Limit | **48 hours/week** average (EU Working Time Directive) |

### 2.2.4 Job Design Strategies

1. **Job Simplification** — Repetitive, narrow tasks (Taylorism). *Ergonomic Risk: HIGH.*
2. **Job Rotation** — Workers move between tasks periodically. Reduces monotony and MSD risk.
3. **Job Enlargement** — Add more tasks of similar level horizontally.
4. **Job Enrichment** — Add vertical depth — planning, control, decision-making (Herzberg).
5. **Job Crafting** — Worker redesigns their own job (modern concept).

### 2.2.5 Workload and Stress

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Workload}_{\text{perceived}} &= f(\text{Demands}, \text{Control}, \text{Support}) \\
\text{Stress} &\propto \frac{\text{Demands}}{\text{Control}} \quad \text{(Karasek's Demand-Control Model)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Karasek Model:** Jobs with **HIGH demands + LOW control** = highest strain (e.g., assembly-line workers, air traffic controllers).

### 2.2.6 Participatory Ergonomics

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A process in which workers, supervisors, and management **actively participate** in identifying ergonomic problems and designing solutions. It is the most effective organizational ergonomics intervention (proven by meta-analyses, Cole et al., 2005).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = \dfrac{d}{d'}$ | Visual acuity (Snellen fraction) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{h}{d}\right)$ | Visual angle subtended by object of height $h$ at distance $d$ | radians / arcmin |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | Illuminance (luminous flux per unit area) | lux $\left(\text{lm/m}^2\right)$ |
| 4 | $E = \dfrac{I \cos \alpha}{d^2}$ | Inverse-square law with incidence angle $\alpha$ | lux |
| 5 | $L = \dfrac{I}{A \cos \theta}$ | Luminance (photometric brightness) | $\text{cd/m}^2$ |
| 6 | $C = \dfrac{\vert L_b - L_o \vert}{L_b}$ | Weber contrast (object vs background) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $CR = \dfrac{L_{\max}}{L_{\min}}$ | Contrast ratio (Michelson-style) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $UGR = 8 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{0.25}{L_b} \sum \dfrac{L_s^2 \omega}{p^2}\right)$ | Unified Glare Rating | dimensionless |
| 9 | $f_{\text{critical flicker}} = k \log L + C$ | Ferry-Porter law (flicker fusion) | Hz |
| 10 | $T_{\text{recovery}} \geq 11 \text{ h}$ | Minimum inter-shift rest | hours |
| 11 | $H_{\text{weekly}} \leq 48 \text{ h}$ | Maximum weekly working hours (avg) | hours |
| 12 | $P_{\text{strain}} \propto \dfrac{\text{Demand}}{\text{Control}}$ | Karasek strain index | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Mnemonic for Lighting Units:**
> **"FIA-ELC"** — **F**lux (lm), **I**ntensity (cd), **A**ngle (sr), **E**xits on **A**rea → **E** (lux), **L**uminance (cd/m²), **C**ontrast (–).

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Architectural lighting designers** use the inverse-square law to position luminaires in offices.
- **Automotive dashboard designers** apply luminance contrast to ensure legibility in sunlight.
- **HR & Operations managers** use Karasek's model to redesign call-center schedules and prevent burnout.
- **Shift planners in process industries** apply circadian principles to schedule rotating crews in refineries and power plants.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Snellen Visual Acuity Fraction

### Statement
The Snellen acuity $V$ is defined as the ratio of the **test distance** $d$ to the **distance at which the smallest resolvable letter subtends a standard visual angle of 1 arcminute (′)**.

### Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Define standard letter.} &\rightarrow \text{Letter height } h \text{ such that each stroke = 1 arcmin.} \\
\text{Step 2: Visual angle subtended.} &\rightarrow \theta = \dfrac{h}{d_{\text{standard}}} = 1' \text{ (at standard distance)} \\
\text{Step 3: For a given subject,} &\rightarrow \text{the same letter subtends angle } \theta' = \dfrac{h}{d_{\text{test}}} \\
\text{Step 4: Acuity definition.} &\rightarrow V = \dfrac{\theta'}{1'} = \dfrac{d_{\text{test}}}{d_{\text{standard}}} \\
\text{Result:} \quad V &= \dfrac{d}{d'} \quad \text{(Snellen fraction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example:** A subject reads a line at 6 m that a normal eye reads at 6 m → $V = 6/6 = 1.0$ (normal).
A subject reads at 6 m what a normal eye reads at 12 m → $V = 6/12 = 0.5$ (reduced acuity).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Visual Angle for a Task Object

### Statement
A printed character of height $h$ placed at viewing distance $d$ from the eye subtends a visual angle $\theta$. Derive $\theta$ for small angles.

### Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Exact trigonometric relation.} &\rightarrow \tan(\theta) = \dfrac{h}{d} \\
\text{Step 2: Small-angle approximation.} &\rightarrow \theta \text{ (in rad)} \approx \dfrac{h}{d} \\
\text{Step 3: Convert rad to arcmin.} &\rightarrow \theta_{\text{arcmin}} = \theta_{\text{rad}} \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} \times 60 \\
\text{Step 4: Numerical constant.} &\rightarrow \theta_{\text{arcmin}} = 3438 \times \dfrac{h}{d} \\
\text{Result:} \quad \theta_{\text{arcmin}} &\approx 3438 \cdot \dfrac{h}{d}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A 2.5 mm tall letter is viewed at 500 mm. Find the visual angle in arcmin.
> **Is it legible if 1 arcmin is the threshold?**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{\text{arcmin}} &= 3438 \times \dfrac{2.5}{500} \\
&= 3438 \times 0.005 \\
&= 17.19 \text{ arcmin}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $17.19 \gg 1$, the letter is comfortably legible. **Recommended minimum:** 12–18 arcmin for sustained reading.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inverse-Square Law for Illuminance

### Statement
A point source of luminous intensity $I$ emits light uniformly in all directions. Show that illuminance at distance $d$ falls off as $1/d^2$.

### Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Total flux through sphere.} &\rightarrow \Phi = 4\pi I \\
\text{Step 2: Flux density at distance } d. &\rightarrow E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A_{\text{sphere}}} = \dfrac{4\pi I}{4\pi d^2} \\
\text{Step 3: With oblique incidence } (\alpha). &\rightarrow E = \dfrac{I \cos \alpha}{d^2} \\
\text{Step 4: Practical form.} &\rightarrow E_{d_2} = E_{d_1} \times \left(\dfrac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example

> **Problem:** A lamp produces illuminance of 800 lux at 1 m on a horizontal surface. Find illuminance at 2 m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_2 &= E_1 \times \left(\dfrac{d_1}{d_2}\right)^2 \\
&= 800 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 \\
&= 800 \times \dfrac{1}{4} \\
&= 200 \text{ lux}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Doubling distance reduces illuminance to ONE-FOURTH.** This is why workstation task lights must be kept close to the work.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Weber Contrast Ratio for Display Legibility

### Statement
For a symbol of luminance $L_o$ on a background of luminance $L_b$, derive the Weber contrast $C$ and the recommended minimum for legible displays.

### Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Define Weber contrast.} &\rightarrow C = \dfrac{\vert L_b - L_o \vert}{L_b} \\
\text{Step 2: Legibility threshold.} &\rightarrow C \geq 0.5 \text{ for comfortable reading (ISO 9241-303)} \\
\text{Step 3: For dark text on light bg.} &\rightarrow L_o \ll L_b \Rightarrow C \to 1 \\
\text{Step 4: Contrast Ratio (CR) form.} &\rightarrow CR = \dfrac{L_{\max}}{L_{\min}} \quad (\text{common in VDT specs}) \\
\text{Step 5: Conversion.} &\rightarrow CR = \dfrac{1}{1-C} \quad \text{(when } L_o \text{ is darker)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A VDT screen has maximum luminance $250 \text{ cd/m}^2$ and minimum luminance $2.5 \text{ cd/m}^2$. Find CR and Weber contrast.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= \dfrac{250}{2.5} = 100 \;\!: 1 \\
C &= 1 - \dfrac{1}{CR} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{100} = 0.99
\end{aligned}
$$

For ISO compliance (≥ 1000:1 for medical imaging), this falls short — suitable only for general office use.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Karasek Strain Index

### Statement
Derive the relationship between psychological demand, decision latitude (control), and resulting job strain.

### Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Demand axis (D).} &\rightarrow \text{Quantitative workload + time pressure} \\
\text{Step 2: Control axis (C).} &\rightarrow \text{Decision authority + skill discretion} \\
\text{Step 3: Strain ratio.} &\rightarrow S = \dfrac{D}{C} \quad \text{(qualitative)} \\
\text{Step 4: Four-quadrant map.} &\rightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Low D, High C} \rightarrow \text{Low Strain (Active)} \\ \text{High D, High C} \rightarrow \text{Low Strain (Active)} \\ \text{Low D, Low C} \rightarrow \text{Low Strain (Passive)} \\ \text{High D, Low C} \rightarrow \text{HIGH STRAIN} \end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-strain jobs** (assembly-line workers, call-center agents) are the prime targets for **organizational ergonomics** interventions.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Intervention

| Symptom Identified | Visual Ergonomics Intervention | Organizational Ergonomics Intervention |
|---|---|---|
| Eye-strain reports | Improve lighting, anti-glare screens | Mandatory micro-break policy (20-20-20 rule) |
| High absenteeism | Upgrade monitor refresh rate (≥ 75 Hz) | Flexible scheduling, shift rotation reform |
| Repetitive errors | Increase task contrast & illuminance | Job rotation, training |
| Musculoskeletal complaints | Adjust monitor height | Workstation redesign + team-based work |
| Burnout / turnover | — | Participative ergonomics, job enrichment |
| Night-shift accidents | Brighter task lighting in dim areas | Eliminate backward rotation; limit to 8 h |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Visual Ergonomics. List any FOUR visual functions considered in its design.**

**Model Answer:**

Visual Ergonomics is the branch of ergonomics that studies the interaction between the human visual system and the visual environment at the workplace, optimizing visual performance and comfort.

Four visual functions:
1. **Visual acuity** — ability to discriminate fine detail.
2. **Accommodation** — ability to focus at varying distances.
3. **Adaptation** — adjustment of sensitivity to different light levels.
4. **Color discrimination** — ability to distinguish hues.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks for four functions.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is meant by "Socio-Technical Systems" in organizational ergonomics?**

**Model Answer:**

A Socio-Technical System (STS) views an organization as two interrelated sub-systems:
- **Technical sub-system:** tools, machines, processes, environment.
- **Social sub-system:** people, skills, attitudes, culture, rewards.

The principle of **Joint Optimization** states that an organization performs best when both sub-systems are designed simultaneously to fit each other. *(3 marks)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Visual Ergonomics Focus

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**Explain in detail the lighting quantities: luminous flux, illuminance, luminance, and contrast ratio. State the SI units and derive the inverse-square law of illuminance.**

**Model Solution:**

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Definition |
|---|---|---|---|
| Luminous flux | $\Phi$ | lumen (lm) | Total light energy emitted per unit time, weighted by human eye sensitivity $V(\lambda)$ |
| Luminous intensity | $I$ | candela (cd) | Flux emitted per unit solid angle |
| Illuminance | $E$ | lux ($\text{lm/m}^2$) | Flux incident on a surface per unit area |
| Luminance | $L$ | $\text{cd/m}^2$ | Flux emitted/reflected per unit projected area per unit solid angle |
| Contrast ratio | $CR$ | dimensionless | $\dfrac{L_{\max}}{L_{\min}}$ |

**Derivation of Inverse-Square Law:**

A point source emitting total flux $\Phi = 4\pi I$ illuminates a sphere of radius $d$ whose surface area is $4\pi d^2$. Hence:

$$
E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A} = \dfrac{4\pi I}{4\pi d^2} = \dfrac{I}{d^2}
$$

With oblique incidence at angle $\alpha$ to the normal:

$$
E = \dfrac{I \cos \alpha}{d^2}
$$

For example, an 800-cd lamp at 2 m produces $E = \dfrac{800}{4} = 200 \text{ lux}$ on a horizontal surface. *(7 marks: 3 for definitions + 4 for derivation & example.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Discuss the causes, symptoms, and preventive measures of Computer Vision Syndrome (CVS) in a VDT workstation.**

**Model Solution:**

**Causes:**
- Inadequate illuminance / glare on screen.
- Improper viewing distance (< 40 cm or > 70 cm).
- Low contrast / small font on screen.
- Continuous focus without blinking.
- Screen refresh rate ≤ 60 Hz.

**Symptoms:**
- Eyestrain, burning or itching eyes.
- Blurred or double vision.
- Headaches, neck/shoulder pain.
- Dry eyes (reduced blink rate from 18 → 4 per minute on screens).

**Preventive Measures (Visual Ergonomics):**
1. **20-20-20 Rule:** Every 20 minutes, look at an object 20 feet away for 20 seconds.
2. **Lighting:** 300–500 lux on screen; matte anti-glare filters.
3. **Viewing Distance:** 50–70 cm; top of screen at or just below eye level.
4. **Refresh Rate:** ≥ 75 Hz to avoid flicker.
5. **Contrast Ratio:** ≥ 1000:1 for sustained work.
6. **Annual eye examination** for all VDT operators.

*(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3 distribution.)*

---

### Question B — Organizational Ergonomics Focus

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**Explain the Karasek Demand-Control model of job strain. How does it guide organizational design?**

**Model Solution:**

Karasek's (1979) Demand-Control model maps jobs on two axes:

- **Psychological Demands** (workload, time pressure) on the vertical axis.
- **Decision Latitude / Control** (authority + skill discretion) on the horizontal axis.

This creates four quadrants:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Low D, Low C} &\rightarrow \text{Passive Jobs (low strain, but deskilled)} \\
\text{High D, High C} &\rightarrow \text{Active Jobs (motivating, low strain)} \\
\text{Low D, High C} &\rightarrow \text{Low-strain Jobs (relaxed)} \\
\text{High D, Low C} &\rightarrow \text{HIGH-STRAIN Jobs (worst for health)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Implications for Organizational Design:**
1. **Increase control** through participative decision-making and empowerment.
2. **Reduce demands** through workload balancing and adequate staffing.
3. **Job enrichment** to give workers vertical depth and decision authority.
4. **Social support** acts as a buffer (Johnson's extension to the model).

**Application:** Used widely in redesigning call-center work, nursing schedules, and assembly lines.

*(7 marks: 3 for theory + 2 for diagram + 2 for application.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Discuss shift work and its effects on the human circadian rhythm. Suggest principles of an ergonomic shift schedule.**

**Model Solution:**

**Circadian Basis:**
The human circadian pacemaker in the **suprachiasmatic nucleus** has a free-running period of ~24.2 hours. Light is the dominant *zeitgeber* (time-giver), acting via the retino-hypothalamic tract to suppress melatonin.

**Effects of Shift Work (especially night shifts):**
- **Sleep:** reduced total sleep, fragmented sleep, accumulated sleep debt.
- **Performance:** peak accident risk between 02:00–05:00; reaction time impaired.
- **Health:** increased risk of cardiovascular disease, GI disorders, metabolic syndrome, breast cancer (IARC Group 2A carcinogen).
- **Social:** disruption of family life and leisure.

**Principles of an Ergonomic Shift Schedule (per Knauth, 1993 & ILO):**

| # | Principle | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Forward rotation** (Day → Evening → Night) | Body adapts ~1 h/day in forward direction |
| 2 | **Fast rotation** (2–3 day cycles) | Avoids chronic circadian disruption |
| 3 | **Maximum 8-hour shifts** | Reduces fatigue accumulation |
| 4 | **Minimum 11 hours between shifts** | Adequate rest recovery |
| 5 | **Maximum 48 hours/week average** | EU Working Time Directive compliance |
| 6 | **Avoid permanent night shifts** | Permanent nights increase long-term health risk |
| 7 | **Free weekends every 3 weeks** | Social & family recovery |
| 8 | **Predictable schedule** | Allows worker planning & sleep regulation |

*(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3 distribution.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing illuminance with luminance** — *illuminance* is incident light (lux), *luminance* is emitted/reflected brightness (cd/m²). Mixing them = **–2 marks**.
> 2. **Writing "20/20 vision = perfect"** — it is **normal**, not maximum. Many young adults achieve 20/15 or better.
> 3. **Forgetting the SI units** in the formula sheet → **–1 mark per missing unit**.
> 4. **Treating Karasek's model as a numerical formula** — it is qualitative; do not write $S = D/C$ as a final equation on the answer sheet without stating "qualitative index."
> 5. **Recommending "backward rotation"** in shift work → this is **ergonomically wrong**; forward rotation only.
> 6. **Omitting examples** in 7-mark questions — KTU valuators reserve **2 marks** for an illustrative example.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### Visual Ergonomics
- ☐ Visual acuity is measured by **Snellen fraction** $V = d/d'$.
- ☐ Threshold resolution = **1 arcminute** (≈ 0.00029 rad).
- ☐ **Illuminance** $E$ in **lux**, **Luminance** $L$ in **cd/m²**.
- ☐ **Inverse-square law** $E = I/d^2$ — doubling distance → quarter the illuminance.
- ☐ **Weber contrast** $C = \vert L_b - L_o \vert / L_b$; minimum **0.5** for legibility.
- ☐ **Glare** = brightness > adapted level; types: disability, discomfort, direct, reflected.
- ☐ **Computer Vision Syndrome** is the modern epidemic of visual fatigue.
- ☐ **Recommended illuminance** for offices: **300–500 lux**; for fine work: **≥ 1000 lux**.
- ☐ **20-20-20 rule** to prevent digital eye strain.

### Organizational Ergonomics
- ☐ **Macroergonomics** = organizational ergonomics.
- ☐ **Socio-Technical Systems (STS)** by Trist & Bamforth (1951) — *Joint Optimization*.
- ☐ **Karasek's Demand-Control Model** — high demand + low control = high strain.
- ☐ **Circadian rhythm** ≈ 24.2 h; synchronized by light via the SCN.
- ☐ **Forward rotation** of shifts is the only ergonomic option.
- ☐ **Max 8 h/shift, 11 h rest between shifts, 48 h/week average.**
- ☐ **Participative ergonomics** is the most effective macro-intervention.
- ☐ **Job rotation, enlargement, enrichment, crafting** — four job design strategies.
- ☐ **Night-shift work is a probable carcinogen** (IARC 2A).
- ☐ **Knauth's 8 principles** are the gold standard for shift design.

### Cross-Cutting (Integration)
- ☐ Visual fatigue ↑ in poor organizational design (long hours, no breaks).
- ☐ High strain jobs need BOTH visual AND organizational fixes.
- ☐ ISO 9241 series governs VDT ergonomics; ISO 6385 governs work design.
- ☐ **IEA Core Competencies** require knowledge of BOTH topics for certification.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** Always close a 14-mark answer with a **"Conclusion / Real-World Relevance"** line of 2–3 sentences. KTU examiners award up to **+1 bonus mark** for demonstrating application awareness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Technological System (HTS): concept and classifications
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET604 - APPLIED ERGONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Human Technological System (HTS): concept and classifications

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Human Technological System (HTS)** is an integrated, goal-directed socio-technical configuration in which one or more human operators interact synergistically with machines, tools, software, environmental artefacts, and procedural protocols to accomplish a defined task or mission. The human is treated as an *active, information-processing component* — not a passive element — whose perceptual, cognitive, and motor capabilities are coupled with the deterministic logic of the technological subsystem.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (aligned with the International Ergonomics Association - IEA), HTS is the foundational unit of analysis for ergonomic intervention. Every workplace, control room, automobile cockpit, hospital theatre, or assembly cell is modelled as an HTS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly defines the HTS as comprising **{Human, Machine, Environment, Task, Interface}** — often remembered by the mnemonic **H-M-E-T-I**. Ergonomic design optimises the *fit* between these five sub-systems.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are **driving a car on a winding hill road at night**.

- **Human** = You (the driver). Your eyes detect the curve, your brain decides to turn, your hands rotate the steering wheel, your foot presses the brake.
- **Machine / Technology** = The car itself — engine, brakes, headlights, steering, ABS sensors, GPS.
- **Environment** = The road, weather, traffic, lighting, ambient noise.
- **Task** = "Reach the destination safely within 30 minutes."
- **Interface** = Steering wheel, pedals, dashboard, mirrors, gear lever — the *touch-points* where you and the car exchange information and energy.

If any one of these five is poorly designed (e.g., dim dashboard, slippery pedal, foggy road), the *entire system fails* — even though the car engine and your brain are both individually excellent. **This is the central thesis of HTS ergonomics: the system is only as strong as its weakest coupled link.**

## 1.3 Why HTS Concept Matters in Ergonomics

| Perspective | Without HTS View | With HTS View |
|---|---|---|
| Accident analysis | Blames "human error" | Identifies interface/environmental mismatch |
| Design | Optimises machine alone | Optimises the *joint* human-machine performance |
| Productivity | Measures output only | Measures system effectiveness & human well-being |
| Safety | Reactive (post-incident) | Proactive (latent failure identification) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** An HTS is *not* a human *plus* a machine. It is a *new, emergent entity* whose properties cannot be predicted by studying the human or the machine in isolation. This is the **systems-theoretic axiom of emergence** in ergonomics.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Sub-Systems of HTS (H-M-E-T-I Model)

### 2.1.1 Human Sub-System (H)
The human operator is itself a complex sub-system with three cascaded stages:

1. **Sensory / Perceptual Stage** — Receptors (visual, auditory, tactile, vestibular, proprioceptive) transduce physical stimuli into neural signals.
2. **Cognitive / Central Processing Stage** — Brain integrates perception with memory, training, and decision rules to produce an *intention*.
3. **Motor / Effector Stage** — Musculoskeletal apparatus executes the intention as physical motion or speech.

The human sub-system is characterised by **bounded rationality**, **stochastic response time**, **fatigue**, and **learning curve effects**.

### 2.1.2 Machine / Technology Sub-System (M)
Any artefact that stores, transforms, transports, or displays information/energy. Classification by function:
- **Display instruments** (provide information to H) → *visual, auditory, tactile displays*
- **Control instruments** (receive commands from H) → *steering wheels, keyboards, pedals*
- **Tools & machinery** → *power-assisted devices that amplify H's physical output*

### 2.1.3 Environment Sub-System (E)
The ambient physical, organisational, and psychosocial context: lighting, temperature, humidity, noise, vibration, social climate, shift schedule, corporate culture. KTU 2024 emphasises **ISO 6385** and **ISO 9241** standards for environmental ergonomics.

### 2.1.4 Task Sub-System (T)
The *goal* and *procedure* assigned to the HTS. Tasks are decomposed into:
- **Elemental tasks** (sensory, cognitive, motor)
- **Composite tasks** (combined elemental tasks)
- **Meta-tasks** (task-management itself)

### 2.1.5 Interface Sub-System (I)
The **physical, cognitive, and organisational boundary** where H and M exchange signals. Includes hardware interfaces (HMI panels) and software interfaces (UI/UX). This is the *primary ergonomic design target* in the 2024 syllabus.

## 2.2 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop HTS

| Feature | Open-Loop HTS | Closed-Loop HTS |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback path | **Absent** | **Present** |
| Error correction | None (feed-forward only) | Continuous (servo-corrected) |
| Human role | Pre-programmed executor | Monitor + controller |
| Example | Toaster, automatic washing machine (older) | Car driving, manual aircraft piloting, CNC with operator |
| Failure mode | Undetected drift | Detected and corrected |

Mathematically, a closed-loop HTS follows the control-theory paradigm where the **error signal** $e(t)$ is the difference between desired output $r(t)$ and actual output $y(t)$:

$$e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$$

## 2.3 Classification of HTS — KTU Board Pattern

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates coverage of **four primary classes** based on the *degree of mechanisation* (a metric of the ratio of machine work to human work per unit task).

### Classification A — By Degree of Mechanisation (Meister, 1987)

1. **Class I — Manual System**
   - Human provides ALL power, control, and information processing.
   - Example: hand-hoe farming, manual calculation.
2. **Class II — Mechanised (Man-Machine) System**
   - Human provides control & information processing; machine provides power amplification.
   - Example: bicycle, hand-cranked sewing machine.
3. **Class III — Automated System**
   - Machine handles power + control; human supervises.
   - Example: automated bottling plant, autopilot cruise control.
4. **Class IV — Computer-Integrated (Autonomous) System**
   - Adaptive, learning, networked. Human as *strategic overseer*.
   - Example: Industry 4.0 cyber-physical systems, AI-driven CNC cells.

### Classification B — By Type of Control (Sheridan, 1987 — widely tested)

| Level | Descriptor | Human's Role |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Manual | Does everything by hand |
| 2 | Action support | Machine assists physical action |
| 3 | Batch / pre-programmed | Human plans, machine executes |
| 4 | Shared control | Human + machine share control dynamically |
| 5 | Decision support | Machine recommends, human decides |
| 6 | Supervisory control | Human manages multiple machines |
| 7 | Fully autonomous | Human out of loop |
| 8 | Mixed-initiative | Initiative switches H ↔ M |
| 9 | Traded control | Control authority is traded on task demands |
| 10 | Pairwise adaptive | H & M adapt to each other |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Favourite:** Sheridan Levels **1, 5, 6, 8** are repeatedly asked. Memorise the column "Human's Role" verbatim.

### Classification C — By Information Flow Topology
- **Closed-loop with compensatory display** — only error is shown.
- **Closed-loop with pursuit display** — both target and current value are shown (preferred for novices).
- **Open-loop / feedforward** — no feedback.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Law | Meaning | Units | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{sys} = H \cdot M \cdot I \cdot T \cdot E$ | System effectiveness = product of reliabilities | dimensionless | Reliability engineering |
| 2 | $H_{rel} = 1 - \lambda_{H}$ | Human reliability (1 − error probability) | dimensionless | Human-error modelling |
| 3 | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2 \!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right)$ | **Fitts' Law** — Movement Time vs target | ms | Pointing devices, HCI |
| 4 | $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | **Hick's Law** — choice Reaction Time | ms | Display design |
| 5 | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Closed-loop error signal | task unit | Control theory |
| 6 | $G_{OL}(s) = G_{H}(s) \cdot G_{M}(s)$ | Open-loop transfer function of HTS | dimensionless | Stability analysis |
| 7 | $W = f(P, C)$ | workload = f(demand, capacity) | varies | Workload design |
| 8 | $S = \log_2(I) + C$ | **Stevens' Power Law** (sensation magnitude) | subjective units | Display sizing |
| 9 | $\eta_{HTS} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | HTS efficiency | dimensionless | System optimisation |
| 10 | $V = k \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{R}$ | Visual acuity limit | arc-minutes | Display design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pipeline Check:** When typing the Fitts' Law formula inside any markdown table, the absolute-value / division must be rendered with `\frac{}{}` or `\left( \right)` and the inside **must NOT** contain the pipe character `|`. Use `\dfrac` or `\left( 2D / W \right)` for safety.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Avionics** — Pilot + autopilot = closed-loop HTS; Sherry Level 6.
- **Power-plant control rooms** — Sheridan Level 5 / 6 (decision support + supervisory).
- **Robotic surgery (Da Vinci)** — Man-machine with shared control (Level 4).
- **Autonomous vehicles (SAE L3-L5)** — Traded control (Level 9).
- **Manufacturing 4.0 cells** — Class IV, pairwise-adaptive (Level 10).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section builds the **mathematical model of a Human Technological System** from first principles, suitable for KTU 14-mark analytical questions.

## 3.1 Modelling the Human as a Transfer Function

A human operator performing a continuous manual-control task (e.g., steering a car) can be represented by a quasi-linear transfer function in the Laplace domain. The classical McRuer model assumes the human behaves as a **gain + integrator + time-delay** element.

$$
G_{H}(s) \;=\; K_{H} \cdot \frac{(1 + T_{L}\, s)\, e^{-\tau\, s}}{s}
$$

Where:
- $K_{H}$ = human gain (voluntary effort scaling, units depend on task)
- $T_{L}$ = human lead time constant (anticipatory response, seconds)
- $\tau$ = human reaction time delay (≈ **0.15 s** for visual, **0.20 s** for vestibular)
- $e^{-\tau s}$ = Padé approximation of pure time delay

### Step-by-Step Derivation of System Effectiveness $E_{sys}$

System effectiveness of an HTS is the probability that the system accomplishes its mission successfully when called upon under stated conditions. It is decomposed into three multiplicative components:

$$
E_{sys} \;=\; A \cdot D \cdot C
$$

**Step 1 — Availability $A$:** Probability the system is operational at $t=0$.

$$
A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}
$$

where $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures (human + machine combined), $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair.

**Step 2 — Dependability $D$:** Conditional probability of completing mission given availability. Depends on **reliability** over mission duration $T$:

$$
D(t) = e^{-\lambda t}, \quad \lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF}
$$

So at the end of mission time $T$:

$$
D(T) = e^{-T / MTBF}
$$

**Step 3 — Capability $C$:** Probability that, given mission completion, the system performed within acceptable performance tolerances. For HTS:

$$
C = 1 - P(\text{human error within time window})
$$

If human-error probability per task is $p_e$ and the task has $n$ independent sub-tasks:

$$
C = (1 - p_e)^{n}
$$

**Combining Steps 1–3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{sys} &= A \cdot D(T) \cdot C \\[4pt]
&= \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \cdot e^{-T / MTBF} \cdot (1 - p_e)^{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** Adding more human sub-tasks ($n \uparrow$) **multiplicatively degrades** $E_{sys}$. This is why automation of even *one* of several manual sub-tasks can dramatically improve HTS effectiveness — a directly examinable conclusion in KTU Part B.

## 3.2 Fitts' Law — Step-by-Step Derivation & Application

Fitts' Law relates **Movement Time (MT)** to **task difficulty (ID)**:

$$
MT \;=\; a \;+\; b \cdot ID
$$

Where the **Index of Difficulty** $ID$ (in bits) is:

$$
ID \;=\; \log_2\!\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right)
$$

$D$ = distance to target centre (metres), $W$ = width of target (metres).

### Worked Numerical Derivation (typical KTU 14-mark part)

**Problem:** A worker must move a cursor to a target 20 cm away, with target width 4 cm. Given $a = 50$ ms and $b = 100$ ms/bit, find MT.

**Step 1 — Compute $D/W$:**

$$
\frac{D}{W} = \frac{0.20}{0.04} = 5.0
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $2D/W$:**

$$
\frac{2D}{W} = 2 \times 5.0 = 10.0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Index of Difficulty:**

$$
ID = \log_2(10.0) \approx 3.3219 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Movement Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT &= a + b \cdot ID \\
&= 50 + 100 \times 3.3219 \\
&= 50 + 332.19 \\
&= 382.19 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** A worker will take approximately **382 ms** to acquire this target. If the target width is halved to 2 cm (more precise aim required), recompute to show non-linear workload increase:

$$
\frac{2D}{W} = \frac{2 \times 0.20}{0.02} = 20 \;\Rightarrow\; ID = \log_2(20) \approx 4.32 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
MT = 50 + 100 \times 4.32 = 482 \text{ ms}
$$

Thus, halving target precision increases MT by **≈ 26 %** — demonstrating the *non-linear* (logarithmic) penalty of precision demands on the human sub-system.

## 3.3 Hick-Hyman Law — Choice Reaction Time

$$
RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n + 1)
$$

Where $n$ = number of equally probable stimuli. Adding stimulus choices increases RT logarithmically.

**Sanity check:** For $n=1$ (binary), $RT = a + b \cdot 1 = a + b$. For $n=7$ (octal keypad), $RT = a + b \cdot 3$. Each doubling of choices adds only $b$ ms — proving the human's robust information-handling capacity.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Closed-Loop HTS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    taskGOAL["Task Goal Setpoint r of t"] --> compareNode["Comparator"]
    feedbackOut["Feedback y of t"] --> compareNode
    compareNode --> errorSig["Error Signal e of t"]
    errorSig --> displayA["Display Interface I"]
    displayA --> sensorH["Human Sensors Vision Audition"]
    sensorH --> brainH["Human Central Processor Cognition Memory"]
    brainH --> motorH["Human Motor Effector Hands Voice"]
    motorH --> controlM["Machine Control Knob Lever Pedal"]
    controlM --> machineActuator["Machine Actuator Engine Robot"]
    machineActuator --> outputHTS["HTS Output y of t"]
    outputHTS --> environmentEff["Environment Effects"]
    environmentEff --> feedbackOut
    machineActuator -.perturbs.-> humanEnv["Human Environment Noise Vibration"]
    humanEnv --> sensorH
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- The **outer loop** is the technological sub-system (machine + interface).
- The **inner loop** is the human sub-system (sensor → processor → motor).
- **Interface** is the bridge block (`displayA` for input, `controlM` for output).
- **Environment** perturbs both loops as an exogenous noise channel.

## 4.2 Classification Tree of HTS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    htsRoot["HTS Classification"]
    htsRoot --> byMech["By Degree of Mechanisation"]
    htsRoot --> byControl["By Type of Control Sheridan"]
    htsRoot --> byLoop["By Feedback Topology"]
    htsRoot --> byDomain["By Application Domain"]

    byMech --> cls1["Class I Manual"]
    byMech --> cls2["Class II Mechanised"]
    byMech --> cls3["Class III Automated"]
    byMech --> cls4["Class IV Computer Integrated"]

    byControl --> lv1["L1 Manual"]
    byControl --> lv2["L2 Action Support"]
    byControl --> lv3["L3 Preprogrammed Batch"]
    byControl --> lv4["L4 Shared Control"]
    byControl --> lv5["L5 Decision Support"]
    byControl --> lv6["L6 Supervisory Control"]
    byControl --> lv7["L7 Fully Autonomous"]
    byControl --> lv8["L8 Mixed Initiative"]
    byControl --> lv9["L9 Traded Control"]
    byControl --> lv10["L10 Pairwise Adaptive"]

    byLoop --> openLoopA["Open Loop No Feedback"]
    byLoop --> closedLoopA["Closed Loop With Feedback"]

    byDomain --> aviationA["Aviation"]
    byDomain --> medicalA["Medical Surgery"]
    byDomain --> industryA["Industry 4 0"]
    byDomain --> transportA["Transport ADAS"]
```

## 4.3 Human Sub-System as Information Processor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stimIn["Physical Stimulus"]
    stimIn --> receptorA["Receptor Stage Transduction"]
    receptorA --> neuralA["Neural Encoding"]
    neuralA --> perceptualA["Perception Pattern Recognition"]
    perceptualA --> cognitiveA["Cognition Decision Memory"]
    cognitiveA --> motorPlanA["Motor Planning"]
    motorPlanA --> effectorA["Effector Muscles"]
    effectorA --> responseOut["Behavioural Response"]

    memoryStore["Long Term Memory Rules Schemata"] -.retrieves.-> cognitiveA
    feedbackLoopA["Internal Proprioceptive Feedback"] -.modulates.-> motorPlanA
    fatigueMod["Fatigue Vigilance Drift"] -.attenuates.-> cognitiveA
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **fatigue/vigilance** block in the diagram is the *modelling hook* for *human-reliability degradation* over an 8-hour shift — a frequent KTU 14-mark analytical angle.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**Define the term "Human Technological System" (HTS). List its five sub-systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Human Technological System (HTS) is an integrated, goal-directed configuration in which one or more human operators interact with technological artefacts to achieve a defined objective, treating the human as an active information-processing component.
The five sub-systems are:
1. **H** – Human (sensory, cognitive, motor)
2. **M** – Machine / Technology
3. **E** – Environment (physical, psychosocial)
4. **T** – Task
5. **I** – Interface

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing + mnemonic explanation)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Differentiate between an open-loop and a closed-loop Human Technological System with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Open-Loop HTS | Closed-Loop HTS |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Absent | Present |
| Error correction | Not possible | Continuous |
| Human role | Pre-set operator | Active controller |
| Example | Automatic bread toaster, washing machine | Car driving, manual piloting |

*(1.5 marks table + 1.5 marks example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students **lose 2–3 marks** by writing only Sheridan's levels *without* contextualising them with **Indian industry examples** (e.g., Kudankulam control room, UPI interface). Always **anchor theory to at least one Indian scenario**.
> 2. For 14-mark "Classify and explain" questions, the examiner expects a **markdown table** *plus* a **diagram** *plus* a **real-world example**. Missing any of these three → you lose 4 marks.
> 3. **Do not** confuse "Mechanised System" with "Automated System" — many students lose marks here. Mechanised = human still in control loop; Automated = control loop closed *without* human.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Classify Human Technological Systems based on the degree of mechanisation. Explain each class with a suitable example and a block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

According to Meister's classification, HTS is classified into four classes based on the degree to which the machine sub-system takes over the human's physical, control, and information-processing functions.

**Class I — Manual System:**
- Human provides ALL power, control, and information processing.
- No machine aid (or only passive tools).
- Example: hand-shovel digging, manual arithmetic calculation, traditional pottery wheel.
- Block flow: `Human → Direct Task Output` (no machine block).

**Class II — Mechanised (Man-Machine) System:**
- Human retains **control and information-processing**; machine provides **power amplification**.
- Example: bicycle, hand-cranked sewing machine, manually driven lathe.
- Block flow: `Human (control) → Interface (lever) → Machine (amplified power) → Task`.

**Class III — Automated System:**
- Machine handles **power AND control**; human becomes a **supervisor**.
- Example: Automatic bottling plant, domestic washing machine (modern), cruise control in cars.
- Block flow: `Human (monitor) ← Feedback display ← Machine (control loop closed internally)`.

**Class IV — Computer-Integrated / Autonomous System:**
- Adaptive, learning, networked. Human is a *strategic overseer*.
- Example: Industry 4.0 cyber-physical cell, autonomous vehicle fleet management.
- Block flow: `Human (strategic) ↔ AI Controller ↔ Networked Machines`.

**Block Diagram (draw as Mermaid in exam):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classI["Class I Manual Human Only"]
    classII["Class II Mechanised H controls M amplifies"]
    classIII["Class III Automated M controls M supervises"]
    classIV["Class IV Computer Integrated Adaptive Networked"]

    classI --> classII --> classIII --> classIV
```

*(1 mark per class + 1 mark for the diagram + 1 mark for examples + 1 mark for continuity narrative = 7 marks)*

#### (b) Discuss the ten levels of automation in Sheridan's classification with a focus on supervisory and traded-control levels. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Sheridan (1987, 1992) proposed a 10-level taxonomy describing the progressive transfer of control authority from the human to the machine.

| Level | Name | Human's Authority | Indian Industry Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Manual | 100 % | Bullock-cart driving, manual lathe |
| 2 | Action support | 90 % | Power-steering in cars |
| 3 | Batch pre-programmed | 70 % | CNC punch-press with operator |
| 4 | Shared control | 50 % | Robotic surgery (Da Vinci) |
| 5 | Decision support | 60 % | Clinical decision-support in ICU |
| 6 | Supervisory control | 30 % | Nuclear-plant operator (Kudankulam) |
| 7 | Fully autonomous | 0 % | ATM cash dispenser |
| 8 | Mixed-initiative | Variable | UPI fraud-detection AI |
| 9 | Traded control | Dynamically swapped | SAE Level-3 autonomous car |
| 10 | Pairwise adaptive | Mutual adaptation | Cobot in automotive assembly (Tata Motors) |

**Focus on Level 6 – Supervisory Control:**
The human manages *multiple* automated sub-systems, intervening only when an alarm triggers. Information flow is *asymmetric*: the machine floods the human with telemetry, while the human's commands are sparse and high-level. Risk: **vigilance decrement** and **out-of-the-loop unfamiliarity**.

**Focus on Level 9 – Traded Control:**
Control authority is *traded* between human and machine based on context. Example: in autonomous vehicles, the machine drives on highways (Level 7) but *trades* control to the driver during dense urban manoeuvres. Risk: **mode-confusion** and **handover latency**.

**Focus on Level 10 – Pairwise Adaptive:**
The human and machine *learn from each other*. Modern collaborative robots (cobots) sense the worker's fatigue and slow their cycle-time accordingly. This is the frontier of **Industry 5.0** ergonomic design.

*(1 mark for table + 2 marks supervisory + 2 marks traded + 2 marks pairwise adaptive = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the components of a closed-loop Human Technological System. Derive an expression for the open-loop transfer function of the system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A closed-loop HTS consists of: **Comparator → Human Sub-system → Interface → Machine Sub-system → Plant → Output**, with feedback tapped from the output back to the comparator.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refSig["Reference Input R of s"]
    refSig --> compNode["Comparator Subtractor"]
    fbSig["Feedback H of s times Y of s"] --> compNode
    compNode --> errOut["Error E of s"]
    errOut --> humBlock["Human G sub H of s"]
    humBlock --> ifaceBlock["Interface G sub I of s"]
    ifaceBlock --> machBlock["Machine G sub M of s"]
    machBlock --> plantBlock["Plant G sub P of s"]
    plantBlock --> outSig["Output Y of s"]
    outSig --> fbTap["Feedback Tap"]
    fbTap --> fbBlock["Feedback H of s"]
    fbBlock --> fbSig
```

**Derivation of Open-Loop Transfer Function:**

In the forward path, the four blocks multiply (cascade rule of block diagrams):

$$
G_{OL}(s) = G_{H}(s) \cdot G_{I}(s) \cdot G_{M}(s) \cdot G_{P}(s)
$$

**Derivation of Closed-Loop Transfer Function:**

From the comparator:

$$
E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot Y(s)
$$

From the forward path:

$$
Y(s) = G_{OL}(s) \cdot E(s)
$$

Substituting $E(s)$:

$$
Y(s) = G_{OL}(s) \cdot \bigl[ R(s) - H(s) \cdot Y(s) \bigr]
$$

$$
Y(s) + G_{OL}(s) \cdot H(s) \cdot Y(s) = G_{OL}(s) \cdot R(s)
$$

$$
Y(s) \cdot \bigl[ 1 + G_{OL}(s) \cdot H(s) \bigr] = G_{OL}(s) \cdot R(s)
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{Y(s)}{R(s)} &= \frac{G_{OL}(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s) \cdot H(s)} \\[4pt]
G_{CL}(s) &= \frac{G_{H}(s)\, G_{I}(s)\, G_{M}(s)\, G_{P}(s)}{1 + G_{H}(s)\, G_{I}(s)\, G_{M}(s)\, G_{P}(s) \cdot H(s)}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks diagram + 2 marks forward-path derivation + 3 marks closed-loop derivation = 7 marks)*

#### (b) A worker is performing a repetitive pick-and-place task. The target distance is 30 cm and target width is 5 cm. Using Fitts' Law with $a = 60$ ms and $b = 110$ ms/bit, calculate the movement time. If a redesign halves the target width to improve precision, what is the percentage increase in MT? Comment on the ergonomic implication. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Original Configuration**
$D = 0.30$ m, $W = 0.05$ m, $a = 60$ ms, $b = 110$ ms/bit.

$$
\frac{2D}{W} = \frac{2 \times 0.30}{0.05} = 12
$$

$$
ID_{1} = \log_2(12) \approx 3.585 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
MT_{1} = 60 + 110 \times 3.585 = 60 + 394.34 = 454.34 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Redesigned Configuration** (W halved to 0.025 m)

$$
\frac{2D}{W} = \frac{2 \times 0.30}{0.025} = 24
$$

$$
ID_{2} = \log_2(24) \approx 4.585 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
MT_{2} = 60 + 110 \times 4.585 = 60 + 504.34 = 564.34 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Increase**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\Delta MT &= \frac{MT_2 - MT_1}{MT_1} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{564.34 - 454.34}{454.34} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{110.00}{454.34} \times 100 \\
&\approx 24.21 \,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Ergonomic Implication**
- Halving the target precision target increases movement time by ~24 % — a **non-linear (logarithmic)** penalty.
- Over a shift of 8 hours with, say, 10 such tasks per minute, this adds ≈ 30 minutes of accumulated muscular effort → **increased risk of Repetitive Strain Injury (RSI)** and **reduced throughput**.
- **Design recommendation:** Provide **target highlighting** (colour, light), **funnel guides**, or **tool jig** to enlarge the *effective* target width without changing physical dimensions.
- This illustrates the **Fitts' Law paradox**: tighter tolerances improve product quality but disproportionately tax the human sub-system.

*(1 mark Step 1 + 1 mark Step 2 + 1.5 marks Step 3 + 2 marks Step 4 + 1.5 marks calculation accuracy = 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS — MEMORISE BEFORE EXAM**

- **HTS = {H, M, E, T, I}** — H-M-E-T-I mnemonic is mandatory.
- **Three human stages:** Sensory → Cognitive → Motor (always in that order).
- **Meister's 4 Classes:** Manual / Mechanised / Automated / Computer-Integrated.
- **Sheridan's 10 Levels** — memorise levels **1, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10** especially.
- **Open-loop** = no feedback (toaster); **Closed-loop** = feedback present (car driving).
- **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \log_2(2D/W)$ — tighter W → larger ID → longer MT (non-linear).
- **Hick's Law:** $RT = a + b \log_2(n+1)$ — more choices → longer RT (logarithmic).
- **Closed-loop transfer function:** $G_{CL} = \dfrac{G_{OL}}{1 + G_{OL} H(s)}$ — always begin the denominator with **1**.
- **System effectiveness:** $E_{sys} = A \cdot D \cdot C$ — product (not sum) → *weakest link governs*.
- **Human is a stochastic, learning, fatiguing component** — never treat as a deterministic linear element.
- **Interface is the ergonomic design target** — poor interface design is the #1 cause of human-error incidents.
- **Always quote one Indian industry example** — Kudankulam (nuclear supervisory), UPI (mixed-initiative), Tata Motors cobots (pairwise adaptive).
- **Draw at least one Mermaid-style block diagram** in any 14-mark HTS answer.
- **Avoid the pipe character `|`** in formula tables — use `\left( \right)` or `\dfrac`.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of HTS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET604 - APPLIED ERGONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design of HTS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## What is HTS in Ergonomics?

**HTS stands for Human Thermal System** — a core ergonomic framework that studies how the human body regulates its internal temperature and exchanges heat with the surrounding environment. The "Design of HTS" refers to engineering and designing work environments, clothing systems, and equipment that maintain the human body in a state of **thermal equilibrium** (heat production = heat loss).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** The Human Thermal System (HTS) is a biophysical model of the human body treated as a thermal engine, where metabolic energy is converted into mechanical work and heat. The design objective is to ensure the rate of heat storage in the body core remains within safe physiological limits (typically ±1°C from the 37°C set-point) by balancing metabolic heat gain against environmental heat loss.

## Conceptual Analogy — The "Body as a Power Plant"

Imagine the human body as a **100-watt incandescent light bulb** running 24/7. Just like a bulb generates heat that must dissipate through its glass surface, the human body continuously produces metabolic heat (from digesting food, muscle activity, even thinking) and must dump this heat into the surrounding air, or risk overheating.

- If the room is **cold** → bulb cools down easily (body loses heat quickly → shivering, discomfort).
- If the room is **hot** → bulb cannot dissipate heat (body core temperature rises → heat stroke risk).
- A **fan blowing air** = forced convection = faster heat removal.
- A **sweating person** = evaporative cooling = the body's emergency radiator.

The "Design of HTS" is essentially designing the **room (workplace climate), the bulb's outer casing (clothing insulation), and the fan/humidifier (environmental controls)** so the bulb stays at a steady, safe operating temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric:** Thermal comfort is universally benchmarked against the **PMV (Predicted Mean Vote)** scale defined by **ISO 7730**, where a PMV of **0** represents optimal thermal neutrality for a population.

## The Four Pillars of HTS Design

Every HTS design decision revolves around **four interdependent variables**:

1. **Air Temperature (Tₐ)** — measured in °C, the dry-bulb temperature of ambient air.
2. **Mean Radiant Temperature (Tᵣ)** — the uniform temperature of an imaginary enclosure where radiant heat transfer equals that of the actual environment.
3. **Air Velocity (Vₐ)** — measured in m/s, affects convective heat loss.
4. **Humidity (RH)** — Relative humidity, affects evaporative cooling efficiency.

Plus two human factors:
5. **Metabolic Rate (M)** — W/m² of body surface area.
6. **Clothing Insulation (Icl)** — measured in **clo units** (1 clo = 0.155 m²·K/W).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Human Heat Balance Equation

The foundational principle governing HTS design is the **steady-state thermal balance equation** developed by **Fanger (1970)**, which is the cornerstone of modern thermal comfort science.

The body achieves thermal equilibrium when:

$$S = 0$$

where **S** is the rate of heat storage in the body (W/m²). If S ≠ 0, the body either heats up (S > 0) or cools down (S < 0).

## 2.2 Heat Production — The Internal Engine

The human body produces heat internally through **metabolic processes**. The metabolic rate is the sum of:
- **Basal metabolic rate** (organ function, cell maintenance)
- **Activity-induced metabolism** (muscle work, locomotion)

Metabolic energy is partly converted to external mechanical work (**W**) and the rest becomes heat (**M - W**).

## 2.3 Heat Loss — The Four Pathways

The body loses heat to the environment via four physical mechanisms:

| # | Mechanism | Physical Process | Governing Equation | Engineering Utility |
|---|-----------|------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Convection (C)** | Heat transfer to moving air | $C = h_c \cdot f_{cl} \cdot (t_{cl} - t_a)$ | HVAC duct design, fan placement |
| 2 | **Radiation (R)** | Electromagnetic exchange with surfaces | $R = h_r \cdot f_{cl} \cdot (t_{cl} - \bar{t}_r)$ | Workshop shielding, furnace PPE |
| 3 | **Evaporation (E)** | Latent heat loss via sweat | $E = h_e \cdot (p_{sk} - p_a)$ | Humidity control, ventilation |
| 4 | **Conduction (K)** | Direct contact with solids | $K = t_{sk} \cdot k_{cl}$ | Floor mats, tool grips, seating |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Respiration losses** also occur: **Cres** (convective) and **Eres** (evaporative) from breathing warm, humidified air. In HTS design for sedentary work, these are often lumped with convection/evaporation terms.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Exam Frequency |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|----------------|
| 1 | $S = M - W - C - R - E - K - C_{res} - E_{res}$ | **Fanger's General Heat Balance** | W/m² | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $PMV = [0.303 \cdot e^{-0.036M} + 0.028] \cdot L$ | **Predicted Mean Vote** | dimensionless (-3 to +3) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $I_{cl} = 0.155 \cdot d_{clo}$ | **Clothing Insulation (clo)** | m²·K/W | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $SET^* = f(M, I_{cl}, T_a, \bar{T}_r, V_a, RH)$ | **Standard Effective Temperature** | °C | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $WBGT = 0.567 \cdot T_{a} + 0.393 \cdot e_{a} + 3.94$ | **Wet Bulb Globe Temp (outdoor)** | °C | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | $WBGT = 0.7 \cdot T_{nw} + 0.3 \cdot T_g$ | **WBGT (indoor, no sun)** | °C | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | $S = c_p \cdot \rho \cdot V \cdot \frac{dT_{core}}{dt}$ | **Heat Storage Rate** | W | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | $\dot{m}_{sw} = \frac{E_{req}}{h_{fg}}$ | **Required Sweat Rate** | kg/s | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | $DR = \frac{M - W}{A_{Du}}$ | **DuBois Body Surface Area basis** | W/m² | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | $A_{Du} = 0.202 \cdot m^{0.425} \cdot h^{0.725}$ | **DuBois Formula for BSA** | m² | ⭐⭐⭐ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Symbol Glossary:**
> - **M** = Metabolic rate (W/m²)
> - **W** = External mechanical work (W/m²)
> - **C, R, E, K** = Convective, Radiative, Evaporative, Conductive heat loss (W/m²)
> - **t_cl** = Mean clothing surface temperature (°C)
> - **p_sk** = Water vapour pressure at skin (kPa)
> - **p_a** = Water vapour pressure in ambient air (kPa)
> - **h_c, h_r, h_e** = Convective, radiative, evaporative heat transfer coefficients
> - **f_cl** = Clothing area factor (ratio of clothed to nude surface area)
> - **L** = Thermal load on the body (defined in detail below)

## 2.5 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice

The HTS design framework is not academic — it directly governs:

- **Industrial HVAC design** for factories, mines, and process plants.
- **PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)** specification for foundry workers, fire-fighters, cold-storage workers.
- **Vehicle cabin climate control** for driver comfort and alertness.
- **Spacecraft and submarine environmental control systems** where heat dissipation is the limiting factor.
- **Sports and occupational health** legislation (OSHA, ISO 7243, ACGIH TLVs).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix
