| 8 | **NIOSH Lifting Index (LI)** | $LI = \dfrac{\text{Actual Load}}{\text{RWL}}$ | LI ≤ 1.0 safe, > 3.0 hazard |
| 9 | **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** | $RWL = LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot FM \cdot CM$ | LC = 23 kg constant |
| 10 | **Visual angle (minutes)** | $\theta = 3438 \cdot \dfrac{h}{d}$ | h = height, d = distance (m) |
| 11 | **Illuminance (point source)** | $E = \dfrac{I \cos\theta}{d^2}$ | Lux, I = candela |
| 12 | **Allowance for clothing/shoes** | $D_{adj} = D_{base} + 25$ to $40$ mm | Seat height, reach |
| 13 | **Reach ratio (5th female : 95th male)** | $\approx 0.86$ | Population constant |
| 14 | **Proportional correction** | $D_{design} = D_{std} \cdot \dfrac{P_{target}}{P_{std}}$ | Anthropometric scaling |
| 15 | **L5/S1 disc compression limit** | $F_{disc} \le 3400$ N (static) | Manual handling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners *love* the **Z-score formula** (Row 3). Memorize it cold. You will be asked to size a dimension for a given percentile.

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

Detailed design and development is what separates a *drafting exercise* from a *shippable product*. The dimensions, tolerances, and specifications generated here flow directly into:
- **CAD models** (SolidWorks, CATIA human models)
- **Digital human mannequins** (RAMSIS, JACK, SAFEWORK)
- **Ergonomic verification reports** (used in ISO 9241, ISO 6385, EN 1005 compliance)
- **Manufacturing drawings** that the factory floor consumes.

> Without this stage, ergonomic principles remain theoretical — the design would "look comfortable" on paper but fail in the field.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sizing a Workspace Dimension for a Given Percentile

### Problem Context
You are designing a **standing-work counter height** for an industrial inspection station. The 5th percentile female elbow height (standing) is needed to ensure the shortest operator can work without shoulder elevation.

**Given data (from standard anthropometric tables, mixed population):**
- Mean elbow height (standing) $\bar{D} = 1050$ mm
- Standard deviation $\sigma_D = 52$ mm
- Distribution: approximately normal (Gaussian)

**Required:** Elbow height at the 5th percentile.

### Step 1 — Identify the target percentile
We want $P = 5$, meaning 5 % of the population is shorter than this value.

### Step 2 — Find the Z-score for the 5th percentile
For the standard normal distribution, the cumulative probability of $Z = -1.645$ is 0.05 (5 %).

$$Z_{5} = \Phi^{-1}(0.05) = -1.645$$

### Step 3 — Apply the dimensional percentile formula

$$\begin{aligned}
D_P &= \bar{D} + Z \cdot \sigma_D \\
D_{5} &= 1050 + (-1.645) \cdot (52) \\
D_{5} &= 1050 - 85.54 \\
D_{5} &= 964.46 \text{ mm} \\
D_{5} &\approx 964.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Engineering interpretation
The counter must be **at or below 964.5 mm** to avoid forcing the shortest 5 % of female operators to raise their shoulders. For **precision work**, the surface should be set **50–100 mm above elbow height**, giving a recommended counter height of roughly **1015–1065 mm**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Adjustable Seat Height Range (Male/Female Combined Population)

**Given data (typical Western population):**
- 5th percentile female popliteal height (lower leg): $D_{5,F} = 360$ mm
- 95th percentile male popliteal height: $D_{95,M} = 510$ mm
- Shoe allowance (standard): $S = 25$ mm

### Step 1 — Minimum seat height (lowest adjustment position)
This must accommodate the 5th percentile female plus her shoes:

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{seat,min} &= D_{5,F} + S \\
H_{seat,min} &= 360 + 25 \\
H_{seat,min} &= 385 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Maximum seat height (highest adjustment position)
This must accommodate (but not exceed) the 95th percentile male:

$$H_{seat,max} = D_{95,M} = 510 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3 — Total adjustment range

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{adj} &= H_{seat,max} - H_{seat,min} \\
R_{adj} &= 510 - 385 \\
R_{adj} &= 125 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Number of locking detents (engineering specification)
If each detent corresponds to a **25 mm** increment (typical for office chairs):

$$N_{detents} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{R_{adj}}{25} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \dfrac{125}{25} \right\rfloor + 1 = 6 \text{ positions}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (Compact Form)

The NIOSH equation (1981, revised 1991) for the Recommended Weight Limit is:

$$RWL = LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot FM \cdot CM$$

Where:
- $LC = 23$ kg (load constant)
- $HM$ = horizontal multiplier
- $VM$ = vertical multiplier
- $DM$ = distance multiplier
- $AM$ = asymmetry multiplier
- $FM$ = frequency multiplier
- $CM$ = coupling multiplier

### Worked sub-derivation for a typical horizontal multiplier

$$HM = \dfrac{25}{H}$$

Where $H$ = horizontal distance from the load to the mid-point between the ankles (cm), valid for $H \le 63$ cm.

For $H = 30$ cm:

$$\begin{aligned}
HM &= \dfrac{25}{30} \\
HM &= 0.833
\end{aligned}$$

If all other multipliers equal 1.0 (ideal conditions):

$$\begin{aligned}
RWL &= 23 \cdot 0.833 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1.0 \\
RWL &= 19.16 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Dimension-by-Dimension Specification

| Body Dimension | Used For | Design for | Rationale |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Stature | Doorway, cabin height | **95th percentile** | Clearance — everyone must pass |
| Eye height (standing) | Display height for standing | **5th to 95th** range | Adjustable display mount |
| Shoulder breadth | Aisle width, seat width | **95th percentile** | Clearance + comfort |
| Elbow height (sitting) | Desk surface height | **5th percentile** | Reach — surface must not be too high |
| Popliteal height | Seat height (lowest) | **5th percentile** + shoes | Feet must rest on floor |
| Buttock-popliteal length | Seat depth | **95th percentile** | Clearance — short users use cushion |
| Hip breadth | Seat width | **95th percentile** | Clearance |
| Forward reach (functional) | Control placement | **5th percentile** | Reach — must be reachable |
| Vertical reach (standing) | Upper storage shelf | **5th percentile** | Reach — must be reachable |
| Thigh clearance | Underside of desk | **95th percentile** | Clearance |
| Knuckle height | Handle height (pulling) | **5th percentile** | Reach |
| Hand grip diameter (power) | Tool handle | **50 mm ± 10** | Optimal diameter range |
| Pinch grip span | Tweezer / pen | **8–25 mm** | Precision grip window |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Two-Bucket Rule:** If you cannot remember the percentile logic, just ask — *Is this dimension a clearance (must fit the largest) or a reach (must reach for the smallest)?*

## 3.5 Worked Example: Designing a Bus Driver Compartment (Composite)

**Constraints:**
- Population: 5th percentile female to 95th percentile male
- Seat height adjustable, pedals fixed

| Specification | Body Dimension | Value (mm) | Source |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pedal-to-seat-back distance | Buttock-knee length, 95th M | 645 | 95th percentile |
| Seat height (min) | Popliteal, 5th F + shoe | 385 | 5th percentile |
| Seat height (max) | Popliteal, 95th M | 510 | 95th percentile |
| Steering wheel clearance | Chest depth, 95th M | 240 | 95th percentile |
| Steering reach (max) | Forward reach functional, 5th F | 720 | 5th percentile |
| Mirror sight line | Eye-to-eye, 50th | per trial | 50th percentile |
| Dashboard control zone | Functional reach, 5th F | 500 mm sweep | 5th percentile |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Designing seat depth for the 5th percentile (shortest user). This is **WRONG** — short users simply sit forward and use the lumbar support. Designing for the 5th percentile makes the seat too short for tall users, leading to thigh support loss and back pain.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Detailed Design & Development Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Concept Approved] --> B[Identify User Population]
    B --> C[Select Anthropometric Dimensions]
    C --> D{Percentile Decision}
    D -->|Clearance| E[Use 95th Percentile]
    D -->|Reach| F[Use 5th Percentile]
    D -->|Adjustable| G[Span 5th F to 95th M]
    E --> H[Compute Dimensions via Z-score]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Workspace Layout: Reach Zones]
    I --> J[Control-Display Mapping]
    J --> K[Biomechanical & Force Specification]
    K --> L[Environmental Specification]
    L --> M[Build Mock-up / CAD Mannequin]
    M --> N[User Trial with 8-12 Subjects]
    N --> O{Pass Criteria Met?}
    O -->|No| P[Iterate: Modify Spec]
    P --> H
    O -->|Yes| Q[Final Ergonomic Specification]
    Q --> R[Issue Engineering Drawings]
    R --> S[Hand-off to Manufacturing]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Layers of Detailed Ergonomic Specification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHYSI[Layer 1 - Physical / Anthropometric]
        PHYSI1[Stature 95th]
        PHYSI2[Reach 5th]
        PHYSI3[Clearance 95th]
    end

    subgraph BIOM[Layer 2 - Biomechanical]
        BIOM1[Force Limits]
        BIOM2[Posture Angles]
        BIOM3[Lifting Index]
    end

    subgraph COG[Layer 3 - Cognitive / Interface]
        COG1[Fitts Hick Mapping]
        COG2[Display Character Size]
        COG3[Auditory Visual Alarms]
    end

    subgraph ENV[Layer 4 - Environmental]
        ENV1[Illuminance lux]
        ENV2[Noise dB A]
        ENV3[Temperature C]
    end

    subgraph ORG[Layer 5 - Organizational]
        ORG1[Shift Length]
        ORG2[Rest Cycles]
        ORG3[Training Load]
    end

    PHYSI --> FINAL[Integrated Ergonomic Spec Sheet]
    BIOM --> FINAL
    COG --> FINAL
    ENV --> FINAL
    ORG --> FINAL
```

## 4.3 Reach Zone Schematic (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Operator Shoulder Pivot] -->|Upper arm at side, forearm sweep| B[Primary Zone 250-300 mm]
    A -->|Elbow flexed, upper arm forward| C[Secondary Zone 400-500 mm]
    A -->|Full arm extension| D[Tertiary Zone 600-750 mm]
    B --> E[Frequent Controls]
    C --> F[Occasional Controls]
    D --> G[Emergency / Rare Controls]
```

## 4.4 Iterative Prototype Testing Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Detailed Design Spec] --> B[Mock-up Build]
    B --> C[Representative User Trial]
    C --> D[Measure Performance]
    D --> E{Usability Criteria Met}
    E -->|Yes| F[Sign-off]
    E -->|No| G[Root Cause Analysis]
    G --> H[Modify Specification]
    H --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why block diagrams and not physical drawings?**
> Per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol, Mermaid cannot render freehand human figures. The block-level architecture above faithfully captures the *logical* structure of detailed design — the same structure examiners mark in the answer sheets.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
> *Define "detailed design and development" in the context of applied ergonomics. List any two outputs of this stage.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (2 marks):** Detailed design and development is the ergonomic design phase in which an approved concept is converted into a fully dimensioned, validated specification by applying anthropometric, biomechanical, cognitive, and environmental data to ensure that the product, system, or workplace fits the intended user population.
**Outputs (1 mark):** (i) Dimensioned engineering drawings / CAD models, (ii) Ergonomic specification sheet (force, reach, lighting, noise), (iii) Mock-up or prototype for user trials.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> *State the "two-bucket rule" used in ergonomic dimensioning. Give one example for each bucket.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The two-bucket rule states that anthropometric dimensions fall into two categories:
- **Clearance bucket** — dimensions for which the design must accommodate the **95th percentile** (largest user must fit). *Example:* doorway height, seat width, legroom.
- **Reach bucket** — dimensions for which the design must accommodate the **5th percentile** (smallest user must reach). *Example:* emergency stop button height, overhead control reach.
- *(A third hybrid bucket — Adjustable bucket — spans both, e.g., seat height adjustment range.)*

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> *(a)* With neat sketches explain the **three reach zones** (primary, secondary, tertiary) used in workspace design. List **two design rules** for placement of controls in each zone. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* An inspection workstation is to be designed for a mixed-gender adult population. The **elbow height (standing)** data is: mean $\bar{D} = 1020$ mm, standard deviation $\sigma = 48$ mm. Assuming a normal distribution, calculate the **work-surface height** suitable for **precision inspection work** for the **5th percentile female** operator, and justify your choice. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Reach Zones (7 marks)

| Zone | Anatomical Definition | Radius (mm) | Control Placement Rule |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary (Normal)** | Forearm sweep, upper arm vertical at side, elbow flexed 90° | **250 – 300** | (1) Place most frequently used controls here. (2) Use fingertip-only operations; no force > 2 N required. |
| **Secondary (Functional)** | Upper arm swung forward, elbow still flexed, full forearm range | **400 – 500** | (1) Place occasionally used controls. (2) Allow up to 10–20 N force; whole-hand grips acceptable. |
| **Tertiary (Maximum)** | Full arm extension, trunk may rotate slightly | **600 – 750** | (1) Place emergency or very rarely used controls. (2) Two-handed operation; label clearly. |

*Sketch description (as Mermaid cannot draw arms):* Concentric semicircles on the operator's sagittal plane, centered at the shoulder joint.

**Design Rules (any two per zone acceptable, 1 mark each):**
- Primary zone: lowest force, shortest MT (Fitts' Law), best tactile feedback.
- Secondary zone: medium force, comfortable for occasional reach.
- Tertiary zone: high-force push buttons or pull handles; must be marked with contrasting colour.

#### Part (b) — Work Surface Height Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Find the Z-score for the 5th percentile (1 mark):**
$$Z_5 = \Phi^{-1}(0.05) = -1.645$$

**Step 2 — Compute 5th percentile elbow height (2 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
D_5 &= \bar{D} + Z \cdot \sigma \\
D_5 &= 1020 + (-1.645) \cdot (48) \\
D_5 &= 1020 - 78.96 \\
D_5 &= 941.04 \text{ mm} \approx 941 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply precision-work rule (2 marks):**
For **precision work**, the surface should be **50–100 mm above the operator's elbow** to allow unsupported fine motor control.

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{surface,min} &= 941 + 50 = 991 \text{ mm} \\
H_{surface,max} &= 941 + 100 = 1041 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering recommendation (2 marks):**
The recommended work-surface height for precision inspection is **991–1041 mm**, with a fixed design value (for a non-adjustable station) of approximately **1015 mm** (mid-point). The choice of 5th percentile **ensures the shortest female operator is not forced into shoulder elevation**, which is the textbook ergonomic justification for precision tasks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> *(a)* Explain the **percentile selection logic** for three different categories of dimensions: clearance, reach, and adjustable. Provide **one numerical example** for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> *(b)* A control panel has **6 identical push-buttons** of width **15 mm**, spaced **40 mm apart**, on a horizontal surface. Using **Fitts' Law**, compute the **Index of Difficulty (ID)** and the **predicted Movement Time (MT)** for an operator using an index finger. Take the empirical constants as $a = 50$ ms, $b = 150$ ms/bit. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — Percentile Selection Logic (7 marks)

**Category 1 — Clearance dimensions (2.5 marks):**
- Designed for the **95th percentile** so that the **largest** user fits comfortably.
- *Example:* A doorway for an office must clear the 95th percentile male shoulder breadth, say **510 mm**. Designing for 95th guarantees that 95 % of the population passes through without turning sideways.

**Category 2 — Reach dimensions (2.5 marks):**
- Designed for the **5th percentile** so that the **smallest** user can reach the control.
- *Example:* An emergency stop button on a machine must be reachable by the 5th percentile female at a vertical reach of, say, **1850 mm**. Designing for 95th (2200 mm) would leave short operators unable to reach it — a safety hazard.

**Category 3 — Adjustable dimensions (2 marks):**
- Range must span the **5th percentile female to 95th percentile male**.
- *Example:* Office chair seat height adjustment: 5th F popliteal + shoe = **385 mm**; 95th M popliteal = **510 mm**; therefore the range is **385–510 mm** (125 mm travel, e.g., 6 detents × 25 mm).

#### Part (b) — Fitts' Law Application (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters (1 mark):**
- $A$ (distance) = 40 mm (centre-to-centre spacing of adjacent buttons)
- $W$ (target width) = 15 mm

**Step 2 — Compute the Index of Difficulty (2 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{A}{W} + 1\right) \\
ID &= \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{40}{15} + 1\right) \\
ID &= \log_2(2.667 + 1) \\
ID &= \log_2(3.667) \\
ID &= 1.874 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Movement Time (2 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
MT &= a + b \cdot ID \\
MT &= 50 + 150 \cdot (1.874) \\
MT &= 50 + 281.1 \\
MT &= 331.1 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation and engineering recommendation (2 marks):**
- An ID of 1.87 bits corresponds to a **moderately easy targeting task** (ID < 3 bits is acceptable; > 4 bits is considered high difficulty).
- The predicted MT of **~331 ms** is consistent with the empirical range (200–400 ms) for finger-based targeting.
- **Recommendation:** If the operator must use the panel under time pressure (e.g., emergency response), the **button width should be increased to ≥ 25 mm** or the **spacing reduced to ≤ 30 mm** to lower ID below 1.5 bits, reducing MT and error rate.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic (KTU 2024 valuation patterns):**
> 1. **Wrong percentile direction** — Designing seat *width* for the 5th percentile or seat *height* for the 95th percentile. (Loss: 2–3 marks per instance.)
> 2. **Forgetting shoe/clothing allowance** — Seat height calculation must add 25–40 mm for shoes; failing to do so loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping unit conversion** — mm vs cm vs m inconsistency in Fitts' Law / NIOSH calculations. Always state units.
> 4. **Omitting the engineering justification** — Just giving a number without explaining *why* it is the 5th or 95th percentile loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **No user-trial mention** — For 7-mark design questions, KTU now requires at least a *one-line reference* to mock-up / user validation for full marks.
> 6. **Conflating "average" with "median"** — These differ for skewed distributions; in anthropometry, the 50th percentile is the median, not necessarily the mean.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (pin this on your wall before the exam):**

- **Definition:** Detailed design = quantified, dimensioned specification that integrates human factors into a buildable product.
- **Two-bucket rule:** Clearance → 95th percentile; Reach → 5th percentile; Adjustable → span 5th F to 95th M.
- **Three reach zones:** Primary (250–300 mm), Secondary (400–500 mm), Tertiary (600–750 mm).
- **Master formula:** $D_P = \bar{D} + Z \cdot \sigma_D$ (Z = −1.645 for 5th, 0 for 50th, +1.645 for 95th).
- **Adjustable range formula:** $R_{adj} = D_{95,M} - D_{5,F}$ (plus shoe allowance 25–40 mm where relevant).
- **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \log_2(A/W + 1)$ — larger $W$ or smaller $A$ reduces MT.
- **Hick's Law:** $RT = a + b \log_2(n+1)$ — minimize the number of choices.
- **NIOSH equation:** RWL = LC × HM × VM × DM × AM × FM × CM (LC = 23 kg).
- **Static load limit:** ≤ 15 % MVC for sustained effort.
- **L5/S1 disc limit:** ≤ 3400 N static.
- **Lifting index:** LI ≤ 1.0 = safe; LI ≥ 3.0 = serious hazard.
- **Illuminance:** Office 300–500 lux, inspection 750–1000 lux.
- **Noise:** Office ≤ 55 dB(A), industrial 8-hr ≤ 85 dB(A).
- **Comfort temperature:** 20–26 °C; air velocity < 0.25 m/s.
- **Working posture angles:** Elbow 80°–100°, wrist deviation ≤ 15°, trunk forward lean ≤ 20°.
- **User-trial sample size:** 8–12 representative subjects (Nielsen norm).
- **Output deliverables:** (i) Dimensioned drawings, (ii) Ergonomic spec sheet, (iii) Validated prototype.
- **Standards to cite in answers:** ISO 9241, ISO 6385, ISO 11226, EN 1005, NIOSH Lifting Equation.
- **Iteration is mandatory** — KTU awards marks for showing the *spiral*, not the *line*.
- **Always justify your percentile choice** with a one-line ergonomic reason.

> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Detailed Design and Development**
> *(Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 / NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme)*

topics.title: System reliability and modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET604 - APPLIED ERGONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** System reliability and modelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 System Reliability — The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Reliability** is defined as the *probability that a system, together with its human operators and supporting elements, will perform its intended function satisfactorily under stated operating conditions for a specified period of time.*

In the ergonomic context, a "system" is never just a machine — it is a **Man–Machine–Environment (MME) triad**. Therefore, the reliability of any industrial, transport, aviation, or process-control system depends simultaneously on:

1. **Hardware reliability** $R_h(t)$ — reliability of mechanical and electronic components.
2. **Human reliability** $R_{hu}(t)$ — reliability of the operator's cognitive and motor performance.
3. **Software / procedural reliability** $R_s(t)$ — reliability of instructions, displays, and decision-support logic.
4. **Environmental reliability** $R_e(t)$ — robustness against temperature, noise, vibration, illumination.

> [!NOTE]
> The classical Ergonomics literature (e.g., KTU 2024 syllabus framing) defines **System Reliability Modelling** as the *mathematical and graphical representation of how component reliabilities combine to determine the overall system reliability*, including the human operator as one of the components.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Airplane Cockpit"

Imagine an airplane cockpit. The aircraft has four engines. The plane flies **only if all four engines work in a meaningful sense** — if even one engine dies on takeoff, the system is compromised. That is a **series reliability configuration**.

Now imagine there are **three pilots** in the cockpit, and any one of them can land the plane. The system succeeds if **at least one** pilot is functioning correctly. That is a **parallel (redundant) reliability configuration**.

System reliability modelling is simply the science of mathematically deciding: *"Is my cockpit more like the engines (series) or the pilots (parallel)?"* And then computing the probability that the whole system succeeds.

### 1.3 Core Reliability Metrics (Always Bold for the Board)

| Metric | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Failure Rate** | $\lambda$ | Constant $\lambda$ (failures per hour) for exponential distribution |
| **Mean Time Between Failures** | **MTBF** | $\frac{1}{\lambda}$ for exponential |
| **Mean Time To Repair** | **MTTR** | Average downtime per failure |
| **Availability** | $A$ | $\frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ |
| **Unreliability** | $F(t)$ | $1 - R(t)$ |
| **Hazard Function** | $h(t)$ | Instantaneous failure rate at age $t$ |

> [!TIP]
> In a 14-mark KTU question, the examiner will **always** test whether you remembered the difference between **MTBF** and **MTTR** — these are the most-missed terms in the answer script.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Reliability Function $R(t)$

The reliability function is the probability that a component or system survives up to time $t$:

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

where $T$ is the random variable representing the time-to-failure.

If the failure density function is $f(t)$ and the cumulative failure (unreliability) function is $F(t)$, then:

$$R(t) = 1 - F(t) = 1 - \int_{0}^{t} f(\tau)\, d\tau$$

For a large class of electronic and mechanical components, the failure rate $\lambda$ is approximately **constant** during the useful life (the flat middle of the "bathtub curve"). Under this assumption we get the **exponential reliability model**:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

This is the single most-tested reliability equation in KTU papers.

## 2.2 The Bathtub Curve

Reliability over the life of a component is typically described by the **Bathtub Curve**, which has three regions:

1. **Infant Mortality (Burn-in)** — Decreasing failure rate due to manufacturing defects.
2. **Useful Life (Random Failures)** — Constant failure rate $\lambda$. This is where the exponential model applies.
3. **Wear-out Region** — Increasing failure rate due to aging, fatigue, corrosion.

> [!NOTE]
> The exponential model $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is valid **only** in Region 2. Examiners frequently award 2 marks simply for stating this assumption.

## 2.3 Series, Parallel, and Combined Configurations

### 2.3.1 Series System (Weakest-Link)

In a series configuration, **all components must work** for the system to succeed. The system's reliability is the **product** of the individual reliabilities:

$$R_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A series system is **always less reliable** than its least-reliable component. This is a counterintuitive but board-favourite point.

### 2.3.2 Parallel (Redundant) System

In a parallel configuration, **at least one component must work**. The system fails only if **all** components fail. The unreliabilities multiply:

$$R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} \big(1 - R_i(t)\big)$$

> [!TIP]
> For a parallel system with $n$ identical components each of reliability $R$, the formula simplifies to $R_{parallel} = 1 - (1-R)^n$.

### 2.3.3 Combined (Series–Parallel) Systems

Real ergonomic systems (e.g., an aircraft's flight control loop: sensor → computer → actuator → display → pilot) are neither pure series nor pure parallel. We simplify the block diagram by collapsing sub-blocks iteratively using the two rules above.

### 2.4 Human Reliability

Humans are the **least reliable** component in any Man–Machine system. The probability of human error in routine industrial tasks is often modelled using the **Swain–Guttmann Human Error Rate Prediction (THERP)** technique, but for KTU-level problems we use a simple deterministic human-reliability value $R_{hu} \in [0.7,\, 0.9999]$.

For a typical **man–machine series system**:

$$R_{system} = R_h \times R_{hu}$$

where $R_h$ is the hardware reliability.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reliability Function | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Dimensionless probability |
| 2 | Unreliability | $F(t) = 1 - R(t)$ | $0 \le F \le 1$ |
| 3 | Mean Life (Exponential) | $\theta = \text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Hours |
| 4 | Series System | $R_s = \prod R_i$ | Less than smallest $R_i$ |
| 5 | Parallel System | $R_p = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$ | Greater than largest $R_i$ |
| 6 | Two-Component Parallel | $R_p = R_1 + R_2 - R_1 R_2$ | Special case |
| 7 | Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Fraction or % |
| 8 | Inherent Availability | $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBM}}{\text{MTBM} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Excludes logistics delay |
| 9 | Hazard / Failure Rate | $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)}$ | Failures / hour |
| 10 | Weibull Distribution | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $\beta$ = shape, $\eta$ = scale |
| 11 | M/M/1 Queue Availability | $A = 1 - \rho$ | $\rho = \lambda / \mu$ |
| 12 | Human–Machine Series | $R_{mm} = R_h \cdot R_{hu}$ | Used in ergonomic analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> The vertical-bar absolute value in M/M/1 expressions like $\vert 1 - \rho \vert$ should be rendered as $\mid 1 - \rho \mid$ in your answer script to avoid the markdown table breaking.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation:** Redundant parallel hydraulic and electrical systems in fly-by-wire aircraft are designed to meet ultra-high $R_{system} \ge 0.999999999$ (the "nine nines" requirement).
- **Nuclear Power Plants:** Safety-instrumented systems use 2-out-of-3 (2oo3) voting logic, which is a *combined series–parallel* redundancy.
- **Automotive:** Anti-lock braking systems (ABS) use parallel wheel-speed sensors.
- **Healthcare:** Patient monitoring in ICUs uses parallel redundant sensors to avoid alarm fatigue.
- **Process Industry:** Safety Integrity Levels (SIL 1–4) in IEC 61508 require quantitative reliability targets that are computed exactly as in our series/parallel formulas.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Reliability as a Poisson-Process Outcome

**Starting Premise:** For a non-repairable component with constant failure rate $\lambda$, the number of failures $N(t)$ in time $t$ follows a Poisson distribution with mean $\lambda t$:

$$P(N(t) = k) = \frac{(\lambda t)^k e^{-\lambda t}}{k!}$$

The component is **operational** if and only if **zero** failures have occurred in $[0, t]$, i.e., $N(t) = 0$.

**Step 1.** Substitute $k = 0$ into the Poisson PMF:

$$P(N(t) = 0) = \frac{(\lambda t)^0 e^{-\lambda t}}{0!}$$

**Step 2.** Evaluate the trivial factors: $(\lambda t)^0 = 1$ and $0! = 1$.

$$P(N(t) = 0) = 1 \cdot e^{-\lambda t} \cdot 1$$

**Step 3.** By the definition of the reliability function $R(t) \equiv P(\text{no failure in } [0,t]) = P(N(t)=0)$:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 4.** Differentiation yields the failure density $f(t)$:

$$f(t) = -\frac{dR}{dt} = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 5.** The mean-time-to-failure is:

$$\text{MTBF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t f(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \lambda e^{-\lambda t}\, dt$$

Using integration by parts (or the Gamma function identity $\Gamma(2) = 1!$):

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

Hence $\boxed{R(t) = e^{-t/\text{MTBF}}}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Series System Reliability from Independence

**Premise:** Components $1, 2, \ldots, n$ are *statistically independent* and the system succeeds only if **all** components succeed.

**Step 1.** The event "system survives" is the intersection:

$$S = \bigcap_{i=1}^{n} S_i$$

**Step 2.** By the multiplication rule for independent events:

$$P(S) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(S_i)$$

**Step 3.** Substituting $P(S_i) = R_i$:

$$R_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$$

**Step 4.** With constant $\lambda_i$ for component $i$:

$$R_{series} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t} = e^{-\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i\right) t}$$

**Step 5.** Therefore the equivalent system failure rate is additive:

$$\lambda_{sys} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$$

This is the **key engineering insight**: adding components to a series system *always* lowers its reliability.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Parallel System Reliability from De Morgan's Laws

**Premise:** System fails only if **all** components fail. The failure event is the intersection of the failure events $\bar{S_i}$.

**Step 1.** Failure of the system:

$$\bar{S} = \bigcap_{i=1}^{n} \bar{S_i}$$

**Step 2.** By De Morgan's law on the complement of the union:

$$P(\bar{S}) = 1 - P\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} S_i\right)$$

**Step 3.** Using independence of the complements $P(\bar{S_i}) = 1 - R_i$:

$$P(\bar{S}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$$

**Step 4.** Subtract from unity to obtain system reliability:

$$R_{parallel} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)$$

**Step 5.** For $n = 2$ identical components with reliability $R$ each:

$$R_{parallel} = 1 - (1-R)^2 = 2R - R^2$$

**Step 6.** For $n = 3$ identical components:

$$R_{parallel} = 1 - (1-R)^3 = 3R - 3R^2 + R^3$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Steady-State Availability

**Premise:** The system alternates between an UP state of expected duration MTBF and a DOWN state of expected duration MTTR.

**Step 1.** The long-run fraction of time spent UP is the ratio of UP-duration to the total cycle time:

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

**Step 2.** Let $u = \text{MTBF}$ and $d = \text{MTTR}$. Cycle length is $u + d$. Probability of being in UP is $u / (u + d)$ by renewal-reward theorem.

**Step 3.** Define the *unavailability* $U = 1 - A$:

$$U = \frac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For mission-critical ergonomic systems (e.g., anaesthetic-gas delivery), the design target is $U \le 10^{-6}$, i.e., less than 30 seconds of downtime per year.

## 3.5 Worked Example (Long Form, 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A control panel contains three push-buttons in series, each with $R = 0.95$ at $t = 1000$ hours. A backup parallel indicator lamp is added with $R_{lamp} = 0.90$. The combined button-string is in series with the parallel lamp block. Compute (a) system reliability and (b) MTBF, assuming constant failure rates.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Button-string reliability:

$$R_{btns} = 0.95 \times 0.95 \times 0.95 = 0.857375$$

Parallel-lamp block:

$$R_{lamps} = 1 - (1 - 0.90)(1 - 0.90) = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99$$

System reliability:

$$R_{sys} = R_{btns} \times R_{lamps} = 0.857375 \times 0.99 = 0.8488$$

**(b)** For each button, $\lambda_b = -\ln(0.95)/1000 = 0.0005129\text{ hr}^{-1}$. For the lamp, $\lambda_l = -\ln(0.90)/1000 = 0.0001054\text{ hr}^{-1}$.

Parallel-lamp equivalent failure rate (from Section 3.2 result, system reliability in parallel does not decompose into a single $\lambda$ unless the parts are identical; for two identical lamps $R_{par} = 2R - R^2$, we can solve for an equivalent $\lambda$ numerically).

$$e^{-\lambda_{par} t} = 0.99 \Rightarrow \lambda_{par} = -\frac{\ln 0.99}{1000} = 0.00001005\text{ hr}^{-1}$$

System failure rate (series addition):

$$\lambda_{sys} = 3\lambda_b + \lambda_{par} = 3(0.0005129) + 0.00001005 = 0.001549\text{ hr}^{-1}$$

MTBF:

$$\text{MTBF}_{sys} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{sys}} = \frac{1}{0.001549} \approx 645.6 \text{ hours}$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of Series vs Parallel

| Number of components $n$ | Identical $R_i$ | $R_{series}$ | $R_{parallel}$ | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.95 | 0.950 | 0.950 | Baseline |
| 2 | 0.95 | 0.903 | 0.998 | Series drops 5%; parallel jumps 5% |
| 3 | 0.95 | 0.857 | 0.99993 | Series collapses; parallel saturates near 1 |
| 5 | 0.95 | 0.774 | 0.99999997 | Series unacceptable; parallel almost perfect |
| 10 | 0.99 | 0.904 | 0.9999999999 | Even high-$R$ parts decay fast in series |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reliability Block Diagram — Series Man-Machine System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Human Operator Rhu] --> A2[Display Unit Rd]
    A2 --> A3[Controller Rc]
    A3 --> A4[Actuator Ra]
    A4 --> A5[Process Plant Rp]
    A5 --> A6((System Output))
    style A1 fill:#ffd6d6
    style A6 fill:#d6ffd6
```

> The arrow direction here is *information / causal flow*, not current flow. Every block must succeed for the system to succeed — this is a **series** reliability topology.

## 4.2 Reliability Block Diagram — Parallel Redundancy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In((Input)) --> B1[Channel A Ra]
    In --> B2[Channel B Rb]
    In --> B3[Channel C Rc]
    B1 --> Out((Output))
    B2 --> Out
    B3 --> Out
    style Out fill:#d6ffd6
```

> Three parallel channels feeding a common output. The system succeeds if **any** channel works. This is the standard model for triplex flight-control computers and 2-out-of-3 voting logic.

## 4.3 Combined Series-Parallel Architecture (Aircraft Spoiler System)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pilot 1] --> C1[Computer 1]
    P2[Pilot 2] --> C2[Computer 2]
    P3[Copilot] --> C3[Computer 3]
    C1 --> A1[Actuator 1]
    C2 --> A2[Actuator 2]
    C3 --> A3[Actuator 3]
    A1 --> S((Spoiler))
    A2 --> S
    A3 --> S
    style S fill:#fff3b0
```

> Three parallel (pilot–computer–actuator) branches converge on the spoiler. The spoiler deploys as long as at least one branch is functional. This is a *high-level* parallel topology where each branch is itself a series of subsystems.

## 4.4 System Reliability Life-Cycle (Bathtub Curve Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Region 1: Burn-in\nDecreasing Failure Rate] --> R2[Region 2: Useful Life\nConstant Lambda\nExponential Model]
    R2 --> R3[Region 3: Wear-out\nIncreasing Failure Rate]
    R1 -.->|Manufacture Test| P[Production]
    P --> R1
    R3 -.->|Replacement| X((Retire))
    style R2 fill:#d6ffd6
    style R1 fill:#ffd6d6
    style R3 fill:#ffe0b3
```

> The shaded green Region 2 is the *operational validity zone* for the equation $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. Examiners love asking why the exponential model breaks down in Regions 1 and 3.

## 4.5 Availability — UP/DOWN Renewal Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    U[UP State\nDuration MTBF] -->|Failure Event| D[DOWN State\nDuration MTTR]
    D -->|Repair Complete| U
    style U fill:#d6ffd6
    style D fill:#ffd6d6
```

> Over many cycles, the proportion of time the system is in the UP state is the steady-state availability $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define system reliability. How is it different from availability?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** *System reliability* is the probability that a system performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified period of time.
- **(1 mark)** *Availability* is the long-run fraction of time the system is operational, accounting for both operating and repair durations.
- **(1 mark)** Reliability is a *time-bound, no-repair* probability; availability considers *repairable* systems and is the steady-state operational fraction $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$.

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a parallel redundant system? Write its reliability expression for two identical components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** A parallel redundant system is one in which the system functions as long as **at least one** component functions.
- **(1 mark)** Unreliabilities multiply: $R_p = 1 - (1 - R_1)(1 - R_2)$.
- **(1 mark)** For two identical components each with reliability $R$, the expression reduces to $R_p = 2R - R^2$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** A safety alarm system consists of **two sensors in parallel** ($R_1 = 0.92$, $R_2 = 0.88$), connected **in series** with a logic controller ($R_c = 0.95$) and a horn ($R_h = 0.99$). Compute:
1. (3 marks) The reliability of the parallel sensor block.
2. (4 marks) The reliability of the entire alarm system.

**(b)** For the horn alone, the failure rate is constant at $\lambda_h = 0.0005\text{ hr}^{-1}$.
1. (3 marks) Compute the reliability of the horn at $t = 500$ hours.
2. (4 marks) If the MTTR of the horn is 4 hours, compute the steady-state availability of the horn.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### (a1) Parallel sensor block [3 marks]

$$R_{sens} = 1 - (1 - 0.92)(1 - 0.88)$$
$$= 1 - (0.08 \times 0.12) = 1 - 0.0096 = \boxed{0.9904}$$

#### (a2) Total system reliability [4 marks]

The series chain is: **sensor block → controller → horn**.

$$R_{sys} = R_{sens} \times R_c \times R_h = 0.9904 \times 0.95 \times 0.99$$

Multiplying step-by-step:

$$0.9904 \times 0.95 = 0.9409$$
$$0.9409 \times 0.99 = \boxed{0.9315}$$

#### (b1) Horn reliability at $t = 500$ hr [3 marks]

$$R_h(500) = e^{-\lambda_h t} = e^{-0.0005 \times 500} = e^{-0.25}$$

$$e^{-0.25} \approx \boxed{0.7788}$$

#### (b2) Availability of horn [4 marks]

MTBF $= 1/\lambda_h = 1/0.0005 = 2000$ hours.

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{2000}{2000 + 4} = \frac{2000}{2004} \approx \boxed{0.9980 \;(99.80\%)}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the **bathtub curve** of component reliability with a neat diagram. Discuss the three regions and identify which region justifies the exponential model $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. (7 marks)

**(b)** A man–machine system consists of a hardware block with $R_h = 0.98$ in series with a human operator block with $R_{hu} = 0.95$. To improve safety, an *identical* redundant hardware unit is added in parallel.
1. (3 marks) Compute the new hardware-block reliability.
2. (4 marks) Compute the new overall system reliability and the percentage improvement.

---

### MODEL SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### (a) Bathtub Curve Explanation [7 marks]

- **(1 mark)** The bathtub curve plots failure rate $h(t)$ on the Y-axis and time $t$ on the X-axis. It is shaped like a bathtub in cross-section.
- **(2 marks) Region I — Infant Mortality:** Failure rate *decreases* with time. Caused by manufacturing defects, poor quality control, substandard materials. Mitigated by *burn-in* testing before shipment.
- **(2 marks) Region II — Useful Life:** Failure rate is *approximately constant* at $\lambda$. Failures are random and independent. The exponential reliability model $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is valid **only** in this region.
- **(2 marks) Region III — Wear-out:** Failure rate *increases* with time. Caused by fatigue, corrosion, aging, wear. The Weibull model with $\beta > 1$ is more appropriate here.

#### (b1) New hardware-block reliability [3 marks]

Two identical hardware units in parallel, each with $R_h = 0.98$:

$$R_{h,new} = 1 - (1 - 0.98)(1 - 0.98) = 1 - (0.02)^2 = 1 - 0.0004 = \boxed{0.9996}$$

#### (b2) New system reliability & improvement [4 marks]

Old system reliability:

$$R_{old} = 0.98 \times 0.95 = 0.9310$$

New system reliability (parallel hardware block in series with human):

$$R_{new} = 0.9996 \times 0.95 = 0.9496$$

Percentage improvement:

$$\Delta\% = \frac{0.9496 - 0.9310}{0.9310} \times 100 = \frac{0.0186}{0.9310} \times 100 \approx \boxed{2.00\% \text{ improvement}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **MTBF vs MTTR confusion (–2 marks typical):** Students often invert the availability formula. Memorize: $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$, *not* the reciprocal.
> 2. **Series vs Parallel sign error (–3 marks typical):** Series *multiplies* reliabilities; parallel *multiplies unreliabilities* and then *subtracts from 1*. The most common mistake is writing $R_{par} = R_1 R_2$ (which is series).
> 3. **Units of $\lambda$:** Forgetting to convert failure rate to per-hour units costs 1 mark in any numerical problem.
> 4. **Forgetting independence:** The product rule $R_s = \prod R_i$ requires statistical independence of component failures. If dependencies exist (e.g., common-cause failures), the answer is **invalid** — examiners will deduct a mark if you blindly multiply.
> 5. **Exponential model outside Region II:** Applying $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ to infant-mortality or wear-out data is a *theoretical error*; mention the Weibull alternative for full marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Reliability = probability of no failure up to time $t$.** $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$.
- **Exponential model:** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, valid only in the **constant-failure-rate** region (Region II of the bathtub curve).
- **MTBF** for exponential model is $1/\lambda$. **Never** confuse with MTTR.
- **Series system:** all components must work → $R_s = \prod R_i$ → adding components *reduces* reliability.
- **Parallel system:** at least one component must work → $R_p = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$ → adding components *improves* reliability.
- **Two-identical-component parallel:** $R_p = 2R - R^2$. Three-identical: $R_p = 3R - 3R^2 + R^3$.
- **Equivalent failure rate of series system** = sum of individual failure rates: $\lambda_{sys} = \sum \lambda_i$.
- **Availability** $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$; **Unavailability** $U = 1 - A$.
- **Man–Machine series:** $R_{mm} = R_h \times R_{hu}$ — humans typically have the lowest reliability.
- **Combined systems:** collapse inner blocks first using the two-product rules, then propagate outwards.
- **Weibull extension** (for non-constant $\lambda$): $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$, with $\beta < 1$ for burn-in, $\beta = 1$ for exponential, $\beta > 1$ for wear-out.
- **Hazard function:** $h(t) = f(t)/R(t)$ — instantaneous failure rate at age $t$.
- **High-reliability rule of thumb:** for safety-critical ergonomic systems, design target $R \ge 0.99999$ (SIL-3/4).
- **Independence assumption** is required for the product rule — flag it in your answer if common-cause failures exist.
- **In the human-in-the-loop:** the operator is *always* a series element, so the maximum theoretical system reliability is bounded above by the operator's own reliability.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition and measurement of information
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET604 - APPLIED ERGONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Definition and measurement of information

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Information (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Information** is a precisely quantifiable measure of the **reduction of uncertainty** that occurs when a particular message, symbol, or event is received from a source. Mathematically, it is the negative logarithm of the probability of occurrence of an event, expressed in **bits** (binary digits) when base 2 is used.

In the context of **Applied Ergonomics**, information is not merely "data" — it is the *meaningful, decision-relevant content* extracted from a display, signal, or stimulus that reduces the operator's uncertainty about the state of the system being controlled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-tier hierarchy used by KTU examiners:**
> - **Data** → Raw, unprocessed facts (e.g., voltage readings, RGB pixel values).
> - **Information** → Processed data that reduces uncertainty (e.g., "engine temperature is 95°C — within safe range").
> - **Knowledge** → Information integrated with context and experience (e.g., recognizing a thermal trend predicts failure).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

**Real-world analogy: The Traffic Signal Analogy**

Imagine you are a driver approaching an intersection at night. Before you see the signal, there are **three possible states** (Red, Yellow, Green). Your uncertainty is high — any of the three could be active. The moment you see the signal, your uncertainty collapses to **zero**, because you now know the exact state.

How much *information* did the signal convey?
- **3 equally likely alternatives** → $I = \log_2(3) = 1.585$ bits.
- Compare with a binary switch (ON/OFF, 2 alternatives) → $I = \log_2(2) = 1$ bit.
- The traffic signal carries **more information per observation** because it discriminates among more states.

**Ergonomic insight:** A control panel with 10 unlabeled identical buttons gives the operator **far more uncertainty** (and therefore carries more potential information) than a panel with 3 well-labelled buttons — but if the 10 buttons are poorly distinguishable, the *effective* information transmitted drops due to perceptual noise. This is the central ergonomic problem of **display-coding efficiency**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **1 bit** = the information required to discriminate between **2 equally likely alternatives** ($1 = \log_2 2$).
- **1 nat** = information measured using natural logarithm; **1 bit = ln 2 ≈ 0.6931 nats**; **1 nat ≈ 1.4427 bits**.
- **1 Hartley (or decit)** = information measured using base-10 logarithm; **1 Hartley = log₂ 10 ≈ 3.3219 bits**.
- **Channel capacity** is measured in **bits per second (bps)** when time is involved.
- **Entropy** (average information) is measured in **bits per symbol**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the **SI system**, the unit of information is the **shannon** (symbol: Sh), formally adopted in 2021, equal to 1 bit of information content. KTU exam answers should consistently use "**bits**" unless the base is explicitly different.

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Applied Ergonomics

Information theory is the **mathematical backbone** of human–machine interface design because:

1. **Displays** are information channels — their capacity must match human sensory channel capacity.
2. **Controls** generate information at rates determined by the operator's motor and cognitive limits.
3. **Workload** can be quantified as the **bit-rate of information processing** demanded of the operator.
4. **Signal detection**, vigilance, and absolute identification all have precise information-theoretic limits (Miller's 7±2, Hick's law, Fitts' law in bits).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — From Hartley to Shannon

### A. Hartley's Classical Measure (1928)

Ralph Hartley, working at Bell Labs, proposed the first rigorous measure of information:

$$I = \log_b(N) \text{ bits (when } b = 2\text{)}$$

**Operational logic — step by step:**

- A source emits one of **N** symbols, all **equally likely** ($p = 1/N$).
- To identify *which* symbol was emitted, an observer must ask binary (yes/no) questions.
- Each question halves the uncertainty → it yields **1 bit**.
- Number of questions required to pinpoint the symbol = $\log_2(N)$.
- Therefore: $I = \log_2(N) = \log_2(1/p)$ bits.

**Why it works:** Logarithms convert the *counting* of alternatives into an *additive* information measure. If two independent sources each offer N alternatives, the joint source offers $N^2$ alternatives, and $\log_2(N^2) = 2\log_2(N)$ — information adds naturally.

### B. Shannon's Self-Information (1948)

Claude Shannon extended Hartley's idea to **unequally likely** events. For a single event $x_i$ with probability $p_i$:

$$I(x_i) = -\log_2(p_i) = \log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{p_i}\right) \text{ bits}$$

**Axiomatic justification (the three Shannon axioms):**

1. $I(x_i)$ must be a **continuous, monotonically decreasing** function of $p_i$.
2. $I(x_i) \geq 0$ for all $p_i \in [0,1]$, and $I = 0$ when $p_i = 1$ (a certain event carries no information).
3. For two **independent** events, information must be **additive**: $I(p_1 \cdot p_2) = I(p_1) + I(p_2)$.

The **only** function satisfying all three is the logarithm. The negative sign ensures the value is positive (since $0 < p \leq 1$).

### C. Shannon's Entropy — Average Information per Symbol

For a discrete source with alphabet $\{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ and probability distribution $\{p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n\}$:

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i \text{ bits/symbol}$$

This is the **expected value** of self-information, $H(X) = E[I(X)]$.

**Key properties of entropy:**

- $H(X) \geq 0$ (non-negativity).
- $H(X) = 0$ iff the source is deterministic (one symbol has $p = 1$).
- $H(X) \leq \log_2(n)$, with **equality when all symbols are equiprobable** (maximum uncertainty).
- $H(X)$ is **concave** in the probability distribution (implications for human decision consistency).

### D. Joint, Conditional, and Mutual Information

For two related random variables $X$ and $Y$:

$$H(X, Y) = -\sum_{i}\sum_{j} p(x_i, y_j) \log_2 p(x_i, y_j)$$

$$H(X \mid Y) = -\sum_{i}\sum_{j} p(x_i, y_j) \log_2 p(x_i \mid y_j)$$

$$I(X; Y) = H(X) - H(X \mid Y) = H(Y) - H(Y \mid X)$$

**Ergonomic meaning of mutual information $I(X;Y)$:** the amount of information that the display output $Y$ provides *about* the system state $X$. This is the **usable information** for the operator.

### E. Channel Capacity & Information Rate

$$C = \max_{p(x)} I(X; Y) \text{ bits/symbol (channel capacity)}$$

$$R = r_s \cdot H(X) \text{ bits/second (information rate of source)}$$

where $r_s$ = symbol rate in symbols/second. Reliable communication requires $R \leq C$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Key Condition |
|---|----------|---------|-------|----------------|
| 1 | Hartley's information | $I = \log_2(N)$ | bits | Equally likely outcomes, $N$ alternatives |
| 2 | Self-information | $I(x_i) = -\log_2(p_i)$ | bits | Single event of probability $p_i$ |
| 3 | Shannon entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits/symbol | Discrete source with distribution $\{p_i\}$ |
| 4 | Maximum entropy | $H_{\max} = \log_2(n)$ | bits/symbol | $n$ equiprobable symbols |
| 5 | Joint entropy | $H(X, Y) = -\sum_i\sum_j p(x_i,y_j)\log_2 p(x_i,y_j)$ | bits | Pair of random variables |
| 6 | Conditional entropy | $H(X \mid Y) = -\sum_i\sum_j p(x_i,y_j)\log_2 p(x_i \mid y_j)$ | bits | Given observation of $Y$ |
| 7 | Mutual information | $I(X;Y) = H(X) - H(X \mid Y)$ | bits | Information shared by $X$ and $Y$ |
| 8 | Channel capacity | $C = \max I(X;Y)$ | bits/symbol or bits/sec | Maximized over source distribution |
| 9 | Source information rate | $R = r_s \cdot H(X)$ | bits/second | $r_s$ = symbol transmission rate |
| 10 | Ergonomic coding efficiency | $\eta = I(X;Y) \,/\, H(X)$ | dimensionless | $\eta \leq 1$ |
| 11 | Hick–Hyman reaction time | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | seconds | $n$ equiprobable choices |
| 12 | Fitts' law (ID) | $ID = \log_2(2A/W)$ | bits | Motor targeting task |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical for KTU board exams:** Units are *mandatory*. A correct numerical answer without "bits" or "bits/symbol" usually loses 0.5–1 mark in the valuation key.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aircraft cockpit displays** are engineered so that the *information rate* of any single display does not exceed the pilot's perceptual channel capacity (~10–50 bits/sec for combined visual+auditory channels under stress).
- **Medical ECG monitors** use entropy analysis to detect arrhythmias — regions of high entropy indicate irregular (high-uncertainty) heart rhythms.
- **Industrial control rooms** use Hick's law to limit the number of alarm states a worker must discriminate among during emergencies (target: $\leq 4$ to keep reaction time within safe bounds).
- **Rehabilitation engineering** uses Fitts' law in bit-form to design accessible touchscreens (predicting movement time for users with motor impairments).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hartley's Formula from First Principles

**Premise:** A source emits one of $N$ equally likely symbols. The receiver must identify *which* symbol was sent. We seek the minimum number of binary (yes/no) questions required.

**Step 1 — Binary halving strategy.**
Each well-designed yes/no question splits the remaining set of candidates into two equal halves. After $k$ questions, the set is reduced to $N / 2^k$ candidates.

**Step 2 — Termination condition.**
Identification is complete when only 1 candidate remains:

$$\frac{N}{2^k} = 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 2^k = N \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; k = \log_2(N)$$

**Step 3 — Convert to information.**
Each binary question resolves 1 bit of uncertainty:

$$I = k \cdot 1 = \log_2(N) \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of probability.**
For equiprobable symbols, $p_i = 1/N$:

$$I = \log_2(N) = \log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{p_i}\right) = -\log_2(p_i)$$

This is the **self-information** formula generalized to the equiprobable case. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Self-Information from the Three Shannon Axioms

**Axiom 1:** $I(p)$ is continuous and monotonically decreasing in $p$.
**Axiom 2:** $I(1) = 0$ (a certain event yields no surprise).
**Axiom 3:** $I(p_1 \cdot p_2) = I(p_1) + I(p_2)$ for independent events.

**Step 1 — Assume a power-law form.**
By Axiom 1, $I(p)$ is smooth. Try $I(p) = c \cdot p^{\alpha}$ for constants $c, \alpha$:

- Axiom 2: $I(1) = c = 0$ → contradiction unless we change form.

**Step 2 — Switch to a logarithmic candidate.**
Try $I(p) = c \cdot \log_b(p)$:

- Axiom 2: $I(1) = c \log_b(1) = 0$ ✓
- Axiom 3: $I(p_1 p_2) = c \log_b(p_1 p_2) = c[\log_b(p_1) + \log_b(p_2)] = I(p_1) + I(p_2)$ ✓
- Axiom 1 (monotonicity): requires $c < 0$ (since $\log_b(p)$ is increasing for $p \in (0,1)$ and we want $I$ to *decrease* with $p$).

**Step 3 — Enforce non-negativity.**
For $p \in (0, 1)$, $\log_b(p) < 0$. Therefore $c < 0$ makes $I(p) > 0$. ✓

**Step 4 — Choose the base.**
With $b = 2$ (binary base) and $c = -1$:

$$I(p) = -\log_2(p) \text{ bits}$$

This is the **unique** function satisfying all three axioms. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shannon's Entropy as Expected Self-Information

**Premise:** A source emits symbol $x_i$ with probability $p_i$. The information conveyed by $x_i$ is $I(x_i) = -\log_2 p_i$. We seek the *average* information per symbol.

**Step 1 — Define the average.**

$$\bar{I} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot I(x_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot (-\log_2 p_i)$$

**Step 2 — Factor the negative sign.**

$$H(X) = \bar{I} = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i \text{ bits/symbol}$$

This is Shannon's entropy. $\blacksquare$

**Step 3 — Verification: equiprobable case.**
If $p_i = 1/n$ for all $i$:

$$H = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{n} \log_2\!\left(\frac{1}{n}\right) = -n \cdot \frac{1}{n} \cdot (-\log_2 n) = \log_2(n)$$

Recovers Hartley's formula. ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Information Rate of a Source

**Premise:** A source produces $r_s$ symbols per second. Each symbol carries on average $H(X)$ bits of information.

**Step 1 — Definitions.**

$$R = (\text{symbols/sec}) \times (\text{bits/symbol}) = r_s \cdot H(X) \text{ bits/second}$$

**Step 2 — Alternative forms.**
Using $r_s = 1/T_s$ where $T_s$ is the symbol duration:

$$R = \frac{H(X)}{T_s} \text{ bits/second}$$

**Step 3 — Ergonomic application.**
If a typist generates characters at $r_s = 5$ chars/sec and each character is one of 26 equiprobable letters:

$$R = 5 \cdot \log_2(26) = 5 \times 4.700 = 23.5 \text{ bits/sec}$$

This approximates the *upper bound* of human text-production information rate.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Examples (Board-Exam Ready)

### Example 1 — Single Event Information `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 style]`

**Problem:** A flight-deck warning light is on with probability 0.05 during a flight. Calculate the information conveyed when the light illuminates.

**Solution:**

$$I = -\log_2(0.05) = \frac{\log_{10}(0.05)}{-\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{-1.3010}{-0.3010} = 4.322 \text{ bits}$$

**Interpretation:** A rare event conveys more information than a common one. The 5% warning event gives the pilot more useful "news" than a 50% event would.

---

### Example 2 — Shannon Entropy of a Source `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 style]`

**Problem:** A digital instrument panel displays four indicator states with probabilities: A = 0.5, B = 0.25, C = 0.125, D = 0.125. Compute the average information per display.

**Solution:**

$$H = -[0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125]$$

$$H = -[(0.5)(-1) + (0.25)(-2) + (0.125)(-3) + (0.125)(-3)]$$

$$H = -[-0.500 - 0.500 - 0.375 - 0.375] = 1.750 \text{ bits/display}$$

**Compare with maximum:** $H_{\max} = \log_2(4) = 2$ bits. The current source operates at $\eta = 1.75/2 = 87.5\%$ of theoretical capacity.

---

### Example 3 — Hick–Hyman Law in Ergonomics `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 style]`

**Problem:** An industrial control panel has 4 emergency-stop buttons, each equally likely. Using Hick's law, $T = 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ ms, find the predicted reaction time. Then repeat for a redesigned panel with 1 button and compare.

**Solution (Case 1: 4 buttons):**

$$T_1 = 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(4 + 1) = 200 + 150 \cdot 2.322 = 200 + 348.3 = 548.3 \text{ ms}$$

**Solution (Case 2: 1 button):**

$$T_2 = 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(1 + 1) = 200 + 150 \cdot 1 = 350 \text{ ms}$$

**Time saved:** $\Delta T = 548.3 - 350 = 198.3$ ms — a critical margin in emergency situations.

---

### Example 4 — Fitts' Law as Information `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 style]`

**Problem:** A surgeon moves a pointer from a starting position to a target of width $W = 10$ mm located $A = 200$ mm away. Compute the **index of difficulty** in bits using Fitts' information-theoretic form.

**Solution:**

$$ID = \log_2\!\left(\frac{2A}{W}\right) = \log_2\!\left(\frac{2 \times 200}{10}\right) = \log_2(40) = 5.322 \text{ bits}$$

**Engineering implication:** A surgical targeting task of this difficulty demands about 5.3 bits of motor information — feasible for a trained surgeon, but a *re-designed instrument* with $W = 20$ mm would lower ID to $\log_2(20) = 4.322$ bits, reducing movement time by approximately $\Delta MT = b \cdot (5.322 - 4.322) = b$ ms.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Derivation | Input | Output | Underlying Principle | Ergonomic Use |
|------------|-------|--------|----------------------|---------------|
| Hartley | $N$ equiprobable | $\log_2 N$ bits | Binary halving | Discrete display sizing |
| Self-info | $p_i$ | $-\log_2 p_i$ bits | Three Shannon axioms | Rare-event salience design |
| Entropy | $\{p_i\}$ | $-\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Expectation of self-info | Workload estimation |
| Channel cap. | $p(x), p(y \mid x)$ | $\max I(X;Y)$ | Optimization | Display resolution limits |
| Hick's law | $n$ choices | $a + b\log_2(n+1)$ | Information & choice RT | Emergency switch design |
| Fitts' law | $A, W$ | $\log_2(2A/W)$ bits | Motor information | Touchscreen, surgical tools |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Shannon–Weaver Communication Model (Adapted for Human Factors)

The original Shannon–Weaver model describes a generic communication system. In ergonomics, we adapt it so the **channel** is a human operator's sensory–cognitive system, and the **destination** is the motor system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Information Source] --> enc[Transmitter / Display Encoder]
    enc --> ch[Channel with Noise]
    ch --> dec[Receiver / Human Sensory System]
    dec --> dst[Destination / Operator Response]
    noise[Noise Source] -.-> ch
    fb[Feedback Loop] -.-> src
```

**Reading guide for the diagram:**

- **Information Source:** the system state the operator must monitor (e.g., aircraft altitude, patient heart rate, industrial pressure).
- **Transmitter/Display Encoder:** translates the physical variable into a human-readable format — a digital readout, an auditory alarm, a haptic vibration.
- **Channel:** the physical medium — visual field, auditory pathway, tactile interface.
- **Noise Source:** visual clutter, auditory masking, vibration, fatigue, stress. Noise *reduces* mutual information $I(X;Y)$.
- **Receiver:** the human eye, ear, or skin — bounded by sensory channel capacity.
- **Destination:** the operator's motor response — turning a dial, pressing a button, issuing a verbal command.
- **Feedback Loop:** the operator observes the *effect* of their action on the system, closing the control loop.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Human Information Processor

The diagram below maps how information flows through a human operator in an industrial task. It integrates the iconic *Wickens Multiple Resource Theory* with Shannon's information-theoretic framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[External Stimulus] --> A2[Sensory Register]
    A2 --> A3[Perceptual Encoding]
    A3 --> A4[Working Memory]
    A4 --> A5[Decision Processor]
    A5 --> A6[Response Selection]
    A6 --> A7[Motor Effector]
    A7 --> A8[Action Output]
    A8 --> A9[Feedback from Environment]
    A9 -.-> A2
    L1[Long-Term Memory Store] -.-> A4
    L1 -.-> A5
    cap1[Channel Capacity Limit 50 bits per sec] -.-> A3
    cap2[Cognitive Bottleneck Miller 7 plus minus 2] -.-> A4
    cap3[Hick-Hyman Choice Limit 4 to 6] -.-> A5
    cap4[Fitts Motor Limit 10 to 12 bits per sec] -.-> A7
```

**Reading guide for the architecture:**

- **Sensory Register** receives raw input — visual, auditory, tactile. Holds information for ~250 ms.
- **Perceptual Encoding** classifies stimuli — this stage loses information through *attentional filtering* and *noise*.
- **Working Memory** is the cognitive bottleneck — limited to Miller's 7±2 *chunks* of information.
- **Decision Processor** is constrained by **Hick's law** — the time to choose among $n$ alternatives grows logarithmically.
- **Response Selection** triggers **Motor Effector** output — bounded by Fitts' law in bits per second.
- **Long-Term Memory** interacts bidirectionally with Working Memory and the Decision Processor, providing *chunking* knowledge that effectively expands short-term capacity.

---

## 4.3 Ergonomic Coding Efficiency Block Diagram

This diagram shows how the *theoretical* information of a display compares with the *information actually perceived* by the operator — a direct visualization of human-factors channel losses.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Source Entropy H of X equals 4 bits per symbol] --> m1[Channel Encoding Display Design]
    m1 --> ch1[Human Visual Channel]
    ch1 --> m2[Operator Perception]
    m2 --> s2[Received Information I of X semicolon Y]
    loss[Loss Factors 1 Noise 2 Fatigue 3 Coding Error] -.-> ch1
    loss -.-> m2
    eff[Efficiency eta equals I over H] --> s2
```

**Quantitative interpretation:**

- Suppose a properly designed display should carry 4 bits/symbol.
- Due to glare (noise), operator fatigue, and poor colour coding, only 2.7 bits/symbol actually reach conscious perception.
- Coding efficiency: $\eta = 2.7 / 4.0 = 0.675$ or **67.5 %**.
- The ergonomic redesign goal is to push $\eta \to 1$ by *reducing* the loss factors.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define information in the context of ergonomics. How is it distinguished from data and knowledge?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- (1 mark) **Information** is the quantifiable reduction of uncertainty when a message is received; mathematically, $I = -\log_2 p(x)$ bits for an event of probability $p$.
- (1 mark) **Data** are unprocessed raw facts (e.g., a voltage value of 4.7 V). **Information** is data that has been processed into a meaningful, decision-relevant form (e.g., "battery at 78% — above critical threshold").
- (1 mark) **Knowledge** is information integrated with experience and context, allowing prediction and generalization. In ergonomic systems, displays provide *information*; the operator's training provides *knowledge*; raw sensor values are *data*.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define Shannon's entropy. State its two most important properties used in ergonomic display design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- (1.5 marks) **Shannon's entropy** of a discrete source $X$ with probability distribution $\{p_i\}$ is

$$H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i \text{ bits/symbol}.$$

It represents the **average information** conveyed per symbol emitted by the source.

- (0.75 mark) **Property 1 — Non-negativity and boundedness:** $0 \leq H(X) \leq \log_2 n$. The upper bound is achieved when all symbols are equiprobable.
- (0.75 mark) **Property 2 — Concavity:** $H$ is a concave function of the probability distribution. Ergonomically, this means **uncertainty (and hence operator workload) is maximum when all outcomes are equally likely** — a uniform alarm distribution is the *worst-case* workload scenario.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) regulations, Part B questions of 14 marks carry internal choice. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part is for 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model]`

**Derive Shannon's expression for entropy of a discrete memoryless source from first principles. Hence, compute the entropy of a 4-symbol source with probability distribution $\{0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.125\}$. Discuss the ergonomic significance of the result.**

#### (a) Derivation of Shannon's Entropy (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — State the three axioms of self-information.**
1. $I(p)$ is a continuous, monotonically decreasing function of $p \in (0, 1]$.
2. $I(1) = 0$ (a certain event conveys no information).
3. $I(p_1 p_2) = I(p_1) + I(p_2)$ for two independent events.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Solve for $I(p)$.**
Try $I(p) = c \log_b(p)$. Apply Axiom 2: $I(1) = c \log_b(1) = 0$ ✓. Apply Axiom 3:

$$I(p_1 p_2) = c \log_b(p_1 p_2) = c[\log_b(p_1) + \log_b(p_2)] = I(p_1) + I(p_2) \;\;\checkmark$$

Apply Axiom 1 (monotonic decrease): requires $c < 0$. Choose $b = 2$, $c = -1$:

$$I(p) = -\log_2(p) \text{ bits.}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Define entropy as expected information.**

$$H(X) = E[I(X)] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot I(x_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \cdot (-\log_2 p_i) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i.$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Verify the equiprobable case.**

$$H = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{n} \log_2 \frac{1}{n} = \log_2 n,$$

which is Hartley's classical result.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — State key properties.**

- $H \geq 0$, with equality iff the source is deterministic.
- $H \leq \log_2 n$ (upper bound at equiprobable distribution).
- $H$ is concave in $\{p_i\}$.

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Conclude with a one-line summary.**
"Shannon's entropy is the unique additive, non-negative measure of average information per symbol for a discrete source, satisfying the three classical axioms of self-information."

#### (b) Numerical Computation and Ergonomic Discussion (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Substitute values into the entropy formula.**

$$H = -[0.5 \log_2 0.5 + 0.25 \log_2 0.25 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125 + 0.125 \log_2 0.125]$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Evaluate each term.**

| Term | Value of $p_i$ | $\log_2 p_i$ | $p_i \log_2 p_i$ |
|------|----------------|---------------|--------------------|
| 1 | 0.5 | $-1.000$ | $-0.500$ |
| 2 | 0.25 | $-2.000$ | $-0.500$ |
| 3 | 0.125 | $-3.000$ | $-0.375$ |
| 4 | 0.125 | $-3.000$ | $-0.375$ |

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Sum and apply the negative sign.**

$$H = -[-0.500 - 0.500 - 0.375 - 0.375] = 1.750 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Compare with maximum entropy.**

$$H_{\max} = \log_2 4 = 2.000 \text{ bits/symbol}$$

Coding efficiency: $\eta = 1.75/2.00 = 0.875 = 87.5\%$.

**Step 5 (2 marks) — Ergonomic significance.**

- (1 mark) The display conveys 1.75 bits per symbol. An operator with a perceptual channel capacity of, say, 2.5 bits/symbol can comfortably process this — there is a 0.75-bit *reserve* capacity, useful for error detection and recovery.
- (1 mark) The skewed distribution ($p_1 = 0.5$) means the most-frequent symbol carries the *least* information (1 bit), while the rare symbols carry up to 3 bits. **Ergonomic implication:** frequent symbols can use simple, easily-decoded codes (basic shapes, common colours); rare but critical symbols should be coded with high salience (flashing red, auditory alarm) to match their high information content.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Model]`

**Explain Hartley's classical measure of information. A digital control panel has 8 identical-looking push-buttons, each equally likely to be the correct one. Calculate the information gained when the operator identifies the correct button. If, after a redesign, the buttons are uniquely colour-coded so the operator directly sees the correct one, calculate the new information. Discuss the ergonomic implications.**

#### (a) Hartley's Measure & Initial Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Definition and derivation of Hartley's measure.**

Hartley's measure of information for a source with $N$ equally likely alternatives is:

$$I = \log_2 N \text{ bits.}$$

**Derivation sketch:** Identifying one of $N$ equiprobable alternatives requires $\log_2 N$ binary (yes/no) questions, each yielding 1 bit. Hence $I = \log_2 N$.

**Step 2 (0.5 mark) — Identify $N$.**
For the original panel: $N = 8$ equally likely buttons.

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Calculate the original information.**

$$I_{\text{orig}} = \log_2 8 = 3 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Convert to time via Hick's law.**
Using $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ with $a = 200$ ms, $b = 150$ ms:

$$T_{\text{orig}} = 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(8 + 1) = 200 + 150 \cdot 3.170 = 200 + 475.5 = 675.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — State the limitation of the design.**
The 8 identical buttons impose maximum cognitive load: the operator must search, compare, and select under uncertainty. The information gain is high *because* the uncertainty is high — but this is *unfavourable* ergonomically because the search time is also high.

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Conclude the first part.**
"The original design conveys 3 bits of information per identification event, but at the cost of 675.5 ms of operator time — a poor information-rate-to-cost ratio."

#### (b) Redesign Calculation and Ergonomic Implications (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — State the redesigned condition.**
After colour coding, the operator can *directly perceive* the correct button. The uncertainty is resolved in a single fixation.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Identify the new $N$.**
The redesign effectively reduces $N$ from 8 to 1 (the correct button is uniquely identifiable):

$$N_{\text{new}} = 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; I_{\text{new}} = \log_2 1 = 0 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Interpret zero information correctly.**
**Critical ergonomic insight:** the *information transferred from the display* is now 0 bits, because the display *uniquely determines* the response — there is no remaining uncertainty. **All the prior uncertainty has been removed by the display design itself.** The information has been "moved" from the operator's cognitive process to the display's perceptual coding.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Compute the new reaction time.**
With a single identifiable target:

$$T_{\text{new}} = 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(1 + 1) = 200 + 150 = 350 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Time savings.**
$\Delta T = 675.5 - 350 = 325.5$ ms saved per identification.

**Step 6 (1 mark) — State the ergonomic principle.**
"**Population stereotypy, colour coding, and shape coding** shift the information burden from the operator's *cognitive* channel to the display's *perceptual* channel. Because the human visual system has higher bandwidth than working memory, the redesign is dramatically more efficient — illustrating Fitts' and Hick's laws in human-factors practice."

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read carefully!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in entropy:** Students often write $\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ *without* the leading minus sign, producing a *negative* entropy. This is the **#1 most common error** in KTU board exams. Always verify: since $\log_2 p_i < 0$ and $p_i > 0$, the negative sign is *mandatory* to yield a non-negative entropy.
>
> 2. **Confusing self-information with entropy:** Self-information $I(x_i) = -\log_2 p_i$ is for a *single* event. Entropy $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ is the *average* over the entire source. A 14-mark question that asks for "the information of a source" *always* expects entropy, not self-information.
>
> 3. **Unit omission:** KTU valuation keys deduct 0.5–1 mark per question if units (**bits**, **bits/symbol**, **bits/sec**) are missing. Make it a habit.
>
> 4. **Misapplying Hick's law:** Hick's law $T = a + b \log_2(n+1)$ uses $n$ = *number of alternatives*, not $n - 1$ and not $n^2$. Substituting incorrectly loses 2 marks instantly.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the "0 bits" paradox in Question B(b):** Many students panic when they calculate $I = 0$ for a uniquely identifiable display. The correct ergonomic interpretation is that *the display has absorbed the uncertainty*, transferring the information burden from cognition to perception. Write this explicitly in the answer.
>
> 6. **Misusing $|$ in LaTeX tables:** When writing conditional entropy $H(X \mid Y)$ inside a table, use $\mid$ or $\vert$, **never the bare pipe character** — it breaks the markdown table layout and the valuation script may misread the answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Summary — Re-read this section the night before the KTU exam.**

- **Information** = reduction of uncertainty; mathematically $I(x) = -\log_2 p(x)$ bits for a single event.
- **Hartley's formula** applies to $N$ **equally likely** alternatives: $I = \log_2 N$ bits.
- **Shannon's entropy** is the average self-information: $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ bits/symbol.
- **Maximum entropy** for $n$ symbols is $\log_2 n$, achieved at equiprobable distribution.
- **Joint entropy** $H(X, Y)$, **conditional entropy** $H(X \mid Y)$, and **mutual information** $I(X; Y) = H(X) - H(X \mid Y)$ are essential for display-channel analysis.
- **Channel capacity** $C = \max I(X; Y)$ is the *theoretical* upper bound on reliable information transfer.
- **Source information rate** $R = r_s \cdot H(X)$ bits/second connects symbol rate to entropy.
- **Hick's law** $T = a + b \log_2(n+1)$ quantifies choice reaction time in seconds; usable for emergency-switch design.
- **Miller's Law**: short-term memory holds $7 \pm 2$ *chunks*, not bits — chunking is an ergonomic multiplier.
- **Fitts' Law** in bit form: $ID = \log_2(2A/W)$ bits — used for touchscreen, surgical-tool, and control-knob design.
- **Coding efficiency** $\eta = I(X;Y)/H(X) \leq 1$; the ergonomic design goal is to push $\eta \to 1$.
- **Units are mandatory:** bits, bits/symbol, bits/sec — write them every time.
- **Base conventions:** base 2 → bits; base $e$ → nats (1 bit = 0.6931 nats); base 10 → Hartleys (1 Hartley = 3.3219 bits).
- **Information cannot be negative.** If your computation yields a negative number, re-check the negative sign in $-\log_2 p_i$.
- **Independence vs. equiprobability** — two different concepts. Equiprobable: $p_i = 1/n$. Independent: $p(x, y) = p(x) p(y)$.
- **Redundancy** in a source is $1 - H(X)/H_{\max}$ — high redundancy helps error detection in noisy display environments.
- **Ergonomic decision rule:** when in doubt, reduce the *number of equiprobable choices* facing the operator — this simultaneously lowers entropy, Hick's law reaction time, and cognitive workload.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information processing in human
topics.content:
