> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBIET604 - APPLIED ERGONOMICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Information processing in human ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Information Processing in Humans** is the cognitive and neurophysiological sequence by which sensory stimuli from the environment are **received, encoded, stored, retrieved, transformed, and translated into motor responses**. It is a foundational concept in **Human Factors Engineering** and **Cognitive Ergonomics**, describing the human operator as a limited-capacity information channel with measurable input-output characteristics. In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, this topic is studied under **Module 2: Human Information Processing and Cognition**, where the human is modelled as a **closed-loop information system** analogous (but not identical) to a digital computer. The model assumes that information flows through a series of discrete and parallel stages: **Sensory Input → Perception → Cognition/Decision → Selection of Response → Motor Output → Feedback**. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Air Traffic Controller:** Imagine an air traffic controller (ATC) sitting in a dark tower at 2 AM. A blip appears on the radar (stimulus). The eyes capture it, the brain identifies it as an aircraft, retrieves its flight number from memory, calculates separation with nearby aircraft, decides on a heading instruction, and speaks the command into the microphone. The pilot acknowledges. Between stimulus and response, the ATC acted as a **biological information processor**. Every human workplace action — typing, driving, reading a gauge, pressing a button — is a miniature version of this same processing loop. ### 1.3 The Three Core Pillars | Pillar | Role in Information Processing | Key Metric | |---|---|---| | **Sensory Register** | Brief storage of raw stimulus data (≤ 0.5 s for visual, ≤ 4 s for auditory) | Iconic / Echoic memory duration | | **Working Memory (WM)** | Active, limited-capacity processing buffer | **Capacity: 7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller's Law) | | **Long-Term Memory (LTM)** | Permanent, semantically organised storage | Effectively unlimited capacity | ### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Information Processing - **Reaction Time (RT):** Simple visual RT ≈ **200–250 ms**; Choice RT ≈ **350–500 ms**. - **Channel Capacity (Human):** ≈ **2.5 to 7 bits/second** during sustained cognitive work. - **Absolute Threshold (JND):** Smallest detectable change in stimulus magnitude (Weber's Law). - **Stimulus-Response Compatibility (SRC):** Degree of natural mapping between stimulus and response codes. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Module 2 explicitly links human information processing to **workplace design, displays, controls, signal detection, and mental workload estimation** — all exam-relevant areas. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five-Stage Model of Human Information Processing A widely accepted and KTU-aligned model breaks processing into five sequential (and partially overlapping) stages: **Stage 1 — Sensory Reception & Register** - Specialized receptors transduce physical energy into neural signals. - **Visual channel:** rods (scotopic, low light) and cones (photopic, colour) in the retina. - **Auditory channel:** hair cells in the cochlea (basilar membrane frequency mapping). - **Tactile channel:** Meissner, Pacinian, Ruffini, Merkel corpuscles. - Information decays in **~0.25 s (iconic)** or **~3–4 s (echoic)** unless attended to. **Stage 2 — Perceptual Encoding & Pattern Recognition** - Selective attention filters relevant stimuli (Broadbent's Filter Model, Treisman's Attenuation Model). - Features (colour, shape, orientation) are bound into **perceptual objects** via feature integration theory. - **Why it matters:** Poorly designed displays force excessive feature-search time, raising error rates. **Stage 3 — Cognitive Processing (Working Memory & Decision)** - Information is held in WM while compared with LTM-stored schemata. - **Miller's Law:** Short-term capacity = **7 ± 2 chunks**. - Decisions are made via **heuristics, algorithms, or recognition-primed** patterns. - This stage is the **primary bottleneck** in human information processing. **Stage 4 — Response Selection & Programming** - The CNS chooses an effector (hand, voice, foot) and prepares the motor programme. - Subject to **Hick's Law** (decision time) and **Fitts' Law** (movement time). - **Stimulus-Response Compatibility (SRC)** strongly affects efficiency. **Stage 5 — Motor Execution & Feedback** - Effector carries out the action; proprioceptive and visual feedback close the loop. - Feedback delay > **400 ms** noticeably disrupts closed-loop control. ### 2.2 Memory Architecture (Atkinson–Shiffrin Multi-Store Model) | Memory Store | Capacity | Duration | Coding | Role in HFE | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Sensory Register** | Large | < 1 s (visual), ~ 4 s (auditory) | Modality-specific (raw) | Pre-attentive buffering | | **Short-Term / Working Memory** | 7 ± 2 chunks | 15–30 s without rehearsal | Phonological / Visual | Active problem-solving | | **Long-Term Memory** | Effectively unlimited | Minutes to lifetime | Semantic / Procedural | Skill & knowledge base | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Implication:** A poorly labelled control on a CNC panel forces the operator to shift to **controlled (effortful) WM processing** rather than relying on **automatic LTM schemata**, increasing reaction time by 30–60% and error rates by 2–4×. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $RT_{choice} = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | **Hick's Law** (revised for n+1 choices) | RT in seconds, n = number of equiprobable choices | | 2 | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right)$ | **Fitts' Law** (movement time) | MT in seconds, D = distance, W = target width | | 3 | $ID = \log_2\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right)$ | **Index of Difficulty (ID)** in bits | Used in Fitts' Law | | 4 | $C = \frac{ID}{MT}$ | **Throughput (bits/s)** | Performance measure in Fitts' tasks | | 5 | $JND = k \cdot I$ | **Weber's Law** (Just Noticeable Difference) | k = Weber fraction, I = stimulus intensity | | 6 | $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ | **Signal Detection Sensitivity** | d' = sensitivity, H = hits, F = false alarms | | 7 | $SR = \frac{H}{H+M}$ | **Response Sensitivity (Hit rate)** | H = hits, M = misses | | 8 | $C = \frac{Channel_{capacity}}{Time}$ | **Channel capacity** in bits/s | Human ≈ 2.5–7 bits/s sustained | | 9 | $WM = 7 \pm 2$ | **Miller's Magical Number** | Chunks (re-evaluated to ~4 by Cowan) | | 10 | $T_{feedback} \leq 400$ ms | **Acceptable feedback delay** | Above this, operator perceives sluggishness | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX Pipe Rule:** All absolute values in the table above use \vert-style delimiters inside LaTeX rendering to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Do not write `|x|` raw inside any table cell. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Display design:** Hick's Law justifies minimising the number of gauges (≤ 4–5) in critical panels. - **Control layout:** Fitts' Law predicts optimum size and spacing of push-buttons in cockpits and surgical consoles. - **Alarm systems:** Signal Detection Theory guides alarm threshold setting to balance missed detections vs. false alarms. - **Workload estimation:** WM capacity limits inform staffing in process control rooms and air traffic control towers. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Hick's Law (Choice Reaction Time) **Starting Premise:** Each binary information-processing decision is hypothesized to take a **constant, non-zero time** $\Delta t$. When faced with $n$ equiprobable choices, the brain must perform a sequence of binary "splits" until only one choice remains. **Step 1 — Number of binary splits required:** A complete binary decision tree with $n$ leaves has depth (in bits) given by: $$ H = \log_2(n) $$ **Step 2 — Total decision time = depth × time per bit:** If each binary decision consumes a constant time $b$ (the *slope* of the law), the total decision time is: $$ T_{decision} = b \cdot \log_2(n) $$ **Step 3 — Add the constant base latency $a$** (sensory + motor overhead, common to all choices): $$ RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n) $$ **Step 4 — Revised (more accurate) Hick-Hyman Law with $n+1$ correction** (Hyman, 1953) to account for the implicit "no-go" branch: $$ RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1) $$ **Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example:** Aircraft cockpit has 5 warning lights that may illuminate. Compute the choice reaction time if $a = 200$ ms and $b = 150$ ms/bit. $$ \begin{aligned} RT &= 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(5+1) \text{ ms} \\ RT &= 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(6) \text{ ms} \\ \log_2(6) &= \frac{\ln 6}{\ln 2} = \frac{1.7918}{0.6931} \approx 2.585 \\ RT &= 200 + 150 \cdot 2.585 \text{ ms} \\ RT &= 200 + 387.75 \text{ ms} \\ RT &\approx 587.75 \text{ ms} \approx 588 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** Reducing the number of simultaneously active warning lights from 5 to 2 would cut choice RT to: $RT = 200 + 150 \cdot \log_2(3) = 200 + 150 \cdot 1.585 = 437.7$ ms — a **151 ms saving**, which is critical in time-stressed cockpit scenarios. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Fitts' Law (Movement Time) **Starting Premise:** Movement time to a target depends on the **difficulty** of the movement, which scales with the ratio of target distance to target width. **Step 1 — Define Index of Difficulty (ID):** Fitts modelled movement as an information transmission task. The number of bits of information required to hit a target of width $W$ at distance $D$ is: $$ ID = \log_2\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right) \text{ bits} $$ The factor 2 in the numerator arises from the effective movement range, accounting for endpoint variability of approximately $W/2$ on either side of the target centre. **Step 2 — Movement time is linearly related to ID:** Empirically, movement time grows linearly with information load: $$ MT = a + b \cdot ID $$ **Step 3 — Substitute ID:** $$ MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right) $$ **Step 4 — Throughput (Performance Index):** Throughput $TP$ (bits/s) is the slope-independent measure of human motor performance: $$ TP = \frac{ID}{MT} = \frac{1}{b} \quad \text{(ideally)} $$ **Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example:** A surgeon reaches for a scalpel 30 cm away on a tray; the scalpel handle width is 2 cm. With $a = 50$ ms, $b = 100$ ms/bit, compute MT and throughput. $$ \begin{aligned} ID &= \log_2\left(\frac{2 \cdot 30}{2}\right) = \log_2(30) \text{ bits} \\ \log_2(30) &= \frac{\ln 30}{\ln 2} = \frac{3.4012}{0.6931} \approx 4.907 \text{ bits} \\ MT &= 50 + 100 \cdot 4.907 \text{ ms} \\ MT &= 50 + 490.7 \text{ ms} \\ MT &\approx 540.7 \text{ ms} \\ TP &= \frac{4.907}{0.5407} \approx 9.07 \text{ bits/s} \end{aligned} $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation of Signal Detection Theory Metrics **Step 1 — Define the four outcomes of a detection task:** | | Signal Present | Signal Absent | |---|---|---| | **Operator says "Yes"** | Hit (H) | False Alarm (FA) | | **Operator says "No"** | Miss (M) | Correct Rejection (CR) | **Step 2 — Hit Rate and False Alarm Rate:** $$ HR = \frac{H}{H+M} \qquad FAR = \frac{FA}{FA+CR} $$ **Step 3 — Sensitivity Index $d'$:** Using standard normal z-transforms: $$ d' = z(HR) - z(FAR) $$ **Step 4 — Example:** In a quality-control inspection, an operator achieves HR = 0.90 and FAR = 0.10. $$ \begin{aligned} z(0.90) &\approx 1.2816 \\ z(0.10) &\approx -1.2816 \\ d' &= 1.2816 - (-1.2816) \\ d' &= 2.5632 \text{ bits} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Interpretation:** $d' \geq 2.0$ is generally considered a *good* discriminator in industrial inspection; $d' < 1.0$ indicates the operator is barely better than chance. ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Human Information Processing — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[STIMULUS
Light Sound Pressure] --> B[SENSORY RECEPTORS
Rods Cones Hair Cells] B --> C[SENSORY REGISTER
Iconic Echoic] C --> D{ATTENTION
FILTER} D -- Selected --> E[WORKING MEMORY
7 plus minus 2 chunks] D -- Rejected --> F[Lost Decay] E --> G[DECISION MAKING
Hicks Law Stage] G --> H[RESPONSE SELECTION
Fitts Law Stage] H --> I[MOTOR EXECUTION
Muscle Effector] I --> J[FEEDBACK
Proprioceptive Visual] J --> K[LONG TERM MEMORY
Schema Update] K --> E E --> L[LONG TERM MEMORY
Retrieval] L --> G I --> M[OBSERVABLE RESPONSE
Button Push Speech] ``` ### 4.2 Memory Store Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Sensory Input
Visual Auditory Tactile] --> R1[Sensory Register
Capacity Large Duration 0.25 to 4 s] R1 --> F1{Attention
Filter} F1 -- Attended --> STM[Short Term Memory
7 plus minus 2 chunks
15 to 30 s] F1 -- Ignored --> DEC[Decay and Loss] STM --> REH{Rehearsal
and Coding} REH -- Elaborative --> LTM[Long Term Memory
Unlimited
Minutes to Lifetime] LTM -. Retrieval .-> STM STM --> OUT[Motor Output
Response Execution] ``` ### 4.3 Signal Detection Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart LR S[Stimulus
Arrives] --> N[Internal Noise
plus Signal] N --> CR{Criterion
Threshold} CR -- Exceeded --> YES[Operator Says YES] CR -- Not Exceeded --> NO[Operator Says NO] YES --> S1[Signal Present: HIT] YES --> S2[Signal Absent: FALSE ALARM] NO --> S3[Signal Present: MISS] NO --> S4[Signal Absent: CORRECT REJECTION] ``` ### 4.4 Workload and Capacity Bottleneck Map ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT_LAYER["INPUT LAYER"] I1[Visual 10 million bits per s] I2[Auditory 100 thousand bits per s] I3[Tactile 1 thousand bits per s] end subgraph BOTTLENECK["COGNITIVE BOTTLENECK"] WM[Working Memory
Capacity 7 plus minus 2 chunks
Effective Rate 2.5 to 7 bits per s] end subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER["OUTPUT LAYER"] O1[Manual Channel 50 bits per s] O2[Vocal Channel 30 bits per s] end I1 --> WM I2 --> WM I3 --> WM WM --> O1 WM --> O2 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The cognitive bottleneck at the WM stage is the *physical* reason humans cannot drive a car, hold a phone conversation, and read a novel simultaneously. Ergonomic interface design must respect this bottleneck by minimising WM load. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** Define **Hick's Law** and explain its significance in the design of control panels. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Hick's Law states that the time taken to make a choice reaction is a logarithmic function of the number of possible stimulus-response alternatives. Mathematically: $$ RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1) $$ where $n$ is the number of equiprobable choices. **Significance in control panel design:** (1) Reduces operator reaction time and decision load by minimising the number of alternatives presented simultaneously; (2) Justifies grouping related controls into logical sub-clusters rather than exposing all controls at once; (3) Supports menu-driven HMI design where choices are revealed hierarchically. *(1.5 marks for formula, 1.5 marks for design significance)* --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** Differentiate between **Sensory Memory**, **Short-Term Memory**, and **Long-Term Memory** in terms of capacity and duration. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Memory Type | Capacity | Duration | |---|---|---| | Sensory Memory | Large (modality-specific) | 0.25–4 s | | Short-Term / Working Memory | 7 ± 2 chunks (Miller) | 15–30 s (without rehearsal) | | Long-Term Memory | Effectively unlimited | Minutes to lifetime | *(2 marks for tabular differentiation, 1 mark for naming each type correctly with a key feature.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- **Question A. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]`** **(a)** Describe in detail the **five-stage model of human information processing** with a neat block diagram. (7 marks) **(b)** A cockpit warning panel has **8 indicator lamps**, each equally likely to illuminate. Using **Hick's Law**, calculate the choice reaction time if $a = 180$ ms and $b = 160$ ms/bit. Recommend a redesign strategy that reduces the choice reaction time to below 450 ms. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Five-Stage Model (7 marks):** 1. **Sensory Reception (1 mark):** Specialized receptors (rods, cones, hair cells, mechanoreceptors) transduce physical energy (light, sound, pressure) into neural impulses. Information enters the sensory register with a very brief storage duration (iconic memory ≈ 0.25 s; echoic ≈ 3–4 s). 2. **Perceptual Encoding (1.5 marks):** Selective attention filters the incoming stream. Features are bound into perceptual objects (Feature Integration Theory, Treisman). Pattern recognition compares sensory input with stored schemata in LTM. 3. **Cognitive Processing / Decision (1.5 marks):** Held in working memory (capacity 7 ± 2 chunks), compared with LTM, and a decision is reached using heuristics, algorithms, or recognition-primed strategies. This is the **primary bottleneck**. 4. **Response Selection (1.5 marks):** The CNS prepares a motor programme and selects the effector (hand, foot, voice). Subject to **Hick's Law** (decision time) and **Fitts' Law** (movement time). Stimulus-Response Compatibility (SRC) is a critical design factor. 5. **Motor Execution & Feedback (1.5 marks):** The effector carries out the action. Proprioceptive, visual, and auditory feedback close the loop, allowing correction. Feedback delay > 400 ms disrupts closed-loop control. **Block Diagram (built into the text above — student should draw a labelled flowchart with the five blocks: SENSE → PERCEIVE → DECIDE → SELECT → ACT, plus a feedback return arrow.)** --- **(b) Hick's Law Calculation & Redesign (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Apply Hick's Law to 8 indicators:** $$ \begin{aligned} RT &= a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1) \\ RT &= 180 + 160 \cdot \log_2(8+1) \text{ ms} \\ \log_2(9) &= \frac{\ln 9}{\ln 2} = \frac{2.1972}{0.6931} \approx 3.170 \text{ bits} \\ RT &= 180 + 160 \cdot 3.170 \\ RT &= 180 + 507.2 \\ RT &\approx 687.2 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Redesign strategy — solve for maximum $n$ that yields RT ≤ 450 ms:** $$ \begin{aligned} 450 &= 180 + 160 \cdot \log_2(n+1) \\ 270 &= 160 \cdot \log_2(n+1) \\ \log_2(n+1) &= \frac{270}{160} = 1.6875 \\ n+1 &= 2^{1.6875} \approx 3.218 \\ n &\approx 2.218 \end{aligned} $$ So $n$ must be reduced to **≤ 2 indicators visible at a time**. **Step 3 — Recommendations (3 marks):** - **Hierarchical grouping:** Cluster the 8 lamps into 2–3 functional sub-groups (e.g., engine, electrical, hydraulic), with a *master summary* lamp that only identifies the affected subsystem. The operator first decodes the subsystem, then drills down. - **Colour-coding and position-coding** to use LTM schemata rather than WM scanning. - **Sequential presentation:** Use a "page" of 2–3 lamps at a time; this reduces effective $n$ from 8 to 2 → $RT \approx 180 + 160 \cdot \log_2(3) = 180 + 253.7 = 433.7$ ms. - **Voice synthesis** to deliver pre-attentive auditory warnings that bypass visual WM scanning. --- **Question B. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`** **(a)** Explain **Fitts' Law** with its mathematical form. An industrial operator must move a 2 cm wide hand-grip across 40 cm to engage a fixture. With $a = 60$ ms and $b = 95$ ms/bit, compute the **Index of Difficulty, Movement Time, and Throughput**. (7 marks) **(b)** Describe the **Atkinson–Shiffrin Multi-Store Model of Memory**. How does an understanding of working memory limitations influence the design of **CNC machine HMI screens** used by operators? (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Fitts' Law Calculation (7 marks):** **Theory (2 marks):** Fitts' Law models aimed human movement as an information transmission task, where movement time $MT$ is linearly proportional to the **Index of Difficulty** $ID$, defined as the logarithm of the ratio of movement distance to target tolerance. $$ MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right) $$ **Calculation (5 marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} ID &= \log_2\left(\frac{2 \cdot 40}{2}\right) = \log_2(40) \text{ bits} \\ \log_2(40) &= \frac{\ln 40}{\ln 2} = \frac{3.6889}{0.6931} \approx 5.322 \text{ bits} \\ MT &= 60 + 95 \cdot 5.322 \text{ ms} \\ MT &= 60 + 505.6 \\ MT &\approx 565.6 \text{ ms} \\ TP &= \frac{ID}{MT} = \frac{5.322}{0.5656} \approx 9.41 \text{ bits/s} \end{aligned} $$ **Redesign insight (extra credit):** Doubling the grip width from 2 cm to 4 cm reduces ID to $\log_2(20) \approx 4.322$ bits, lowering MT to $60 + 95 \cdot 4.322 = 470.6$ ms — a **17% reduction in movement time**, with no extra effort from the operator. --- **(b) Multi-Store Memory & CNC HMI Design (7 marks):** **Atkinson–Shiffrin Model (3.5 marks):** - **Sensory Register:** Brief (≤ 0.5 s visual, ~ 4 s auditory), modality-specific storage of raw stimuli. - **Short-Term Store (STS):** Limited capacity (**7 ± 2 chunks** by Miller; updated to ~ 4 chunks by Cowan), duration 15–30 s without rehearsal, acoustic and visual coding. - **Long-Term Store (LTS):** Unlimited capacity, semantic and procedural coding, duration minutes to a lifetime. - Information transfers from STS to LTS through **rehearsal and elaborative coding**. **CNC HMI Design Implications (3.5 marks):** - **Chunking of numerical data:** Show tool offsets and feed rates as preset blocks (e.g., "TOOL-07: 1200 rpm, 250 mm/min") rather than raw streams; this exploits chunking to fit within WM capacity. - **Hierarchical menus:** Use ≤ 5–7 top-level menu items (Miller's limit) and progressive disclosure of sub-options. - **Consistent spatial coding:** Keep the layout fixed across screens so operators develop **automatic LTM schemata**, offloading WM. - **Status icons instead of text:** Icons are recognised as integrated perceptual chunks faster than text strings. - **Minimize concurrent parameters:** Display only 3–4 critical parameters at a time to keep WM load within Cowan's updated 4-chunk limit, reducing mental workload and error rate. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Information Processing Questions:** > 1. **Writing `|x|` inside markdown tables** — this *breaks* the table parser. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in LaTeX, and `abs(x)` in plain text. *(–0 marks if table collapses.)* > 2. **Confusing Hick's Law with Fitts' Law.** Hick's Law deals with *choice/decision time* (number of alternatives); Fitts' Law deals with *movement time* (distance to target width). Examiners award 0 marks for the wrong law. > 3. **Forgetting the "+1" in the modern Hick-Hyman equation** $\log_2(n+1)$. Older textbooks use $\log_2(n)$; the $n+1$ form is the empirically correct one and is expected at KTU 2024 level. > 4. **Mixing up $d'$, HR, and FAR** in signal detection theory. $d'$ is *sensitivity*; HR and FAR are *rates* (0 to 1). Z-transforms are mandatory. > 5. **Skipping the unit conversion in Fitts' Law calculations.** Distance and width must be in *the same units*, and the result of $\log_2$ is in *bits*, not dimensionless. > 6. **Ignoring the feedback loop in the five-stage diagram.** Marks are awarded for explicitly drawing the *return arrow* from motor output back to perception — many students miss this. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - 🧠 **Human as Information Channel:** Inputs → Sensory Register → WM → Decision → Motor Output → Feedback. - ⏱️ **Hick's Law:** $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ — **fewer choices = faster decisions.** - 🎯 **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(2D/W)$ — **bigger targets, shorter distances = faster movements.** - 🧮 **Memory:** Sensory (large/short) → STM ($7 \pm 2$ chunks / 15–30 s) → LTM (unlimited / lifelong). - 📡 **Signal Detection:** $d' = z(HR) - z(FAR)$; high $d'$ = good discriminator. - ⚠️ **Bottleneck:** Working memory capacity is the **rate-limiting** step in all human information processing. - 🛠️ **Engineering Applications:** Display design, control layout, alarm thresholds, HMI screens, CNC consoles, cockpit panels, surgical tools. - 🔁 **Feedback delay ≤ 400 ms** is the upper limit for acceptable closed-loop control. - 📊 **Throughput** $TP = ID/MT$ is the *bias-free* performance measure in Fitts' tasks. - 🎓 **KTU Hot Topics:** Hick's Law in alarm systems, Fitts' Law in control design, WM limits in HMI design, signal detection in quality control.

topics.title: Information processing model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET604 - APPLIED ERGONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Information processing model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Information Processing Model (IPM)** — A cognitive ergonomics framework that describes human cognition as a sequence of discrete, sequential stages through which sensory information is received, encoded, stored, retrieved, and used to select and execute a motor response. The model treats the human operator analogously to a biological **information channel** with limited **bandwidth**, finite **storage capacity**, and bounded **processing speed**.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context (PBIET604 – Applied Ergonomics), the **Information Processing Model** is presented as the foundational cognitive architecture underpinning **human-machine interaction design**, **workload analysis**, **display-control compatibility**, and **error reduction engineering**.

The canonical stages are:

$$\text{Stimulus} \rightarrow \text{Receptor} \rightarrow \text{Sensory Register} \rightarrow \text{Perceptual Encoding} \rightarrow \text{Working Memory (STM)} \rightarrow \text{Long-Term Memory (LTM)} \rightarrow \text{Decision/Response Selection} \rightarrow \text{Motor Output} \rightarrow \text{Feedback}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Computer Help Desk**
>
> Imagine a human operator working like a small office computer system:
>
> | Brain Part | Computer Analogy | Real-world example |
> |---|---|---|
> | Eyes / Ears / Skin | **Input devices** (keyboard, mouse, camera) | Radar signal hits pilot's eye |
> | Sensory Register | **Buffer / Cache** (raw incoming data) | All visual data momentarily held |
> | Attention Filter | **Firewall / Router** | Pilots focus on altitude dial, ignore sunlight |
> | Short-Term Memory (Working Memory) | **RAM** — volatile, limited | "Hold the number 7592 in mind for 20 s" |
> | Long-Term Memory | **Hard Disk** — permanent, huge | Remembering how to ride a bicycle |
> | Decision / Response Selection | **CPU execution unit** | "Pull up on the yoke" |
> | Hands / Voice | **Output device** (printer, speaker) | Pulling back the control stick |
> | Feedback | **Loop-back sensor** | Seeing the horizon tilt as aircraft climbs |

Just like a computer, the human has **bounded throughput** — you cannot process infinite information simultaneously. This bounded throughput is what creates the ergonomic design problem of **attention bottleneck**, **memory overload**, and **reaction delay**.

## 1.3 Why This Model Matters in Ergonomics

Every workplace accident involving **misread gauges**, **forgotten procedures**, or **delayed reactions** can be traced to a breakdown at one of these processing stages. Ergonomic design aims to:

- Reduce **perceptual load** at the sensory register.
- Match **display layouts** to natural attention patterns.
- Provide **chunking aids** to overcome STM limits (**7 ± 2** items per **George Miller, 1956**).
- Supply **retrieval cues** for LTM-dependent tasks.
- Ensure **stimulus-response (S–R) compatibility** for rapid motor output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Highlight for KTU Boards:** The examiner often asks to **"draw and label the information processing model"** (4–6 marks) and to **"explain the role of attention as a selective filter"** (3 marks). Memorize the **six classical stages** and the **three memory stores** with their **duration and capacity** values.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Processing Stages — Detailed Breakdown

### **Stage 1 — Stimulus & Receptor (Sensory Input)**

- A **stimulus** is any physical energy pattern in the environment (light, sound, pressure, temperature, chemical).
- A **receptor** is the transducer that converts physical energy into **neural impulses** (electro-chemical signals).
- Different sensory modalities (vision, audition, touch, vestibular, proprioception) have different **bandwidths**.

> Vision bandwidth ≈ **10⁷ bits/second** (input) but conscious processing ≈ **50 bits/second** — a massive bottleneck!

### **Stage 2 — Sensory Register (Iconic / Echoic / Haptic Memory)**

- **Ultra-short duration storage** lasting **0.25 to 3 seconds**.
- Large capacity, modality-specific:
  - **Iconic memory** (vision): ~250 ms
  - **Echoic memory** (audition): ~2–4 seconds
  - **Haptic memory** (touch): ~2 seconds
- Without **attention**, the sensory trace decays rapidly (Sperling's partial-report experiments).

### **Stage 3 — Perceptual Encoding / Pattern Recognition**

- Information is **encoded** into meaningful symbols (letters, words, shapes, sounds).
- Pattern recognition uses:
  - **Bottom-up processing** (data-driven)
  - **Top-down processing** (knowledge-driven, expectation-based)
- **Schemas** stored in LTM are matched against incoming patterns.
- The **Feature Integration Theory** (Treisman, 1980) explains focused attention.

### **Stage 4 — Working Memory / Short-Term Memory (STM)**

- The **active workspace** of cognition.
- **Capacity:** **7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller's Law, 1956).
- **Duration:** **15–30 seconds** without rehearsal.
- **Rehearsal** (maintenance rehearsal) keeps information active.
- **Chunking** expands effective capacity (e.g., 75921034 → 7592-1034).
- **Phonological loop** (verbal) and **visuospatial sketchpad** (spatial) are sub-components (Baddeley's model).

### **Stage 5 — Long-Term Memory (LTM)**

- **Capacity:** Effectively **unlimited**.
- **Duration:** **Minutes to a lifetime.**
- Stores: **declarative (semantic + episodic)** and **procedural (skills)**.
- Retrieval requires **encoding specificity** — cues present at encoding must be present at retrieval.
- **Recognition** (with cues) is faster and more accurate than **recall** (without cues).

### **Stage 6 — Decision Making & Response Selection**

- Compares perceived state to desired state (from LTM / goals).
- Selects an appropriate motor program.
- Affected by **Hick's Law** — choice reaction time increases logarithmically with number of alternatives.
- The **decision stage** is the most error-prone under **high workload** or **stress**.

### **Stage 7 — Motor Output (Response Execution)**

- Activation of **muscles** via the motor cortex, cerebellum, and spinal cord.
- **Open-loop** (fast, ballistic, pre-programmed) vs **closed-loop** (slow, feedback-driven) control.
- Affected by **Fitts' Law** — movement time depends on distance and target size.

### **Stage 8 — Feedback (Knowledge of Results — KR)**

- **Intrinsic feedback** (from proprioception, vision, hearing).
- **Extrinsic feedback** (from displays, gauges, spoken responses).
- The **closed loop** — output is monitored, compared to goal, and corrections are issued.
- Without feedback, **learning and error correction** are impossible.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Law / Model | Formula | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Miller's Law (STM Capacity)** | $C = 7 \pm 2$ chunks | $C$ = chunks held in STM | Chunking of phone numbers, instrument readings |
| 2 | **Hick's Law (Choice Reaction Time)** | $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | $RT$ = reaction time (s); $a, b$ = constants; $n$ = number of equally likely choices | Menu design, alarm prioritization, control layout |
| 3 | **Hick-Hyman Law (alt. form)** | $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2 n$ | same as above, valid for $n \geq 2$ | Button layout in cockpits / control rooms |
| 4 | **Fitts' Law (Movement Time)** | $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2 \left( \dfrac{2D}{W} \right)$ | $MT$ = movement time (s); $D$ = distance to target; $W$ = target width; $a, b$ = constants | Touchscreen button sizing, pointer design |
| 5 | **Index of Difficulty (ID)** | $ID = \log_2 \left( \dfrac{2D}{W} \right)$ | dimensionless (bits) | Quantifying task difficulty for motor tasks |
| 6 | **Signal Detection Theory** | $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ | $d'$ = sensitivity; $z(H), z(F)$ = z-transforms of hits and false alarms | Alarm detection, vigilance tasks |
| 7 | **Card, Moran & Newell (GOMS)** | $T_{\text{task}} = \sum T_{\text{operators}}$ | $T$ = execution time per operator (Goal, Operator, Method, Selection) | Predicting expert task time on interfaces |
| 8 | **Power Law of Practice** | $T_n = T_1 \cdot n^{-\alpha}$ | $T_n$ = time on trial $n$; $T_1$ = first trial; $\alpha$ = learning rate (~0.2–0.6) | Training schedule design |
| 9 | **Attentional Bottleneck Capacity** | $C_{\text{useful}} \approx 50 \,\text{bits/s}$ | bits per second consciously processed | Workload estimation |
| 10 | **Sternberg's Memory Scan** | $RT = a + b \cdot m$ | $m$ = memory set size (linear search) | Database search, list matching |

## 2.3 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering

- **Hick's Law** drives the design of **cockpit warning hierarchies** — fewer high-priority alerts = faster response.
- **Fitts' Law** determines **touchscreen target sizes** on mobile devices and ATMs (Apple's iOS guideline ≈ 44 × 44 pt).
- **Signal Detection Theory** is used to engineer **medical imaging workstations** and **air traffic control displays**.
- **GOMS modeling** is used by **Microsoft, IBM, Google** to predict expert performance on new UIs before building them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU 2024 Module 2:** Students must be able to (a) **draw the block diagram** of the information processing model, (b) **explain each block** with its storage characteristics, and (c) **apply Hick's & Fitts' laws** to ergonomic design problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Hick's Law (Choice Reaction Time)

Hick (1952) and Hyman (1953) measured reaction time as a function of the number of stimulus–response alternatives. The empirical data follows a logarithmic relation. The mathematical derivation proceeds as follows:

**Step 1 — Define the information-theoretic premise.**

Each stimulus carries **information content** $I$ measured in **bits** (binary digits). For $n$ equally probable stimuli, by **Shannon's source coding theorem**:

$$
I = \log_2(n) \quad [\text{bits}]
$$

**Step 2 — Assume reaction time is linearly proportional to information processed.**

The human operator processes one bit at a sub-decision level. If processing rate is $b^{-1}$ bits per second and motor execution has fixed overhead $a$:

$$
RT = a + b \cdot I
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the information content.**

$$
RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n)
$$

For zero alternatives ($n = 0$) the simple RT is used; to avoid the log-of-zero singularity, the convention is:

$$
RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n + 1)
$$

**Step 4 — Derive the marginal cost of adding one alternative.**

Differentiate $RT$ with respect to $n$:

$$
\frac{d(RT)}{dn} = \frac{b}{(n+1)\ln 2}
$$

For large $n$, doubling the number of alternatives increases RT by a constant $b$ — the **constant marginal cost** property (this is why Hick's Law is **information-theoretic**, not linear).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Hick's Law

> **Problem:** A cockpit has 4 warning lights, all equally likely. After ergonomic redesign, only 2 remain in the same priority group. If $a = 0.20$ s and $b = 0.15$ s/bit, calculate the reduction in reaction time.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RT_{\text{old}} &= a + b \cdot \log_2(n_{\text{old}} + 1) \\
&= 0.20 + 0.15 \cdot \log_2(4 + 1) \\
&= 0.20 + 0.15 \cdot \log_2(5)
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute $\log_2(5)$:

$$
\log_2(5) = \frac{\ln 5}{\ln 2} = \frac{1.6094}{0.6931} \approx 2.3219
$$

Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RT_{\text{old}} &= 0.20 + 0.15 \cdot 2.3219 \\
&= 0.20 + 0.3483 \\
&= 0.5483 \,\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now for the redesigned system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RT_{\text{new}} &= 0.20 + 0.15 \cdot \log_2(2 + 1) \\
&= 0.20 + 0.15 \cdot \log_2(3) \\
\log_2(3) &= 1.5850 \\
RT_{\text{new}} &= 0.20 + 0.15 \cdot 1.5850 \\
&= 0.20 + 0.2378 \\
&= 0.4378 \,\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reduction in reaction time:**

$$
\Delta RT = RT_{\text{old}} - RT_{\text{new}} = 0.5483 - 0.4378 = 0.1105 \,\text{s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for Design:** Reducing the number of equiprobable alternatives from 4 to 2 saves the pilot ~110 ms per decision. In a critical phase of flight, this is the difference between a controlled response and a controlled flight into terrain (CFIT).

## 3.3 Derivation of Fitts' Law (Movement Time)

**Step 1 — Define Index of Difficulty (ID).**

Movement difficulty is the ratio of distance to target width, expressed in bits:

$$
ID = \log_2\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right) \quad [\text{bits}]
$$

**Step 2 — Movement time is linearly proportional to ID.**

$$
MT = a + b \cdot ID
$$

Therefore:

$$
MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Throughput (TP) — ergonomic performance metric.**

$$
TP = \frac{ID_{\text{avg}}}{MT_{\text{avg}}} \quad [\text{bits/s}]
$$

Human throughput on pointing tasks typically ranges from **4 to 10 bits/s**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Fitts' Law

> **Problem:** An industrial touchscreen has buttons placed at $D = 80$ mm from the operator's rest position with target width $W = 20$ mm. Given $a = 0.05$ s and $b = 0.10$ s/bit, find (a) the Index of Difficulty, (b) the Movement Time.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Compute ID:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right) \\
&= \log_2\left(\frac{2 \cdot 80}{20}\right) \\
&= \log_2\left(\frac{160}{20}\right) \\
&= \log_2(8) = 3 \,\text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b) — Compute MT:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MT &= a + b \cdot ID \\
&= 0.05 + 0.10 \cdot 3 \\
&= 0.05 + 0.30 = 0.35 \,\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule of Thumb:** Keep $ID \leq 4$ bits for rapid targeting on industrial HMIs.

## 3.5 Sternberg's Memory Scanning — Step Derivation

Sternberg (1966) showed memory search time is **linear** in set size $m$:

$$
RT = a + b \cdot m
$$

**Interpretation:** The subject performs an **exhaustive serial comparison** — even when the match is found, all remaining items are checked. This produces **slope = intercept × 2** as a hallmark of serial self-terminating vs exhaustive search.

For $m = 4$ items with $a = 0.40$ s, $b = 0.05$ s/item:

$$
RT = 0.40 + 0.05 \cdot 4 = 0.60 \,\text{s}
$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Memory Stores

| Property | Sensory Register | Short-Term Memory (STM) | Long-Term Memory (LTM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Capacity** | Very large (raw sensory) | **7 ± 2 chunks** (Miller) | Effectively unlimited |
| **Duration** | 0.25–3 s | **15–30 s** (without rehearsal) | Minutes → lifetime |
| **Format** | Modality-specific (iconic/echoic) | Primarily phonological + visuospatial | Semantic, episodic, procedural |
| **Coding** | Sensory (raw) | Acoustic / visual codes | Meaning-based (semantic) |
| **Retrieval** | Pre-attentive decay | Rehearsal-based | Cue-dependent (encoding specificity) |
| **Ergonomic lever** | Display visibility, contrast, lighting | Chunking, checklists, mnemonics | Training, procedures, manuals |
| **Failure mode** | Masking | Decay / interference | Forgetting / retrieval failure |

## 3.7 Attention Models — Filter Theories

### Broadbent's Filter Model (Early Selection, 1958)

- All sensory input enters the sensory register.
- A **selective filter** (based on physical features — pitch, location, color) selects one channel for full semantic processing.
- The unattended channel is blocked — **early selection**.

### Treisman's Attenuation Model (1960)

- Unattended channels are not blocked but **attenuated** (turned down).
- A highly meaningful word (e.g., your name) in the unattended channel may break through — the **cocktail party effect**.

### Deutsch & Deutsch (Late Selection, 1963)

- All input is semantically processed; selection occurs **after** meaning extraction.
- The filter is response-based, not stimulus-based.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked *"Compare early and late selection theories of attention,"* always cite **Broadbent (early)** vs **Deutsch & Deutsch (late)** and mention **Treisman's attenuation** as a middle ground.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Classical Information Processing Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stimulus Environment] --> B[Sensory Receptor]
    B --> C[Sensory Register]
    C --> D[Attention Filter]
    D --> E[Perceptual Encoding]
    E --> F[Working Memory STM]
    F --> G[Long Term Memory LTM]
    G --> H[Decision Response Selection]
    H --> I[Motor Output]
    I --> J[Feedback Loop]
    J --> A
    F -.retrieval.-> G
    G -.retrieval.-> F
    style A fill:#e3f2fd
    style C fill:#fff9c4
    style F fill:#ffccbc
    style G fill:#c8e6c9
    style H fill:#f8bbd0
```

## 4.2 Three-Store Memory Architecture (Atkinson–Shiffrin, 1968)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputStage [STAGE 1 INPUT]
        STIM[Visual Auditory Tactile Stimulus]
    end
    subgraph Store1 [SENSORY REGISTER]
        ICN[Iconic Vision 250 ms]
        ECH[Echoic Audition 3 s]
        HAP[Haptic Touch 2 s]
    end
    subgraph Store2 [SHORT TERM MEMORY]
        STM[7 plus minus 2 chunks]
        REH[Rehearsal Loop]
        STM <--> REH
    end
    subgraph Store3 [LONG TERM MEMORY]
        SEM[Semantic Memory]
        EPS[Episodic Memory]
        PRO[Procedural Memory]
    end
    subgraph OutputStage [STAGE 2 OUTPUT]
        DEC[Decision Maker]
        MOT[Motor Program]
    end
    STIM --> ICN
    STIM --> ECH
    STIM --> HAP
    ICN --> STM
    ECH --> STM
    HAP --> STM
    STM --> SEM
    STM --> EPS
    STM --> PRO
    SEM --> DEC
    EPS --> DEC
    PRO --> DEC
    DEC --> MOT
    style ICN fill:#fff59d
    style ECH fill:#fff59d
    style HAP fill:#fff59d
    style STM fill:#ffab91
    style SEM fill:#a5d6a7
    style EPS fill:#a5d6a7
    style PRO fill:#a5d6a7
```

## 4.3 Attention Filter Architecture — Broadbent vs Treisman

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EAR [Early Selection Broadbent 1958]
        E1[All Sensory Input] --> E2[Physical Feature Filter]
        E2 --> E3[Selected Channel to STM]
        E2 --> E4[Blocked Unattended Channel]
    end
    subgraph MID [Attenuation Treisman 1960]
        M1[All Sensory Input] --> M2[Attenuator Volume Control]
        M2 --> M3[Strong Channel to STM]
        M2 --> M5[Weak Channel may still break through]
    end
    subgraph LAT [Late Selection Deutsch 1963]
        L1[All Sensory Input] --> L2[Full Semantic Processing]
        L2 --> L3[Response Filter selects action]
    end
    style E1 fill:#bbdefb
    style E2 fill:#ffccbc
    style E3 fill:#c8e6c9

```

## 4.4 Closed-Loop Control with Human Operator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF[Reference Goal from LTM] --> COMP[Comparator]
    OUT[Plant or Vehicle State] --> SENS[Sensors Displays]
    SENS --> PER[Human Perception]
    PER --> DEC[Decision Response Selection]
    DEC --> MOT[Motor Action]
    MOT --> OUT
    OUT -.feedback.-> COMP
    REF -.goal signal.-> COMP
    COMP --> PER
    style COMP fill:#ff7043

    style OUT fill:#ffa726
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read These Diagrams in the Exam:**
> 1. **Identify the boundary of each store** (sensory register / STM / LTM).
> 2. **Trace the flow** from stimulus → response.
> 3. **Annotate durations and capacities** on each box.
> 4. **Show the feedback loop** as a dashed arrow returning to the comparator.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the Information Processing Model. List its main stages.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Information Processing Model** is a cognitive ergonomics framework that describes human information handling as a sequence of encoding, storage, retrieval, decision, and response stages — analogous to a biological computer.

**Main stages (1.5 marks):**
1. **Stimulus & Receptor** — input detection
2. **Sensory Register** — short-lived modality-specific buffer
3. **Perceptual Encoding** — pattern recognition via attention
4. **Working Memory (STM)** — active workspace (7 ± 2 chunks, 15–30 s)
5. **Long-Term Memory (LTM)** — permanent knowledge store
6. **Decision / Response Selection** — choosing an action
7. **Motor Output** — executing the response
8. **Feedback** — closing the loop (1.5 marks for the numbered list and labeling)

---

### **Q2. State Miller's Law. State its significance in interface design.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Miller's Law (1956):** The capacity of short-term memory is **7 ± 2 chunks** of information, where a chunk is a meaningful unit. (1.5 marks)

**Significance (1.5 marks):**
- Phone numbers, IDs, instrument groupings should be designed in **chunks of 3–4 digits** (e.g., 7592-1034).
- Menu hierarchies should not exceed **7 ± 2 items** per level.
- Display panels should be organized into **chunked groups** to avoid STM overflow.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Hick's Law & Fitts' Law in Design**

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** State and derive Hick's Law. A control panel has 8 equally likely alarm indicators. After re-engineering, the most critical 4 are highlighted. Using $a = 0.25$ s, $b = 0.12$ s/bit, calculate the reduction in reaction time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State Fitts' Law. An emergency stop button is located 120 mm from the operator's hand-rest position. Its diameter is 40 mm. With $a = 0.08$ s, $b = 0.11$ s/bit, compute the Index of Difficulty and the predicted Movement Time. Discuss ergonomic implications. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Hick's Law:**

**Statement (1 mark):** Hick's Law states that the choice reaction time $RT$ increases logarithmically with the number of equally probable stimulus–response alternatives $n$:

$$
RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n + 1)
$$

**Derivation (2 marks):**

Each of $n$ alternatives carries information content (in bits):

$$
I = \log_2(n)
$$

Assuming reaction time is linearly proportional to the bits processed:

$$
RT = a + b \cdot I = a + b \cdot \log_2(n)
$$

Using the $n+1$ convention to avoid $\log(0)$ singularity:

$$
RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n + 1)
$$

**Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

For 8 alarms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RT_1 &= 0.25 + 0.12 \cdot \log_2(8 + 1) \\
&= 0.25 + 0.12 \cdot \log_2(9)
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute:

$$
\log_2(9) = \frac{\ln 9}{\ln 2} = \frac{2.1972}{0.6931} \approx 3.1699
$$

$$
RT_1 = 0.25 + 0.12 \cdot 3.1699 = 0.25 + 0.3804 = 0.6304 \,\text{s}
$$

For 4 highlighted alarms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RT_2 &= 0.25 + 0.12 \cdot \log_2(4 + 1) \\
&= 0.25 + 0.12 \cdot \log_2(5) \\
\log_2(5) &\approx 2.3219 \\
RT_2 &= 0.25 + 0.12 \cdot 2.3219 = 0.25 + 0.2786 = 0.5286 \,\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reduction:**

$$
\Delta RT = RT_1 - RT_2 = 0.6304 - 0.5286 = 0.1018 \,\text{s} \approx 102 \,\text{ms}
$$

**(b) Fitts' Law:**

**Statement (1 mark):** Fitts' Law relates the time to move to a target as a logarithmic function of the ratio of distance to target size:

$$
MT = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{2D}{W}\right)
$$

The term $ID = \log_2(2D/W)$ is the **Index of Difficulty** in bits.

**Calculation (3 marks):**

Given $D = 120$ mm, $W = 40$ mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
ID &= \log_2\left(\frac{2 \cdot 120}{40}\right) = \log_2\left(\frac{240}{40}\right) = \log_2(6) \\
\log_2(6) &= \frac{\ln 6}{\ln 2} = \frac{1.7918}{0.6931} \approx 2.585 \,\text{bits} \\
MT &= 0.08 + 0.11 \cdot 2.585 = 0.08 + 0.2844 = 0.3644 \,\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ergonomic Implications (3 marks):**
- $ID \approx 2.6$ bits is well within the comfortable range (≤ 4 bits).
- The button is **appropriately sized** for rapid emergency activation.
- To further reduce MT, either **decrease $D$** (move button closer) or **increase $W$** (make button larger / add a guard ring).
- For life-critical controls, target size should be at least **double** the predicted minimum to account for **stress, glove use, and vibration**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Memory Stores & Attention**

`[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Compare Sensory Register, Short-Term Memory, and Long-Term Memory with respect to capacity, duration, coding, and failure modes. Draw a labeled block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain Broadbent's Filter Theory and Treisman's Attenuation Model of attention. How does each influence ergonomic design of warning systems? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Memory Store Comparison (7 marks):**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Property | Sensory Register | Short-Term Memory | Long-Term Memory |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capacity | Very large (raw) | **7 ± 2 chunks** | Unlimited |
| Duration | **0.25–3 s** | **15–30 s** | Minutes → lifetime |
| Coding | Sensory | Phonological + Visuospatial | Semantic |
| Failure | Masking, decay | Decay, interference, displacement | Retrieval failure, interference |

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** Show arrows from sensory register → STM → LTM with rehearsal loop and retrieval arrows (use the Atkinson–Shiffrin diagram from Section 4.2).

**(b) Attention Theories (7 marks):**

**Broadbent's Filter Theory — Early Selection (3 marks):**
- Proposed by **Donald Broadbent (1958)**.
- All sensory input enters a sensory buffer.
- A **selective filter** based on **physical features** (pitch, location, color, ear of arrival) selects **one channel** for full semantic processing.
- The unattended channel is **completely blocked**.
- Limitations: cannot explain the **cocktail party effect** (your name breaking through).

**Treisman's Attenuation Model (1960) (2 marks):**
- Unattended channels are not blocked but **turned down (attenuated)**.
- Highly salient stimuli (e.g., your own name, a sudden loud sound) may still exceed the threshold and reach semantic processing.
- Solves Broadbent's cocktail-party problem.

**Ergonomic Implications for Warning Systems (2 marks):**
- Use **distinct physical features** (different pitches, colors, locations) — exploits **Broadbent's filter**.
- Use **highly meaningful semantic labels** (e.g., "FIRE", "COLLISION") — exploits **Treisman's breakthrough** for unattended channels.
- Combine **both**: physical distinctiveness + semantic salience for maximum detection reliability.

---

## KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $n+1$ convention in Hick's Law** — using $\log_2(n)$ when $n=0$ produces $-\infty$. Always justify the +1.
> 2. **Mixing up memory durations** — Sensory Register is **0.25–3 s**, STM is **15–30 s**, LTM is **minutes to lifetime**. Examiners tabulate the wrong values.
> 3. **Confusing Early vs Late Selection** — Broadbent = early (physical feature filter); Deutsch & Deutsch = late (semantic filter). Treisman = middle (attenuation).
> 4. **Not showing feedback loop** in the IPM block diagram — a one-way-only diagram is incomplete and loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Wrong units in Fitts' Law** — $ID$ is in **bits** (dimensionless log ratio). Movement time is in **seconds**.
> 6. **Missing the design implications** — Examiners award marks for **"what does this mean for design?"** Don't just compute; **interpret**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Information Processing Model:**

- **Eight classical stages:** Stimulus → Receptor → Sensory Register → Perception → STM → LTM → Decision → Motor Output → Feedback.
- **Three memory stores** — Sensory (0.25–3 s, large), STM (**7 ± 2 chunks, 15–30 s**), LTM (unlimited, lifetime).
- **Miller's Law (1956):** STM capacity = **7 ± 2 chunks**; use **chunking** in interface design.
- **Hick's Law:** $RT = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ — reduce alternatives for faster reaction.
- **Fitts' Law:** $MT = a + b \cdot \log_2(2D/W)$ — increase target size, decrease distance for faster movement.
- **Sternberg's Memory Scan:** $RT = a + b \cdot m$ — linear search time.
- **Signal Detection Theory:** $d' = z(H) - z(F)$ — sensitivity index for vigilance tasks.
- **Attention Theories:**
  - **Broadbent (1958)** — Early selection, physical-feature filter.
  - **Treisman (1960)** — Attenuation model.
  - **Deutsch & Deutsch (1963)** — Late selection, semantic filter.
- **GOMS Model** — Goal, Operators, Methods, Selection rules; predicts expert task time.
- **Power Law of Practice** — $T_n = T_1 \cdot n^{-\alpha}$; learning reduces time predictably.
- **Coding types:** Sensory (raw), STM (acoustic/visual), LTM (semantic).
- **Retrieval types:** Recognition (easier, cue-aided) vs Recall (harder, self-initiated).
- **Design levers per stage:** Visibility/contrast (sensory), chunking/checklists (STM), training/manuals (LTM), S–R compatibility (motor).
- **Feedback is mandatory** — without KR (Knowledge of Results), no learning or error correction.
- **Workload bottleneck:** ~50 bits/s conscious throughput — design must respect this ceiling.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Information Processing Model**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of visual, auditory, tactual and olfactory displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBIET604 - APPLIED ERGONOMICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Design of visual, auditory, tactual and olfactory displays ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Display (Human-Machine Interface):** A *display* is any **man-made device or arrangement of components** that presents information to one or more of the human operator's sensory channels (visual, auditory, tactual/haptic, or olfactory), thereby supporting **perception, cognition, and motor response** within a man-machine system (Chapanis, 1996; Sanders & McCormick, 1993). In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (per the PBIET604 syllabus), display design is the sub-discipline of **Human Factors Engineering (HFE)** that engineers the **information transfer** from machine to operator. The four canonical modalities taught in Module 2 are: | Modality | Sense Engaged | Primary Application | |---|---|---| | **Visual** | Sight | Dials, gauges, screens, indicator lights | | **Auditory** | Hearing | Alarms, buzzers, speech warnings | | **Tactual** | Touch / Skin | Braille, vibration alerts, force feedback | | **Olfactory** | Smell | Hazard odour warnings, fire cues | ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English View) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Five Senses of a Car Dashboard":** Imagine a car's dashboard. The **speedometer** (visual dial) tells you how fast you are going. The **seatbelt buzzer** (auditory) screams when you forget to buckle up. The **steering wheel vibration** (tactual) warns you on a slippery road. A faint **fuel-smell cue** (olfactory) tells you something is leaking. Each one is a *display* — a translator between the *car* (machine) and the *driver* (human). Good ergonomic design means choosing the *right sense* for the *right job*. ### 1.3 Why Four Modalities? — The Concept of Display Redundancy A single critical warning (e.g., fire alarm) should **never** rely on only one sense. This is the **principle of display redundancy**: - A pilot in a noisy cockpit may **miss** an auditory alarm → use a flashing red light **and** a vibration in the control stick. - A deaf operator in a factory **cannot hear** alarms → use bright strobes **or** tactile pagers. - The design goal is to *match* the display's channel to the operator's environment, urgency, and the *detectability* of the signal. ### 1.4 The Human–Display Information Loop Displays are only **one half** of the human-machine dialogue. The full loop is: **Machine State → Display → Human Sensory Receptors → Brain (Perception + Cognition) → Decision → Motor Action → Control → Machine State** The **ergonomist's job** is to ensure that the *Display* is shaped so that step 2 → 3 (perception) is **fast, accurate, and low-effort**. ### 1.5 The "Optimum Display" Decision Heuristic > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Quick Rule (McCormick's Law):** *"If a display can be presented visually, it usually should be — unless one of the following is true:"* > 1. The operator's **eyes are already busy** (e.g., flying, driving). > 2. The signal must be **perceived in the dark** or **all around the body**. > 3. The message must **cut through noise** that blocks sight. > 4. The information is **spatial/temporal** in nature (e.g., a vibration pulse). > 5. The **urgency** is extreme and auditory is faster than visual. ]<]minimax[>[ ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 VISUAL DISPLAYS — Detailed Design Framework Visual displays dominate industrial, aviation, and consumer interfaces. KTU Module 2 groups them into **four categories**: #### A. Quantitative Displays (Reading a Number) - **Fixed-scale moving-pointer** (analog dial) — best when *trend* matters. - **Moving-scale fixed-pointer** — best when *absolute value* matters. - **Counter / digital readout** — best when *precise number* matters. #### B. Qualitative Displays - Check lights, status icons, GO/NO-GO flags. #### C. Representational / Pictorial Displays - Aircraft attitude indicator, car fuel gauge with a pump icon. #### D. Alphanumeric / Symbolic Displays - LCD/LED text panels, computer monitors. ### 2.2 Critical Design Variables for Visual Displays 1. **Visibility** (can the operator *see* the display?). 2. **Legibility** (can the operator *read* the character?). 3. **Comprehension** (does the operator *understand* the meaning?). #### 2.2.1 Character Height vs Viewing Distance > [!IMPORTANT] > For a person with **20/20 vision (Snellen acuity = 1.0 minute of arc)**, the minimum character height $H$ for legibility is governed by: $$H = \dfrac{D}{200}\ \text{(mm)}$$ where $D$ is the viewing distance in millimetres. For *preferred* (comfortable) reading, use: $$H = \dfrac{D}{300}\ \text{(mm)}$$ #### 2.2.2 Reading Time (Hick's-Type Empirical Law) The time $T$ (seconds) to read a value from a quantitative display depends on the number of alternatives $N$: $$T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(N)$$ - $a \approx 0.5$ s (perceptual fixation time). - $b \approx 0.1$ s per bit of information (Hick-Hyman constant). #### 2.2.3 Contrast Ratio (CR) $$CR = \dfrac{L_{\max}}{L_{\min}}$$ where $L_{\max}$ and $L_{\min}$ are the luminances of the brightest and darkest portions. KTU's minimum recommendation for LCD panels: **$CR \geq 7:1$** for office work and **$CR \geq 3:1$** for general viewing. #### 2.2.4 Visual Angle $$\theta = 2\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{H}{2D}\right) \approx \dfrac{H}{D}\ \text{(radians, for small angles)}$$ Snellen 20/20 corresponds to $\theta = 1'$ of arc (one minute). #### 2.2.5 Color-Coding Guidelines (KTU Standard) | Color | Meaning (ISO 3864 / ANSI Z535) | |---|---| | **Red** | Danger, stop, fire | | **Yellow / Amber** | Caution, warning | | **Green** | Safety, normal, go | | **Blue** | Mandatory action, information | | **Orange** | Warning of dangerous parts | > [!WARNING] > Do not use **red + green** in the same field for color-blind operators (≈ 8% of males are red-green deficient). Use **shape + color** redundancy. ### 2.3 AUDITORY DISPLAYS — Detailed Design Framework Auditory displays are mandatory when: - The operator's **visual channel is saturated**. - The message must be **omnidirectional** (heard from any orientation). - The signal is a **warning/alarm**. #### 2.3.1 Properties of Sound Sound is characterized by three primary attributes: 1. **Frequency** $f$ (Hz) — *pitch*. Human range: **20 Hz – 20 kHz**. Most industrial alarms use **500 Hz – 3000 Hz** (where the ear is most sensitive). 2. **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** $L$ (dB) — *loudness*. 3. **Duration & Pattern** — pulsed vs continuous, warble, siren. $$L = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_{0}}\right)\ \text{dB}$$ where $p$ is the RMS sound pressure and $p_{0} = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ (threshold of hearing). #### 2.3.2 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) Rule > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Mandatory Rule:** An auditory warning signal must be at least **15 dB(A) above the ambient noise level** to be reliably detected, but not so loud as to cause **startle** or **hearing damage** (max 110 dB(A) for short exposure per OSHA). $$L_{\text{signal}} \geq L_{\text{noise}} + 15\ \text{dB(A)}$$ #### 2.3.3 Stevens' Power Law for Loudness The perceived loudness $\psi$ is related to physical intensity $I$ by: $$\psi = k \cdot I^{\beta}$$ with $\beta \approx 0.67$ for a 1 kHz tone (Stevens, 1957). A **10 dB increase** corresponds roughly to a **doubling of perceived loudness**. #### 2.3.4 Audibility Octave-Band Masking If a pure-tone alarm is masked by ambient noise, the alarm's frequency must lie in an octave band where the **noise level is at least 10 dB lower** than the alarm's level. #### 2.3.5 Types of Auditory Displays | Type | Example | Use | |---|---|---| | **Simple warning** | Buzzer, horn | Fire alarm, forklift reversing | | **Voice** | Spoken warning | "Pull up, pull up" (TCAS) | | **Tonal / melodic** | Distinctive 3-tone chime | Doorbell, phone ring | | **Sonification** | Data mapped to pitch | Geiger counter ticking | | **Earcon** | Abstract musical motif | Menu click in smartphone | ### 2.4 TACTUAL (HAPTIC) DISPLAYS — Detailed Design Framework The **tactual** (or *haptic*) channel uses the skin's **mechanoreceptors**. Two design categories: #### 2.4.1 Cutaneous (Skin-Surface) Displays - Vibrotactile — small skin indentation vibrating at 100–300 Hz (Pacinian corpuscles). - Electrotactile — small electric current across the skin. - Thermal — temperature change cues. - Braille — static raised dots. #### 2.4.2 Kinesthetic (Body-Movement) Displays - Force feedback on a joystick. - Resistive pedals, steering resistance. #### 2.4.4 Two-Point Discrimination Threshold (Critical Design Parameter) > [!IMPORTANT] > The minimum spacing $\Delta x$ between two tactile stimuli that the skin can resolve: | Body Region | Two-Point Threshold $\Delta x$ | |---|---| | **Fingertip** | **2 – 3 mm** | | Lip | 5 mm | | Palm | 10 – 15 mm | | Forearm | 25 – 30 mm | | Back | 40 – 70 mm | #### 2.4.5 Vibrotactile Frequency Bands - **Low** (0–10 Hz): pressure change, pulsing - **Medium** (10–100 Hz): flutter (Meissner corpuscles) - **High** (100–300 Hz): buzz (Pacinian corpuscles) → *most common for alerts* ### 2.5 OLFACTORY DISPLAYS — Detailed Design Framework Olfactory displays (also called *odour displays* or *digital scent generators*) release controlled chemical volatiles to be sensed by the **olfactory epithelium** (≈ 400 types of receptors, ~ 5 cm² area, located in the upper nasal cavity). #### 2.5.1 Properties of Olfactory Stimuli - **Detection threshold** (e.g., hydrogen sulfide ≈ 0.5 ppb). - **Identification threshold** (typically 10–100× detection threshold). - **Adaptation** — receptors desensitize after 30–60 s of continuous exposure. - **Memory** — smells evoke strong, durable emotional memory (limbic link). #### 2.5.2 Engineering Uses - **Hazard warning**: mercaptan in natural gas. - **Medical**: anosmia screening, MRI olfactory fMRI. - **Marketing**: scent in retail. - **Aviation simulation**: smells in flight simulators (smoke, hydraulic fluid). #### 2.5.3 Design Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > - Olfactory **adaptation** makes sustained release useless. > - Smells diffuse **slowly** → not good for fast-changing signals. > - Cultural variation in scent interpretation (e.g., jasmine in India vs. funeral homes in UK). > - Health concerns (asthma, allergies) from synthetic musks. ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat-Sheet (Master Table) | # | Formula / Rule | Symbol | Unit | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Min. character height | $H = D/200$ | mm | Visual legibility | | 2 | Preferred character height | $H = D/300$ | mm | Visual comfort | | 3 | Visual angle | $\theta \approx H/D$ | rad | Acuity check | | 4 | Reading time | $T = a + b\log_2 N$ | s | Display density | | 5 | Contrast ratio | $CR = L_{\max}/L_{\min}$ | – | Display legibility | | 6 | SPL | $L = 20\log_{10}(p/p_0)$ | dB | Loudness | | 7 | Audibility rule | $L_{\text{sig}} \ge L_{\text{noise}} + 15$ | dB(A) | Alarm design | | 8 | Stevens' law | $\psi = k I^{\beta}$ | sone | Loudness perception | | 9 | Snellen acuity | $1' = 1$ arcmin | – | Vision testing | | 10 | Two-point threshold (fingertip) | $\Delta x = 2$–$3$ | mm | Tactile layout | | 11 | Octave doubling | $f_2 = 2 f_1$ | Hz | Pitch banding | | 12 | Optimal alarm frequency | $500 \le f \le 3000$ | Hz | Audibility | | 13 | Fitts' law | $MT = a + b \log_2(1 + D/W)$ | ms | Pointer control | | 14 | Weber fraction (vibration) | $\Delta I/I \approx 0.10$ | – | Vibrotactile detection | | 15 | $p_0$ (threshold of hearing) | $20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ | Pa | SPL reference | ### 2.7 Engineering Utility — Why This Topic Matters in the Field - **Aviation** (DGCA / ICAO): Glass-cockpit display design, GPWS "Pull up" voice warning. - **Automotive** (ISO 26262, ASIL): Driver-assist warning lights, lane-departure vibration seats. - **Medical devices** (IEC 60601-1-8): Hospital alarm colour, frequency, and volume standards. - **Nuclear control rooms** (NUREG-0700): Mimic panel + annunciator design. - **Smart wearables**: Apple Watch haptic taps, hearing-aid octave alarms. ]<]minimax[>[ ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reading Time $T = a + b\log_2 N$ from First Principles This is the **KTU 2024 most-favourite 14-mark derivation** for visual displays. **Step 1 — Define "Information Content" (Hartley's Law, 1928).** A display that can adopt $N$ equally-likely states carries an information content $H$ of: $$H = \log_{2}(N)\ \text{bits}$$ This is Hartley's logarithmic measure of information. **Text-row explanation:** The more alternatives a display has, the more *bits* of information must be transmitted to the operator. **Step 2 — Assume Linear Time per Bit (Hick's Empirical Postulate).** Hick (1952) experimentally showed that the choice reaction time grows linearly with information. Let the *transmission rate* be $b$ (seconds per bit), and let the *sensory-motor overhead* be $a$ (seconds of fixed perceptual + motor latency). $$T = a + b \cdot H$$ **Step 3 — Substitute Hartley's $H$.** Substitute $H = \log_2 N$ into the equation above: $$T = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(N)$$ This is the **canonical reading-time equation**. **Step 4 — Plug in KTU Reference Values.** | Parameter | Value | Source | |---|---|---| | $a$ | 0.5 s | Fixation + saccade latency | | $b$ | 0.10 s/bit | Hick-Hyman mean | **Step 5 — Sample Numerical Problem (KTU Practice).** > A digital counter shows digits 0–9. Estimate the time to read *one* digit. $$T = 0.5 + 0.10 \cdot \log_{2}(10) = 0.5 + 0.10 \cdot 3.3219 \approx 0.83\ \text{s}$$ **Step 6 — Sample Numerical Problem (Two Digits).** > A two-digit odometer (00–99): $$T = 0.5 + 0.10 \cdot \log_{2}(100) = 0.5 + 0.10 \cdot 6.6439 \approx 1.16\ \text{s}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Insight:** Each additional bit of information adds only **100 ms**, not an additional digit. Hence *quantitative* digital displays are usually faster than *qualitative* lights for any value > 2 alternatives. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Character-Height Requirement from Snellen Acuity **Step 1 — Snellen Definition.** A person with 20/20 vision can resolve a character whose **critical detail subtends 1 arc-minute (1′) at the eye**. $$1' = \dfrac{1}{60}^\circ = \dfrac{\pi}{10800}\ \text{rad} \approx 2.909 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{rad}$$ **Step 2 — Visual Angle Geometry.** A character of height $H$ at viewing distance $D$ subtends an angle $\theta$: $$\theta = 2 \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{H}{2D}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Small-Angle Approximation.** For $H \ll D$, $\arctan(x) \approx x$, so: $$\theta \approx \dfrac{H}{D}\ \text{rad}$$ **Step 4 — Equate to 1 Arc-Minute.** For 20/20 vision: $$\dfrac{H}{D} = 1' = 2.909 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{rad}$$ Solve for $H$ in metres: $$H = 2.909 \times 10^{-4} \cdot D\ \text{(metres)}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to Millimetres.** $$H = 0.2909 \cdot D\ \text{(mm, with }D\text{ in mm)}$$ **Step 6 — Apply KTU's Engineering Safety Factor of 2.** A safety factor of ~ 2 gives the "comfortable" legibility rule: $$H_{\min} = \dfrac{D}{200},\quad H_{\text{preferred}} = \dfrac{D}{300}$$ **Step 7 — Worked Example.** > An instrument panel is **800 mm** from the pilot's eyes. What minimum character height is needed? $$H_{\min} = \dfrac{800}{200} = 4.0\ \text{mm}$$ $$H_{\text{preferred}} = \dfrac{800}{300} \approx 2.67\ \text{mm}$$ The display designer chooses $H = 5$ mm to be safe (rounded up). ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Audibility Masking and SNR Margin **Step 1 — Define SNR.** $$SNR = L_{\text{signal}} - L_{\text{noise}}\ \text{(dB)}$$ **Step 2 — Empirically, the ear's masked threshold in noise.** Fletcher (1940) showed that a tone is just detectable if its SPL is **10 dB** above the noise's SPL in the **critical band** around the tone. **Step 3 — KTU Engineering Padding.** To allow for inattentiveness, age, and PPE (ear plugs), KTU/ISO 7731 mandates a **15 dB SNR** minimum, and **20 dB** for *critical* alarms. $$L_{\text{signal, design}} = L_{\text{noise, octave}} + 15\ \text{dB(A)}$$ **Step 4 — Sample Numerical Problem.** > A factory hall has an octave-band noise level of **78 dB(A)** at 1 kHz. What is the minimum alarm SPL? $$L_{\text{signal, min}} = 78 + 15 = 93\ \text{dB(A)}$$ To meet OSHA limits, the *duration* must be kept under **2 hours** at this level (or use a warble tone to reduce perceived loudness by ~ 5 dB). ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Vibrotactile Point-Source Detection (Weber's Law) **Step 1 — Weber's Law for Vibration on Skin.** The just-noticeable difference (JND) in vibration intensity is: $$\dfrac{\Delta I}{I} = k \approx 0.10\ \text{(Weber fraction for vibrotactile)}$$ **Step 2 — So, if a baseline amplitude is $A$, the JND is:** $$\Delta A = 0.10 \cdot A$$ **Step 3 — Sample Numerical Problem.** > A smartwatch uses a vibration motor of amplitude **0.5 g**. What is the smallest change the user can perceive? $$\Delta A = 0.10 \cdot 0.5\ g = 0.05\ g$$ Hence the *next* vibration level must be at least **0.55 g** to be reliably distinguished. ### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Olfactory Adaptation Time-Constant **Step 1 — Receptor Response Model.** Olfactory receptor firing rate $R(t)$ under a constant odour concentration $C$ follows a first-order decay: $$R(t) = R_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$ **Step 2 — Typical Time Constant.** $$\tau \approx 15\ \text{s (light odours),}\quad 60\text{–}90\ \text{s (strong odours)}$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation.** After $3\tau$ (≈ 45 s light, ≈ 180 s strong), the firing rate has fallen to 5% of $R_0$ — the operator is **adapted** and will not detect the same scent any more. **Step 4 — Design Rule.** > [!IMPORTANT] > For *continuous* hazard monitoring (e.g., refrigerant leak), the system must *pulse* the scent, not hold it constant. Pulse pattern: 2 s ON / 30 s OFF. ### 3.6 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Display to Choose? | Situation | Recommended Modality | Justification | |---|---|---| | Pilot flying in clouds | Visual + Auditory | Eyes free, but must be alerted to TCAS | | Hearing-impaired factory worker | Visual + Tactual | Cannot rely on auditory | | Nighttime nurse with patients | Tactual (pager) | No noise, no light disturbance | | Fire alarm in a public building | Auditory (primary) + Visual (strobe) | Reaches all, including sleeping deaf | | Detecting refrigerant leak | Olfactory (primary) | Mercaptan smell is uniquely identifiable | | Mobile-phone notification | Tactual (vibration) | Discreet, silent, orientation-independent | | Car "lane departure" | Auditory beep + steering vibration | Eyes-on-road, but tactile jolt reinforces | | Surgical robot feedback | Kinesthetic (force) | Surgeon needs to "feel" tissue resistance | | Aircraft engine temperature trend | Visual analog dial | *Trend* reading is faster than a number | ]<]minimax[>[ ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Block Diagram — The Human–Display Information Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Machine Process State] --> B[Transducer / Sensor] B --> C[Signal Conditioning] C --> D{Display Modality} D -->|Visual| E1[Visual Display
