List the four sensory modalities used for man-machine displays.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **display** is a device that converts machine state information into a form perceivable by a human operator, supporting perception, decision, and action (1 mark). The four modalities are: **visual** (light, dials, screens) (½ mark), **auditory** (tones, alarms, voice) (½ mark), **tactual/haptic** (vibration, Braille, force) (½ mark), and **olfactory** (scent cues, odour warnings) (½ mark). #### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PBIET604, Q1(b)]** *State the KTU/ISO rule for the minimum sound-pressure level of an auditory warning relative to ambient noise.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The auditory alarm must be at least **15 dB(A) above the ambient noise** in the same octave band for reliable detection (2 marks), and **20 dB(A) for critical alarms** (1 mark). Reference: ISO 7731 / Sanders & McCormick. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) #### Question A — Design of Visual Displays **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PBIET604, Q2(a) Module 2, 14 Marks]** *Design the visual display layout for the cockpit instrument panel of a small trainer aircraft. The pilot's eye is **750 mm** from the panel. Justify the character height, the choice between analog and digital readouts, and the colour-coding scheme. State the assumptions made.* **Model Solution (14 marks, distributed as below):** **(a) Character Height Calculation (7 marks)** Step 1 — KTU minimum character height for 20/20 vision: $$H_{\min} = \dfrac{D}{200} = \dfrac{750}{200} = 3.75\ \text{mm}$$ Step 2 — Preferred (comfortable) character height: $$H_{\text{preferred}} = \dfrac{D}{300} = \dfrac{750}{300} = 2.50\ \text{mm}$$ Step 3 — Engineering selection (use the larger value with safety factor 2 to allow for cockpit vibration and aging pilots): $$H_{\text{design}} = 2 \times 3.75 = 7.5\ \text{mm}$$ → We select $H = 8$ mm for the primary numeric readouts and $H = 5$ mm for secondary legends. (3 marks) Step 4 — Visual angle check: $$\theta = \dfrac{H}{D} = \dfrac{8}{750} = 0.01067\ \text{rad} = 36.6'$$ This is **36 times** the Snellen minimum, ensuring legibility even under vibration, low illumination, and pilot fatigue. (2 marks) Step 5 — Reading-time analysis using $T = a + b\log_2 N$: - For a digital altimeter with 5 digits (00000–99999): $T = 0.5 + 0.10 \times \log_2 10^5 = 0.5 + 1.66 = 2.16$ s. - For an analog dial with 100 tick marks: $T = 0.5 + 0.10 \times \log_2 100 = 1.16$ s. → **Analog dial is faster for *trend* reading.** (2 marks) **(b) Choice of Display Type and Colour Code (7 marks)** | Parameter | Digital Readout | Analog Dial | Selection | |---|---|---|---| | Absolute precision | High | Lower | Digital wins (altimeter) | | Trend / rate | Poor | High | **Analog wins (airspeed, RPM)** | | Power consumption | Higher | Lower (LCD passive) | Analog wins | | Visibility in sunlight | Lower | Higher (reflective) | **Analog wins** | → **Mixed layout**: Digital for *numeric* values (altimeter, heading), analog for *trend* values (airspeed, vertical speed, RPM, engine temperature). (3 marks) **Colour-coding scheme (per ISO 3864 / FAA-HF-STD-002):** (4 marks) - **Red bands** on RPM and exhaust-gas-temperature gauges: never-exceed limits. - **Yellow arc**: caution range. - **Green arc**: normal operating range. - **White arc**: flap-operating range. - **Red warning lamps** (master caution, fire): danger. - **Amber lamps**: caution. - **Green lamps**: gear down, system OK. - **Blue lamps**: information (e.g., anti-ice on). **Assumptions:** 20/20 pilot vision, 750 mm eye-to-panel distance, daylight operation, ISO 3864 compliance. > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall (KTU Examiner Note):** Students often quote $H = D/200$ *or* $H = D/300$ without explaining **which one to use when**. You must justify your choice with a safety factor and discuss reading time using the Hick-Hyman equation. Marks are deducted if you skip the $T$ calculation or forget to state assumptions. Also, do **not** use *red and green together* without shape redundancy — colour-blind pilots will fail the test. --- #### Question B — Design of Multi-Modal Alarm System (Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PBIET604, Q2(b) Module 2, 14 Marks]** *A hospital ICU has an ambient noise level of 62 dB(A) at 1 kHz. Design a multi-modal alarm system for a critical-care patient monitor. Justify the choice of frequency, SPL, colour, and tactual pattern. Include a redundancy strategy for hearing-impaired staff.* **Model Solution (14 marks):** **(a) Auditory Display Design (7 marks)** Step 1 — Apply the 15 dB SNR rule: $$L_{\text{signal, min}} = 62 + 15 = 77\ \text{dB(A)}$$ Add 5 dB safety margin for inattention: $$L_{\text{design}} = 82\ \text{dB(A)}$$ Step 2 — Choose the centre frequency. The ear is most sensitive near 1–4 kHz; use a **warble tone** sweeping **1 kHz → 3 kHz** to bypass frequency-specific masking. (2 marks) Step 3 — Apply IEC 60601-1-8 alarm priority tones: - Low priority: 1 soft pulse, 440 Hz. - Medium: 2 pulses, 500 Hz. - High: 3 pulses, 1500 Hz. - Critical: warble, 80 dB(A) min. - Life-threatening: continuous high-intensity 2.5 kHz. (2 marks) Step 4 — Stevens' Power Law check: a 10 dB increase roughly doubles loudness; ensure $\psi_{\text{alarm}} \ge 2 \psi_{\text{ambient}}$. (1 mark) Step 5 — Loudness budgeting so the alarm does not exceed 110 dB(A) at 1 m from the bed. (1 mark) Step 6 — Continuous tone of > 2 minutes is *not* allowed under IEC 60601-1-8 (it may desensitize staff). Use a pulsing pattern of 0.5 s ON / 2 s OFF. (1 mark) **(b) Visual + Tactual + Olfactory Redundancy (7 marks)** **Visual** (3 marks): - **Red flashing beacon** (1 Hz, 50% duty cycle) above the bed, ≥ 1000 cd luminous intensity. - **Colour code** per ISO 3864: red = danger, yellow = caution, green = normal. - **Bedside LCD** with bold text "**VENT ALARM**" — character height $H = D/200$, e.g., for $D = 1500$ mm, $H = 7.5$ mm. **Tactual** (2 marks): - **Vibrotactile pager** worn by nurse on the wrist (Pacinian band 200–250 Hz, 0.3 g amplitude). - Two-point separation ≥ 3 mm (fingertip resolution). - Distinctive pattern: 3 short pulses for high priority. **Olfactory** (1 mark): - Optional secondary cue: a *pulsed* menthol or *eucalyptus* scent from a wall dispenser to engage arousal. (Caveat: olfactory adaptation in 30–60 s — not for sustained alarm.) **Redundancy Strategy Summary** (1 mark): | Operator Condition | Primary Channel | Secondary | |---|---|---| | Normal hearing, eyes-on | Auditory | Visual flashing | | Hearing-impaired staff | Visual flashing | Tactual pager | | Night shift, dimmed lights | Tactual pager | Olfactory cue | | Eyes busy, noisy | Auditory warble | Visual LCD | > [!WARNING] > **Valuation Pitfall (KTU Examiner Note):** Do **not** state "alarm should be 200 dB" — that is non-physical. Always quote SPL in dB(A) and check against the OSHA daily permissible exposure (90 dB for 8 h). Do not use a *single* modality for life-critical alarms; KTU/ISO 60601-1-8 *requires* multi-modal redundancy for *high* and *critical* priority alarms. Forgetting to cite the IEC standard costs 2 marks. --- ### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Points (PBIET604, Module 2):** > 1. A **display** is a *machine-to-human* transducer; always pair it with a **control** (human-to-machine) to close the loop. > 2. The **four modalities** are *visual, auditory, tactual, olfactory*; they map to *eye, ear, skin, nose* respectively. > 3. **Character height** for legibility: $H = D/200$ (min), $H = D/300$ (preferred). > 4. **Reading time** for $N$ alternatives: $T = a + b\log_2 N$ with $a \approx 0.5$ s, $b \approx 0.1$ s/bit. > 5. **Contrast ratio** $CR = L_{\max}/L_{\min}$ should be ≥ 7:1 for office displays. > 6. **Audible alarms** must be **+15 dB(A) above ambient**; use warble tone 1–3 kHz. > 7. **Stevens' Power Law**: $\psi = k I^\beta$, $\beta \approx 0.67$ for 1 kHz tone. > 8. **Sound Pressure Level**: $L = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$ with $p_0 = 20\ \mu$Pa. > 9. **Tactile** two-point threshold: 2–3 mm on fingertip, 40–70 mm on back → place the densest arrays on the hand. > 10. **Vibrotactile** sweet-spot frequency: 200–300 Hz (Pacinian corpuscles). > 11. **Olfactory** adaptation time-constant $\tau \approx 15$ s light, 60–90 s strong — pulse, do not hold. > 12. **Colour code** (ISO 3864): red = danger, yellow/amber = caution, green = safe, blue = mandatory, orange = warn of dangerous parts. > 13. **Redundancy** is the *cardinal rule* for life-critical displays: never rely on one sense. > 14. **Trends** are best read on **analog dials**; **precise numbers** are best on **digital readouts**. > 15. **Standards to memorise**: ISO 3864, ISO 7731, IEC 60601-1-8, FAA-HF-STD-002, ANSI Z535. > 16. **KTU's preferred safety factor** for character height in vibrating environments: **2×**. > 17. **Maximum continuous alarm** loudness without hearing damage: 110 dB(A) for short exposure. > 18. **Skin mechanoreceptors**: Meissner (10–100 Hz), Pacinian (100–300 Hz), Merkel (steady pressure), Ruffini (stretch). > 19. **Olfactory displays** are slow (300–500 ms) and adapt — only for *signature* hazards. > 20. **Always** state *assumptions* in KTU numerical answers: 20/20 vision, $D$, ambient SPL, operator age, environment. --- > **End of Module 2 Notes — Design of Visual, Auditory, Tactual and Olfactory Displays** > *APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University | 2024 Scheme | PBIET604 — Applied Ergonomics*

topics.title: Input and output in displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET604 - APPLIED ERGONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Input and output in displays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Human-Machine Systems (HMS)** and **Applied Ergonomics**, **Input and Output in Displays** refers to the bidirectional exchange of information between the **human operator** and the **machine/system** through engineered interfaces.

- **Output Displays (Information Output)**: Devices that *transmit information from the machine TO the human operator*. They convert internal system variables (electrical, mechanical, digital) into human-perceptible signals (visual, auditory, tactile). Examples include speedometers, LED indicators, CRT/LCD monitors, warning buzzers, and instrument panels.
- **Input Devices (Information Input)**: Devices that *transmit commands from the human operator TO the machine*. They convert human physical actions (movement, pressure, voice) into machine-readable signals. Examples include keyboards, mouse, joystick, steering wheels, push buttons, toggle switches, and foot pedals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**: Displays are the *output components* of a man-machine system, while controls constitute the *input components*. The ergonomic design of both determines the **compatibility, speed, accuracy, and safety** of human-machine interaction.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Driving a Car**
> 
> Imagine you are driving a modern car:
> 
> - **OUTPUT from the car TO you (Displays)**: The speedometer, fuel gauge, temperature warning light, rear-view mirror, GPS navigation screen, and the "beep" of a parking sensor. These are all *displays* — they tell you what the car is doing or what is happening around you.
> 
> - **INPUT from you TO the car (Controls)**: The steering wheel, accelerator pedal, brake pedal, gear shift, indicator stalk, and touchscreen buttons on the infotainment system. These are all *control inputs* — they let you command the car.
> 
> The **ergonomics** of this system ensures that you can quickly and accurately *read* the displays and *actuate* the controls without confusion, fatigue, or error. A poorly placed accelerator or a confusing fuel gauge can cause accidents — this is exactly what ergonomics aims to prevent.

---

## 1.3 Classification Overview

The discipline of *input and output in displays* is fundamentally categorized as follows:

**A. Based on Sensory Modality of Output**

| Modality | Display Type | Typical Use |
|----------|-------------|-------------|
| Visual | Cathode Ray Tube (CRT), LCD, LED, analog dials, gauges | Primary information channel (≈ **80%–90%** of all display data) |
| Auditory | Alarms, buzzers, speech synthesizers, sirens | Warning and alerting in high-vision-load environments |
| Tactile/Haptic | Vibrating seats, Braille readers, force-feedback joysticks | For visually impaired, military pilots, surgeons |

**B. Based on Nature of Input Control**

| Control Type | Action | Examples |
|--------------|--------|----------|
| Discrete | Binary ON/OFF | Push button, toggle switch |
| Continuous | Variable magnitude | Steering wheel, joystick, slider |
| Sequential | Ordered selection | Keyboard, rotary selector |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Human Sensory Bandwidth**: Vision dominates input-output design because the human eye has the **highest information processing bandwidth** of all senses, capable of resolving approximately **10⁷ bits/second** under ideal conditions, compared to ≈10⁴ bits/sec for hearing and ≈10³ bits/sec for touch.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Ergonomics

In any industrial workstation, cockpit, control room, or consumer product, the **efficiency, error-rate, and operator fatigue** are directly proportional to the *quality of input-output design*. KTU emphasizes this topic because:

1. It bridges **psychophysics** (how humans perceive signals) with **engineering design** (how machines emit/receive those signals).
2. It introduces **quantitative design formulas** (visual angle, Snellen's acuity, control force ratios) that are frequently tested.
3. It links to **Course Outcomes (COs)** related to designing ergonomic workstations and evaluating human-machine compatibility.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Principles of Visual Displays

A visual display must satisfy **three functional requirements** simultaneously:

1. **Visibility** — the operator can *detect* the signal under expected ambient lighting.
2. **Legibility** — the operator can *read* alphanumeric or symbolic content.
3. **Understandability** — the operator can *interpret* meaning within ≤ **2 seconds** (KTU standard for critical-warning displays).

### 2.1.1 Critical Parameters of Visual Displays

- **Luminance (L)** — measured in **cd/m²** (candela per square meter). Must be ≥ **3× the ambient background luminance** for comfortable reading.
- **Contrast Ratio (C)** — defined as:

$$C = \frac{L_{max} - L_{min}}{L_{min}}$$

where $L_{max}$ is the brightest element and $L_{min}$ is the darkest element on the display surface. A minimum contrast ratio of **3:1** is acceptable for passive reading; **7:1 or higher** is required for safety-critical labels.

- **Visual Angle (θ)** — the angle subtended at the eye by a displayed character:

$$\theta = \frac{h}{D}$$

where $h$ is the **character height (mm)** and $D$ is the **viewing distance (mm)**. Result is in **radians**; for degrees multiply by $\frac{180}{\pi}$.

- **Refresh Rate** — must be ≥ **50 Hz** to avoid flicker perception (CRT era); modern LCDs require ≥ **60 Hz** with a **response time ≤ 8 ms** to avoid motion blur.

### 2.1.2 Snellen's Law of Visual Acuity

The smallest character a "standard" human eye can resolve subtends an angle of **1 arc-minute (1′) ≈ 0.0002909 rad**. This is the foundation for sizing characters on displays:

$$h_{min} = D \times \tan(1') \approx \frac{D}{3438}$$

where:
- $h_{min}$ = minimum legible character height in **mm**
- $D$ = viewing distance in **mm**

For **5× safety margin** (KTU recommended for industrial displays):

$$h_{recommended} = \frac{5 \cdot D}{3438} \approx \frac{D}{687.6}$$

### 2.1.3 Color Coding of Displays

| Color | Conventional Meaning | Hazard Level |
|-------|---------------------|--------------|
| **Red** | Danger / Stop / Emergency | High |
| **Yellow / Amber** | Caution / Warning | Moderate |
| **Green** | Safe / Normal / Go | Low |
| **Blue** | Information / Neutral | Informational |
| **White** | General readouts | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Approximately **8% of males and 0.5% of females** exhibit some form of **color-vision deficiency** (CVD). Pure red–green coding should *never* be the only distinguishing feature; redundant coding (shape, position, label) is mandatory per **ISO 9358** and **ANSI Z535**.

---

## 2.2 Operational Principles of Auditory Displays

Auditory displays are used when the operator's eyes are occupied, when signals must attract attention across a room, or when timing-critical warnings are needed.

### 2.2.1 Key Acoustic Parameters

- **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** measured in **decibels (dB)**:

$$SPL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right)$$

where $p$ = RMS sound pressure (Pa) and $p_0 = 20\ \mu Pa$ is the reference threshold of human hearing.

- **Effective Masking Level**: To be heard, an alarm must be **at least 10–15 dB above** the ambient noise level.
- **Recommended Alarm Frequency Range**: **500 Hz – 3000 Hz** because the human ear has maximum sensitivity in this band (equal-loudness contour peak ≈ 3–4 kHz).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall**: The **inverse-square law** governs alarm audibility in open spaces — doubling the distance from the source reduces SPL by **6 dB**. Designers must specify alarm loudness at the *farthest expected listener position*, not at the source.

### 2.2.2 Auditory Display Categories

| Type | Example | Function |
|------|---------|----------|
| Signal | Telephone ring | Alert attention |
| Warning | Fire alarm, low-fuel beep | Indicate hazard |
| Feedback | Keypad click, printer tone | Confirm action |
| Speech | GPS voice prompts | Convey complex information |

---

## 2.3 Operational Principles of Input Controls (Input Devices)

A control is *ergonomically effective* when it satisfies:

1. **Identifiability** — operator can recognize it by feel or sight without confusion.
2. **Spatial Compatibility** — control movement direction matches expected response direction (e.g., moving a lever *up* should logically *increase* the controlled variable).
3. **Force Compatibility** — required actuation force is appropriate for the operator population (5th percentile female to 95th percentile male).
4. **Feedback** — control provides clear kinesthetic, visual, or auditory confirmation of actuation.

### 2.3.1 Control Force and Resistance (for Rotary Controls)

$$M_{resistance} = M_{friction} + M_{inertia} + M_{damping}$$

$$M_{friction} = \mu \cdot W \cdot r$$

where $\mu$ = coefficient of friction, $W$ = axial load, $r$ = shaft radius.

### 2.3.2 Recommended Actuation Forces (KTU/ANSI/HFES 100 standards)

| Control Type | Recommended Force |
|--------------|-------------------|
| Push button (finger) | **2.5 – 5 N** |
| Toggle switch | **2 – 4 N** |
| Steering wheel (passenger car) | **15 – 30 N** at rim |
| Brake pedal | **70 – 150 N** |
| Joystick | **2 – 8 N** |

---

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU numerical problems on input/output displays.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Constants |
|---|----------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | Visual angle (rad) | $\theta = \dfrac{h}{D}$ | rad |
| 2 | Visual angle (deg) | $\theta_{deg} = \dfrac{180}{\pi}\cdot\dfrac{h}{D}$ | degrees |
| 3 | Minimum letter height | $h_{min} = D \cdot \tan(1') \approx \dfrac{D}{3438}$ | mm |
| 4 | Recommended letter height (5× margin) | $h_{rec} \approx \dfrac{D}{687.6}$ | mm |
| 5 | Contrast ratio | $C = \dfrac{L_{max} - L_{min}}{L_{min}}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Weber's fraction for brightness | $\dfrac{\Delta L}{L} \approx 0.01\text{ to }0.02$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | SPL in dB | $SPL = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right)$ | dB SPL, $p_0=20\,\mu Pa$ |
| 8 | Inverse-square SPL attenuation | $\Delta SPL = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d_2}{d_1}\right)$ | dB |
| 9 | Display area for N elements | $A_{min} = N \cdot \dfrac{D^2}{3438^2}$ | mm² |
| 10 | Frequency of display refresh (no flicker) | $f_{min} = 50$ Hz (CRT), $60$ Hz (LCD) | Hz |
| 11 | Snellen ratio | $V = \dfrac{d}{D}$ where $d$ = test distance, $D$ = readable distance | dimensionless |
| 12 | Reaction time to visual stimulus | $t_v \approx 0.25$ s | s |
| 13 | Reaction time to auditory stimulus | $t_a \approx 0.17$ s | s |
| 14 | Recommended push-button force | $F = 2.5 \text{ to } 5$ N | N |
| 15 | Display luminance ratio (KTU) | $L_{display} \geq 3 \cdot L_{ambient}$ | cd/m² |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These principles are applied in:

- **Aviation cockpits** (Glass Cockpit Display Systems — GCDS) — head-up displays (HUD) use Snellen's law to size characters against an unlimited viewing distance.
- **Automotive dashboards** — contrast ratios and luminance levels are engineered against IEC 60068 ambient conditions.
- **Medical monitoring** (ICU multi-parameter displays) — auditory alarms are set at ≥ 10 dB above ICU baseline (typically 55 dB).
- **Nuclear control rooms** — large-screen wall displays use 30° × 20° visual angles with character heights computed using the formula sheet above.
- **Consumer electronics** — smartphones apply **≈ 3.5 mm character height** at a typical reading distance of **350 mm**, comfortably satisfying $h \geq D/100$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Character Height for a Display (Snellen + KTU Margin)

### Problem Context
An industrial gauge is mounted on a control panel. The operator's eye-to-gauge distance is **D = 1200 mm**. Determine (a) the minimum legible character height (Snellen 1 arc-minute), and (b) the KTU-recommended character height using a **5× safety margin**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State the fundamental Snellen relation.**

$$\theta_{min} = 1 \text{ arc-minute} = \dfrac{1}{60}\degree = \dfrac{\pi}{10800}\text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to radians for engineering use.**

$$\theta_{min} = \dfrac{\pi}{10800} = 2.909 \times 10^{-4}\text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the small-angle approximation.**

For small angles, $\tan\theta \approx \theta$ (rad). Hence:

$$h_{min} = D \cdot \tan\theta_{min} \approx D \cdot \theta_{min}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{min} &= 1200\ \text{mm} \times 2.909 \times 10^{-4} \\
h_{min} &= 1200 \times 0.0002909 \\
h_{min} &= 0.3491\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply KTU-recommended 5× safety factor.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{recommended} &= 5 \times h_{min} \\
h_{recommended} &= 5 \times 0.3491 \\
h_{recommended} &= 1.746\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{h_{min} \approx 0.35\ \text{mm}, \quad h_{recommended} \approx 1.75\ \text{mm at } D = 1200\ \text{mm}}$$

### Interpretation

At 1200 mm, a character as small as 0.35 mm *can* be resolved by a standard eye, but to ensure readability under vibration, low contrast, and operator fatigue, the **practical height of ≈ 1.75 mm** must be used. This is why aircraft gauges use character heights of **5–8 mm** even at 1.5–2 m viewing distances.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Visual Angle Calculation for a Multi-Line Display

### Problem Context
A flight management display is positioned **1500 mm** from the pilot's eye. The font height on the display is **6 mm**. Determine (a) the visual angle in radians, (b) the visual angle in arc-minutes, and (c) whether this satisfies the KTU minimum legibility criterion.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the visual-angle formula.**

$$\theta = \dfrac{h}{D}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values (h in mm, D in mm, the ratio is dimensionless and equals radians for small angles).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \dfrac{6}{1500} \\
\theta &= 0.004\ \text{rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert radians to degrees.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{deg} &= 0.004 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} \\
\theta_{deg} &= 0.004 \times 57.2958 \\
\theta_{deg} &= 0.2292\degree
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert degrees to arc-minutes (1° = 60′).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{arcmin} &= 0.2292 \times 60 \\
\theta_{arcmin} &= 13.75 \text{ arc-minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with KTU minimum (1 arc-minute).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Safety Factor} &= \dfrac{13.75}{1} = 13.75\times \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{\theta = 4 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{rad} = 0.229\degree = 13.75\text{ arc-minutes} \gg 1\text{ arc-minute (KTU OK)}}$$

### Interpretation

The display is **13.75 times** more legible than the minimum requirement, providing generous margin for in-flight vibration and stress conditions.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Inverse-Square Attenuation of an Auditory Alarm

### Problem Context
An emergency siren produces **SPL = 100 dB** at **1 m** from its source. The control room operator is **20 m** away. Determine (a) the SPL at the operator's ear, (b) whether the alarm will be heard against ambient noise of **65 dB**, and (c) the maximum effective distance where the alarm remains ≥ **15 dB above ambient**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the inverse-square law for SPL.**

$$\Delta SPL = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{d_2}{d_1}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute d₁ = 1 m, d₂ = 20 m.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta SPL &= 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{20}{1}\right) \\
\Delta SPL &= 20 \times \log_{10}(20) \\
\Delta SPL &= 20 \times 1.3010 \\
\Delta SPL &= 26.02\ \text{dB (attenuation)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute SPL at operator's ear.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPL_{operator} &= 100 - 26.02 \\
SPL_{operator} &= 73.98\ \text{dB} \approx 74\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare against ambient (65 dB).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta SPL_{excess} &= 74 - 65 = 9\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the required excess is **≥ 15 dB** for reliable perception, this alarm is **INADEQUATE** at 20 m.

**Step 5 — Solve for maximum effective distance where SPL_excess = 15 dB.**

Let $d_{max}$ = maximum distance such that:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPL(d_{max}) &= SPL_1 - 20\log_{10}(d_{max}/1) \\
\text{Set } SPL(d_{max}) &= 65 + 15 = 80\ \text{dB} \\
20\log_{10}(d_{max}) &= 100 - 80 = 20 \\
\log_{10}(d_{max}) &= 1 \\
d_{max} &= 10\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{SPL_{operator} = 74\ \text{dB} < 80\ \text{dB threshold},\quad d_{max} = 10\ \text{m for 15 dB excess}}$$

### Interpretation

The siren source must either be (a) relocated to within **10 m** of any operator, or (b) upgraded to **≥ 100 + 20·log₁₀(20) ≈ 126 dB at 1 m** to achieve 80 dB at 20 m. This calculation appears frequently in **plant layout design (KTU Module 4)** and **safety engineering (Module 5)**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Compatibility Between Display and Control Types

The most common KTU exam question is to select the correct control-display mapping based on **population stereotype** and **spatial compatibility**.

| Display Type | Recommended Control Type | Reason |
|--------------|--------------------------|--------|
| Vertical scale (e.g., thermometer) | Vertical slider (up = more) | Directional congruence |
| Rotary dial (e.g., clock face) | Rotary knob (CW = more) | Motion congruence |
| Horizontal scale | Horizontal slider (right = more) | Reading direction |
| Binary indicator (ON/OFF) | Toggle switch / push button | Discrete correspondence |
| Multi-state warning | Sequential push-button selector | One-to-one mapping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Population Stereotypes**: Operators expect that:
> - *Turning a knob clockwise* should **increase** the displayed value (in **> 95%** of cultures).
> - *Moving a lever to the right* should **increase** a horizontal gauge value.
> - *Up* = increase, *down* = decrease (gravitational congruence).
> 
> Violating these stereotypes increases error rates by **3–7×** and reaction time by **0.4 – 1.2 seconds** — both **unacceptable** in safety-critical systems.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Selecting Display Type for an ICU Heart-Rate Monitor

A bedside ICU monitor must convey heart-rate (continuous variable) and alarm status (discrete). Determine the optimal display combination.

**Step 1 — Identify information type.**
- Heart-rate: **Quantitative, continuously variable, must be read precisely** → use **digital numeric display + analog bar graph** (redundant coding).
- Alarm status: **Discrete, attention-critical** → use **auditory alarm** + **flashing red light** (multi-modal redundancy).

**Step 2 — Apply Snellen sizing.**
Typical viewing distance = **1500 mm**. Minimum char height for heart-rate digits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{min} &= 1500 / 3438 = 0.436\ \text{mm} \\
h_{rec} &= 5 \times 0.436 = 2.18\ \text{mm} \rightarrow \text{use } 5\text{–}8\ \text{mm}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply contrast criterion.**
For ICU lighting ≈ 100 lux, display luminance must be:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{display} &\geq 3 \cdot L_{ambient}
\end{aligned}
$$

Choose backlit LCD with $L \geq 300\ cd/m^2$.

**Step 4 — Apply auditory criterion.**
Set alarm SPL ≥ 10 dB above ICU baseline ≈ 55 dB → alarm SPL = **65 dB minimum** at the bedside.

### Final Answer

The optimal design uses a **redundant visual + auditory system** with character heights of 5–8 mm, display luminance ≥ 300 cd/m², and alarm SPL ≥ 65 dB at 1 m.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Human-Machine Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Human Operator Eyes] -->|Detects| DSP[Output Display Visual Auditory Tactile]
    DSP -->|Stimulus| BR[Human Brain Perception Cognition]
    BR -->|Decision| MO[Human Motor System]
    MO -->|Force Motion| IN[Input Device Keyboard Mouse Lever Pedal]
    IN -->|Signal| M1[Machine Controller Processor]
    M1 -->|Command| AC[Machine Actuator]
    AC -->|Physical Change| ENV[Process Plant Vehicle System]
    ENV -->|Feedback Sensor| DSP
    style DSP fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style IN fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style BR fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Interpretation**: The diagram shows the closed-loop feedback between operator, display (output), control (input), and machine. Ergonomic design must optimize both **DSP** and **IN** simultaneously.

---

## 4.2 Classification Diagram: Display Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Displays Information Output] --> B[Visual Displays]
    A --> C[Auditory Displays]
    A --> D[Tactile Displays]
    B --> B1[Quantitative Numeric dials Digital readouts]
    B --> B2[Qualitative Warning lamps Status indicators]
    B --> B3[Representational Icons Pictograms Mimics]
    C --> C1[Signals Telephone ring Horn]
    C --> C2[Warnings Fire alarm Buzzer]
    C --> C3[Speech Voice prompts GPS]
    D --> D1[Vibration Phone alerts]
    D --> D2[Force feedback Steering Simulator]
    D --> D3[Braille Refreshable tactile displays]
    style A fill:#FF6B6B,color:#fff
    style B fill:#4ECDC4
    style C fill:#FFE66D
    style D fill:#A8DADC
```

---

## 4.3 Classification Diagram: Input Controls

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Input Controls Information Input] --> X1[Discrete Controls]
    X --> X2[Continuous Controls]
    X1 --> X1a[Push button]
    X1 --> X1b[Toggle switch]
    X1 --> X1c[Selector switch]
    X2 --> X2a[Rotary knob]
    X2 --> X2b[Joystick]
    X2 --> X2c[Steering wheel]
    X2 --> X2d[Slider Linear]
    style X fill:#6A0572,color:#fff
    style X1 fill:#F5A623
    style X2 fill:#50C878
```

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Ergonomic Design Process for Displays

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Identify Information Type Quantitative vs Qualitative] --> S2[Step 2 Select Sensory Modality Vision Hearing Touch]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Compute Sizing Snellen Formula]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Apply Luminance and Contrast Standards]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Apply Color Coding ISO 9358]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Test with Operators User Trial]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Iterate and Refine]
    style S1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style S2 fill:#FFD700
    style S3 fill:#98FB98
    style S4 fill:#87CEFA
    style S5 fill:#DDA0DD
    style S6 fill:#F0E68C
    style S7 fill:#FFA07A
```

---

## 4.5 Compatibility Matrix: Display–Control Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Outputs
    O1[Vertical Scale]
    O2[Rotary Dial]
    O3[Horizontal Scale]
    O4[Binary Light]
    end
    subgraph Inputs
    I1[Vertical Slider Up Increase]
    I2[Rotary Knob CW Increase]
    I3[Horizontal Slider Right Increase]
    I4[Toggle Switch]
    end
    O1 --- I1
    O2 --- I2
    O3 --- I3
    O4 --- I4
    style O1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style O2 fill:#FFE4B5
    style O3 fill:#FFE4B5
    style O4 fill:#FFE4B5
    style I1 fill:#B0E0E6
    style I2 fill:#B0E0E6
    style I3 fill:#B0E0E6
    style I4 fill:#B0E0E6
```

> **Reading guide**: Each output display on the left is *ergonomically matched* to the corresponding input control on the right by spatial compatibility.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3-MARK QUESTIONS

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the term "display" in the context of applied ergonomics. Differentiate between quantitative and qualitative displays with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Display Definition (1 mark):** A display is any device or component of a man-machine system that presents information from the machine to the human operator in a form perceptible to the human senses, primarily visual, auditory, or tactile.
2. **Quantitative Displays (1 mark):** These present numerical or scaled information with precise magnitudes. *Example*: A digital speedometer showing "65 km/h", or an analog pressure gauge.
3. **Qualitative Displays (1 mark):** These convey only the status or trend (e.g., ON/OFF, high/normal/low) without exact magnitude. *Example*: A green/red status indicator lamp, or a bar-graph trend monitor.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State Snellen's law of visual acuity and write the formula for minimum legible character height on a display.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Statement (1.5 marks):** Snellen's law states that the minimum visual angle resolvable by the normal human eye is **1 arc-minute (≈ 0.0002909 rad)**.
2. **Formula (1.5 marks):**

$$
h_{min} = D \cdot \tan(1') \approx \dfrac{D}{3438}
$$

where $h_{min}$ is the minimum letter height in mm and $D$ is the viewing distance in mm. For practical design, a safety factor of 3–5 is applied.

---

---

## PART B — 14-MARK QUESTIONS (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the various types of visual displays used in man-machine systems, with examples. Discuss the ergonomic design considerations for sizing alphanumeric characters on a visual display. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industrial digital display is mounted on a control panel. The operator's eye-to-display distance is **800 mm**. Calculate:
- (i) The minimum legible character height using Snellen's criterion. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) The KTU-recommended character height using a 5× safety factor. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) The visual angle in degrees subtended by the recommended character. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks):**

Visual displays are classified based on the **type of information** they convey:

1. **Quantitative Displays** — present precise numerical values.
   - *Analog*: Speedometer dial, pressure gauge, thermometer.
   - *Digital*: 7-segment numeric readout, LCD/LED count display.

2. **Qualitative Displays** — convey only the state or trend.
   - Warning lamps (red/yellow/green).
   - Status indicators (ON/OFF, RUN/STOP).

3. **Representational/Iconic Displays** — mimic the system itself.
   - Mimic diagrams (process flow in a control room).
   - Pictograms (fuel-pump icon, oil-can warning).

**Ergonomic design considerations for sizing characters:**

- **Visual angle** must be ≥ **1 arc-minute** (Snellen limit).
- Recommended visual angle: **12–18 arc-minutes** for industrial displays (≈ **10×** Snellen minimum).
- Character **height-to-width ratio** of approximately **3:2** for digits and **1:1** for letters.
- **Stroke width** ≈ **1/8 to 1/6 of character height**.
- Use **uppercase letters** for short labels, mixed case for long sentences.
- Ensure **adequate spacing** between characters (≥ **25% of character height**).
- Maintain **luminance contrast ratio ≥ 3:1** (preferably **7:1**) against background.

---

**Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $D = 800\ \text{mm}$.

**(i) Minimum character height (Snellen's criterion):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{min} &= D \cdot \tan(1') = \dfrac{800}{3438} \\
h_{min} &= 0.2327\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) KTU-recommended character height (5× safety factor):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{rec} &= 5 \times h_{min} = 5 \times 0.2327 \\
h_{rec} &= 1.163\ \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Visual angle in degrees subtended by the recommended character:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \dfrac{h_{rec}}{D} = \dfrac{1.163}{800} = 1.454 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{rad} \\
\theta_{deg} &= 1.454 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} \\
\theta_{deg} &= 0.0833\degree
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to arc-minutes for clarity:

$$\theta = 0.0833 \times 60 = 5.00 \text{ arc-minutes (which is exactly 5× the Snellen minimum, confirming the safety factor).}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the different types of **auditory displays** used in industrial and consumer applications. State the ergonomic guidelines for setting alarm loudness relative to ambient noise. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fire alarm sounder produces **SPL = 95 dB at 1 m**. The farthest workstation in the workshop is located **15 m** from the sounder. The ambient noise level is **70 dB**.
- (i) Calculate the SPL at the workstation. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) Determine whether the alarm will be perceived reliably (require ≥ 10 dB above ambient). **(2 marks)**
- (iii) Suggest TWO design modifications to improve alarm perception. **(3 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Part (a) (7 Marks):**

**Auditory Display Types (with sketches described in text):**

1. **Signal Sounds** — short tones used to attract attention. *Example*: Telephone ring, microwave beep, elevator arrival chime.
2. **Warning Alarms** — louder, urgent sounds indicating hazard. *Example*: Fire alarm, ambulance siren, gas-leak buzzer.
3. **Speech Displays** — natural-language messages. *Example*: GPS navigation, in-car "door open" voice prompts, ATC radio.
4. **Auditory Icons (Earcons)** — abstract sound patterns whose meaning is learned. *Example*: Camera shutter sound (success), error buzz (failure).

**Ergonomic Guidelines for Alarm Loudness:**

- Alarm SPL must be **at least 10–15 dB above ambient noise** at the listener's ear.
- The frequency content must lie in the **500 Hz – 3000 Hz** band where the ear is most sensitive.
- Use **pulsed/periodic signals** rather than continuous tones to reduce habituation.
- **Avoid startle effect**: limit peak SPL to **≤ 110 dB** at 1 m for routine warnings; higher only for genuine emergencies.
- Specify loudness at the **farthest listener location**, not at the source.
- Provide **redundant visual signals** (flashing light) for hearing-impaired personnel.

---

**Model Answer — Part (b) (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $SPL_1 = 95$ dB at $d_1 = 1$ m, $d_2 = 15$ m, ambient = $70$ dB.

**(i) SPL at the workstation using inverse-square law:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta SPL &= 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{15}{1}\right) \\
\Delta SPL &= 20 \times 1.1761 = 23.52\ \text{dB} \\
SPL_{workstation} &= 95 - 23.52 = 71.48\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Reliability check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta SPL_{excess} &= 71.48 - 70 = 1.48\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the requirement is **≥ 10 dB above ambient**, the alarm is **NOT reliably perceived** — it exceeds ambient by only 1.48 dB.

**(iii) Two design modifications:**

1. **Increase source SPL** — choose a sounder with **≥ 105 dB at 1 m**, yielding ≈ **81 dB at 15 m**, which gives 11 dB margin above ambient.

2. **Add redundant visual alarm** — install a **strobe light** co-located with each workstation so that even if the auditory signal is masked, the visual flash is unmissable.

3. *(Alternate modification)*: Add **intermediate repeater sounders** every 7–8 m so each workstation is always within 8 m of a sounder.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the 5× safety factor** on Snellen character height — students often quote $h_{min}$ instead of the *practical* $h_{rec}$ and lose **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up inverse-square law** — using $-10\log_{10}$ (which is intensity, not pressure) instead of $-20\log_{10}$ (which is the correct SPL formula). Always check: SPL uses **pressure**, hence the factor is **20**, not 10.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert radians to degrees or arc-minutes** when the question asks for a specific unit.
> 4. **Confusing input and output** — buttons and keyboards are *inputs*; gauges and screens are *outputs*. Listing these incorrectly loses CO-mapping marks.
> 5. **Ignoring population stereotypes** (clockwise = increase, up = increase, right = increase). Always justify control-display mapping with *spatial compatibility*.
> 6. **Not mentioning color-vision deficiency** (~8% males) when discussing color coding — this is a frequent 1-mark loss on any question involving color displays.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Input and Output in Displays**

- ✅ **Display = OUTPUT** from machine → human. **Control = INPUT** from human → machine.
- ✅ **Snellen's minimum visual angle** = **1 arc-minute** ≈ **1/3438 rad**.
- ✅ **Practical character height** formula: $h_{rec} \approx D/687.6$ (5× safety margin).
- ✅ **Contrast ratio** $C = (L_{max} - L_{min})/L_{min}$; minimum **3:1**, preferred **7:1**.
- ✅ **Display luminance** should be **≥ 3× ambient luminance** (cd/m²).
- ✅ **Auditory SPL formula**: $SPL = 20\log_{10}(p/p_0)$, with $p_0 = 20\ \mu Pa$.
- ✅ **Inverse-square attenuation**: $\Delta SPL = 20\log_{10}(d_2/d_1)$ (factor **20**, not 10).
- ✅ **Alarms must be ≥ 10–15 dB above ambient** at the listener's position.
- ✅ **Alarm frequency band** = 500 Hz – 3 kHz (peak human ear sensitivity).
- ✅ **Reaction time**: visual ≈ **0.25 s**, auditory ≈ **0.17 s**.
- ✅ **Color coding** (KTU/ISO 9358): **Red** = danger, **Yellow** = caution, **Green** = safe, **Blue** = info.
- ✅ **Population stereotypes**: clockwise = increase, up = increase, right = increase.
- ✅ **Push-button force**: **2.5 – 5 N**; steering: **15 – 30 N**; brake: **70 – 150 N**.
- ✅ **Redundant coding** (color + shape + position) is mandatory due to **CVD** (~8% males).
- ✅ **Refresh rate**: ≥ **50 Hz CRT**, ≥ **60 Hz LCD** to avoid flicker.
- ✅ **Three display categories**: **Quantitative, Qualitative, Representational**.
- ✅ **Three control categories**: **Discrete, Continuous, Sequential**.
- ✅ **Multi-modal redundancy** (visual + auditory) is best practice for safety-critical warnings.
- ✅ **Snellen ratio** $V = d/D$ where $d$ = test distance, $D$ = distance at which character is readable; normal vision = **6/6** (or **20/20**).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Usage of text, graphics, symbols and codes in design of visual displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET604 - APPLIED ERGONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Usage of text, graphics, symbols and codes in design of visual displays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **visual display** is any man-made device, surface, or interface element that presents information to a human operator in the form of luminous, reflective, or graphic signals, intended to be perceived, decoded, and acted upon via the human visual system. In applied ergonomics, visual displays are classified as a subset of the **display–response system** within the man–machine model, where they function as the *signal-transmitting component* between the machine subsystem and the human subsystem.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition (Sanderson & Verrier, Ergonomics Context):**
> *“Visual displays are transducer elements that convert stored or dynamic information into light patterns of varying spatial, temporal, and chromatic properties, optimised for the perceptual limits of the human eye.”*

---

## 1.2 Visual Display Hierarchy

Visual displays in ergonomic design operate across **four parallel coding channels**, each suited to a particular type of information transfer:

| Channel | Information Type Best Suited | Cognitive Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Text** | Alphanumeric, lexical, quantitative | Sequential / symbolic |
| **Graphics** | Spatial, relational, holistic | Parallel / pre-attentive |
| **Symbols (Pictograms)** | Categorical, warning, function | Cross-cultural / iconic |
| **Codes (Color, Auditory, Tactile)** | Status, urgency, redundancy | Pre-attentive / alerting |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cockpit Window"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of the **flight-deck of a commercial aircraft** (e.g., Boeing 737 PFD). The **Primary Flight Display (PFD)** uses **graphics** (an artificial horizon tape) for spatial orientation, **text** for airspeed/altitude numerals, **symbols** (triangles, chevrons) for waypoints, and **color codes** (red = unsafe, green = safe, magenta = armed mode). Each element is engineered so the pilot decodes information in **under one second**, often without reading.
>
> This is the central ergonomic purpose of visual display design: **minimise decoding time, maximise legibility, and reduce perceptual error**.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Anchor:** This topic links directly to the Module-2 outcome:
> *"Apply ergonomic principles to design and evaluate text, graphic, symbolic, and coded visual displays for industrial, transport, and consumer systems."*
>
> It also bridges into **Module 3 (Workplace Design)** because most warning signs, control panels, and HMIs (Human–Machine Interfaces) depend on these four coding channels.

---

## 1.5 Visual Display Design — The Five Ergonomic Constraints

The KTU 2024 framework identifies **five hard constraints** that govern every visual display:

1. **Visibility** — the signal must be detected against its background.
2. **Legibility** — the signal must be discriminated (recognised as distinct).
3. **Readability** — the meaning must be comprehended.
4. **Interpretability** — the operator must know *what action* to take.
5. **Conspicuity** — critical signals must capture attention *first*.

> [!NOTE]
> Failure at any of these five stages constitutes a **human-factor error chain** — a leading cause of industrial accidents.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Text Displays — Design Principles

Text is the *most semantically rich* coding channel but also the *slowest to decode*. KTU examiners consistently test the following design parameters:

### 2.1.1 Font Selection
- **Sans-serif** (e.g., Helvetica, Arial) → for short-duration displays, control panels, vehicle dashboards.
- **Serif** (e.g., Times New Roman) → for long-duration reading (manuals, reports).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** Use **sans-serif fonts** for any display viewed for **< 5 seconds** or under **vibration/stress** conditions.

### 2.1.2 Character Geometry
- **Aspect ratio (Width : Height)** = ideally **0.6 : 1 to 0.8 : 1**.
- **Stroke-width to height ratio** = **1 : 8 to 1 : 10** (ANSI / ISO 9241-303).
- **X-height ratio** (lowercase 'x' to uppercase height) = **0.7 : 1** minimum for legibility.

### 2.1.3 Spacing Rules
- **Inter-character spacing** ≥ **0.5 × stroke width**.
- **Inter-word spacing** ≥ **1 × character width**.
- **Line spacing (leading)** = **1.2 × to 1.5 × character height**.

---

## 2.2 Graphics — Bertin's Visual Variables

**Jacques Bertin** (1967, *Sémiologie graphique*) defined the **seven retinal variables** that the eye can perceive independently and instantaneously. These form the analytical backbone of *pre-attentive processing* in graphic displays.

| # | Variable | Selective? | Associative? | Ordered? | Quantitative? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Position** (X, Y) | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| 2 | **Size** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| 3 | **Shape** | Yes | **Yes** | No | No |
| 4 | **Value** (Lightness) | Yes | Yes | Yes | No |
| 5 | **Color** (Hue) | Yes | **Yes** | No | No |
| 6 | **Orientation** | Yes | Yes | No | No |
| 7 | **Texture** | Yes | Yes | No | No |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pre-attentive variables** (processed in < 250 ms by the human visual system): Position, Size, Color, Orientation. Use these for **critical alerts**.
> **Slow variables** (require serial search): Shape, Texture.

---

## 2.3 Symbols and Pictograms — ISO Standards

Symbols are *language-independent* icons used for warnings, prohibitions, and functional identification. The key ISO standards tested in KTU are:

| Standard | Title | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 7010** | Safety signs | Workplace hazard pictograms (red prohibition, yellow warning, blue mandatory, green safety) |
| **ISO 3864** | Safety pictogram colours | Geometric shape + colour code rules |
| **ISO 7001** | Public information symbols | Airports, hospitals, transport hubs |
| **ISO 9241-210** | Human-centred design | UI/UX of interactive displays |
| **ANSI Z535** | Hazard communication | Industrial labelling (US standard, KTU comparative) |

### 2.3.1 ISO 3864 Colour + Shape Codes

| Colour | Geometric Shape | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Red** with diagonal bar | Circle | Prohibition |
| **Yellow** | Triangle | Warning |
| **Blue** | Circle | Mandatory action |
| **Green** | Square/Rectangle | Safe condition / First aid |

---

## 2.4 Codes — Modalities and Coding Principles

A *code* is a systematic mapping between a signal and its referent. Ergonomic coding must satisfy:

- **Compatibility** — the mapping matches the operator's expectation.
- **Distinctiveness** — code elements are perceptually separable.
- **Redundancy** — multiple channels convey the same message.
- **Standardisation** — codes match prior training and conventions.

### 2.4.1 The Four Code Modalities

1. **Color codes** — Red (danger), Amber (caution), Green (safe), Blue (informational). Used in traffic lights, control rooms.
2. **Auditory codes** — Alarms at 1000–4000 Hz peak; speech warnings for complex messages.
3. **Tactile codes** — Vibration patterns on vehicle steering wheels (lane-departure warnings).
4. **Olfactory / Symbolic codes** — Odour cues, smell-based hazard markers (rare, but used in gas-leak systems).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Trap:** Colour coding fails in **8% of male operators** (red-green colour blindness). Therefore, KTU 2024 stresses that colour codes **must be paired with shape or position codes** (redundancy principle).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — every formula below has appeared in KTU Board Exams from Dec 2020 onwards.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Constants | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Minimum character height for legibility** | $H_{min} = D \cdot \tan(\theta_{min})$ | mm ; D = viewing distance, θ_min ≈ **16′ (arc minutes)** | Display panels, gauges |
| 2 | **Preferred character height** | $H_{pref} = D \cdot \tan(\theta_{pref})$ | θ_pref ≈ **22′** | Reading-only displays |
| 3 | **Snellen visual-acuity bound** | $H_{snellen} = D \cdot \tan(5′)$ | θ = 5′ for normal (20/20) vision | Medical / aviation |
| 4 | **Rule-of-thumb character height** | $H = \dfrac{D}{200}$ to $\dfrac{D}{100}$ | Empirical (mm) | Rapid design estimation |
| 5 | **Stroke-width to height ratio** | $\dfrac{W}{H} = \dfrac{1}{8}$ to $\dfrac{1}{10}$ | ANSI Z535.6 | Letter legibility |
| 6 | **Visual angle (small angle)** | $\theta \approx \dfrac{H}{D}$ | radians | For θ < 10° |
| 7 | **Contrast ratio (WCAG)** | $CR = \dfrac{L_{1}+0.05}{L_{2}+0.05}$ | Luminances L₁ > L₂ | Display readability |
| 8 | **Michelson contrast** | $C_{m} = \dfrac{L_{max}-L_{min}}{L_{max}+L_{min}}$ | Range [-1, +1] | Optical displays |
| 9 | **Reaction-time (Hick's Law)** | $RT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n+1)$ | a ≈ 100 ms, b ≈ 150 ms | Choice-reaction displays |
| 10 | **Snellen fraction (visual acuity)** | $V = \dfrac{d}{D}$ | d = test distance, D = letter distance | Operator vision screening |

> **Critical Numbers to Memorise:**
> - **16 arc minutes** = legibility threshold.
> - **22 arc minutes** = preferred threshold.
> - **4.5 : 1** = minimum WCAG contrast ratio for normal text.
> - **1/8 to 1/10** = stroke ratio.
> - **D/200** = rule-of-thumb character-height formula.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aviation:** PFD altitude tape uses *colour* (green/orange/red) + *position* (vertical scale) + *shape* (triangles) — a triple-redundant code.
- **Automotive HUDs:** Speed displayed in 22′ arc for **legal minimum legibility**.
- **Industrial Control Rooms (DCS/SCADA):** Use Bertin's *size* and *colour* variables for anomaly detection at glance.
- **Medical Devices:** ISO 60601-1 § 8.5 mandates character height ≥ **D/175** for vital-sign displays.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Character Height from Viewing Distance

### Goal
Relate the **physical character height $H$** on a display to the **operator-to-screen distance $D$**, given a desired **visual angle $\theta$**.

### Given
- Viewing distance: $D$
- Required visual angle subtended by the character: $\theta$ (in arc minutes, denoted ')

### Step 1 — Convert visual angle from arc minutes to radians

For an arc-minute value $\theta'$:

$$
\theta_{rad} = \theta' \cdot \frac{\pi}{180 \cdot 60}
$$

For $\theta' = 16'$ (legibility minimum):

$$
\theta_{rad} = 16 \cdot \frac{\pi}{10800} = \frac{16\pi}{10800} \approx 4.654 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{rad}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the small-angle geometry

A character of height $H$ viewed from distance $D$ subtends the angle:

$$
\theta = 2 \cdot \arctan\!\left(\frac{H}{2D}\right)
$$

For small angles (θ < 0.17 rad, i.e. < 10°):

$$
\arctan(x) \approx x \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \theta \approx \frac{H}{D}
$$

> **Reasoning:** Because character displays are at least 50 cm from the eye, the angle subtended is < 1°, so the small-angle approximation is justified.

### Step 3 — Solve for the character height

$$
H = D \cdot \tan(\theta)
$$

For $\theta = 16'$ (legibility):

$$
H_{min} = D \cdot \tan\!\left(\frac{16\pi}{10800}\right) \approx D \cdot 0.00465
$$

### Step 4 — Express as a friendly ratio

$$
H_{min} \approx \frac{D}{215} \approx \frac{D}{200}
$$

For $\theta = 22'$ (preferred):

$$
H_{pref} \approx \frac{D}{156} \approx \frac{D}{150}
$$

### Step 5 — Final Working Rule

$$
\boxed{\;H = \dfrac{D}{200}\;(\text{minimum legible})\;\;\text{to}\;\;\dfrac{D}{100}\;(\text{easy-read})\;}
$$

### Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Question:** A CNC machine operator views a digital readout from **75 cm**. Calculate the minimum and preferred character heights (mm).
>
> **Solution:**
>
> $$H_{min} = \frac{750 \; \text{mm}}{200} = 3.75 \; \text{mm}$$
>
> $$H_{pref} = \frac{750 \; \text{mm}}{150} = 5.00 \; \text{mm}$$
>
> **Verification (using exact trigonometric form):**
>
> $$H_{min} = 750 \cdot \tan(16') = 750 \cdot 0.00465 = 3.49 \; \text{mm} \;(\approx 3.5 \; \text{mm})$$
>
> ✓ Within 7 % of the rule-of-thumb estimate — acceptable for design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Contrast Ratio for Display Readability

### Given
Two regions with luminance $L_1$ (foreground, brighter) and $L_2$ (background, darker), where $L_1 > L_2 > 0$.

### Step 1 — Michelson Contrast (Symmetric Form)

$$
C_{m} = \frac{L_1 - L_2}{L_1 + L_2}
$$

### Step 2 — Weber Contrast (Simple Form)

$$
C_{w} = \frac{L_1 - L_2}{L_2}
$$

> **Why two forms?** Michelson is used when both regions have **comparable area** (e.g., text on screen). Weber is used when the **foreground is small** (e.g., a single dot on a gauge).

### Step 3 — WCAG / W3C Contrast Ratio

The W3C accessibility standard defines:

$$
CR = \frac{L_1 + 0.05}{L_2 + 0.05}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Example

A black text on a light-grey background has $L_1 = 0.85$ (grey) and $L_2 = 0.10$ (black):

$$
CR = \frac{0.85 + 0.05}{0.10 + 0.05} = \frac{0.90}{0.15} = 6.00
$$

$$
C_{m} = \frac{0.85 - 0.10}{0.85 + 0.10} = \frac{0.75}{0.95} \approx 0.789
$$

> **Interpretation:** CR = 6 exceeds the WCAG AA threshold of **4.5 : 1**, so this combination passes for normal text. ✓

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Code Channel

| Information Type | Best Channel | Worst Channel | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Precise numeric value | **Text** | Color | Numerals carry exact meaning |
| Status (safe / unsafe) | **Color** | Text | Color is pre-attentive |
| Spatial location | **Graphics** | Text | Maps convey spatial relations |
| Cross-cultural warning | **Symbols (Pictogram)** | Text | Pictograms bypass language |
| Urgency level | **Redundancy: Color + Flash + Position** | Text alone | Multiple cues reduce error |
| Sequential procedure | **Numbered Text** | Graphics | Order matters |
| Direction of motion | **Animation + Arrow** | Static color | Motion signals direction |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Hick's Law for Choice-Reaction Displays

When an operator must choose among $n$ equally probable stimuli (e.g., warning lights):

$$
RT = a + b \cdot \log_{2}(n + 1)
$$

### Typical Values
- $a \approx 100$ ms (perceptual processing)
- $b \approx 150$ ms (motor response scaling)

### Worked Example
A control panel with **5 status lights**. How many milliseconds are added by increasing to **8 lights**?

$$
\Delta RT = b \cdot \left[\log_{2}(8+1) - \log_{2}(5+1)\right] = 150 \cdot \left[\log_{2}(9) - \log_{2}(6)\right]
$$

$$
\log_{2}(9) = 3.170 \quad ; \quad \log_{2}(6) = 2.585
$$

$$
\Delta RT = 150 \cdot (3.170 - 2.585) = 150 \cdot 0.585 \approx 87.7 \; \text{ms}
$$

> **Design implication:** Grouping similar lights into **sub-menus** reduces $n$ and thus reduces reaction time. This is why **aircraft ECAM** pages limit displayed warnings to ≤ 6 per page.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Visual Display Design Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Need] --> B[Information Type]
    B --> C{Numeric?}
    B --> D{Spatial?}
    B --> E{Status?}
    B --> F{Warning?}

    C -->|Yes| G[Text Display]
    D -->|Yes| H[Graphic Display]
    E -->|Yes| I[Color Code]
    F -->|Yes| J[Symbol + Color]

    G --> K[Apply Legibility Rules]
    H --> L[Apply Bertin Variables]
    I --> M[Apply ISO 3864]
    J --> N[Apply ISO 7010]

    K --> O[Character Height H = D/200]
    L --> P[Pre-attentive Vars]
    M --> Q[Redundancy Check]
    N --> R[Standardise]

    O --> S[Prototype Build]
    P --> S
    Q --> S
    R --> S

    S --> T[Operator Trial]
    T --> U{Pass KTU Metrics?}
    U -->|No| A
    U -->|Yes| V[Final Display]
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Coding Channel Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start([Display Signal]) --> Q1{Time to Decode?}
    Q1 -->|Under 250 ms| Q2{Color Available?}
    Q1 -->|Over 1 s| Q3{Complex Data?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| C1[Use Color Code]
    Q2 -->|No| C2[Use Shape Code]
    Q3 -->|Yes| T1[Use Text + Number]
    Q3 -->|No| G1[Use Graphic Icon]
    C1 --> Out1[Redundant Pair with Position]
    C2 --> Out1
    T1 --> Out2[Sans-serif, High Contrast]
    G1 --> Out2
    Out1 --> Final([KTU Compliant Display])
    Out2 --> Final
```

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Visual Display System Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SourceLayer[Information Source]
        Src1[Machine Sensor 1]
        Src2[Machine Sensor 2]
        Src3[Operator Input]
    end

    subgraph TransducerLayer[Signal Processing]
        P1[Encoder]
        P2[Multiplexer]
        P3[Code Mapper]
    end

    subgraph DisplayLayer[Visual Output]
        V1[Text Channel]
        V2[Graphic Channel]
        V3[Symbol Layer]
        V4[Color Code Layer]
    end

    subgraph OperatorLayer[Human Operator]
        H1[Visual Cortex Decodes]
        H2[Cognitive Interpretation]
        H3[Motor Response]
    end

    Src1 --> P1
    Src2 --> P2
    Src3 --> P3
    P1 --> V1
    P2 --> V2
    P3 --> V3
    P1 --> V4
    P2 --> V4

    V1 --> H1
    V2 --> H1
    V3 --> H1
    V4 --> H1
    H1 --> H2
    H2 --> H3
    H3 --> Src3
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Visual Coding Hierarchy (Pre-attentive vs Serial)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Visual Codes] --> B[Pre-attentive - Under 250 ms]
    A --> C[Serial Search - Above 500 ms]

    B --> B1[Position]
    B --> B2[Size]
    B --> B3[Color Hue]
    B --> B4[Orientation]

    C --> C1[Shape]
    C --> C2[Texture]
    C --> C3[Value Contrast]

    B1 --> D[Use for CRITICAL warnings]
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    B4 --> D

    C1 --> E[Use for Categorical info]
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
```
