# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Hierarchical Structure of Workplace Hazards

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Workplace Hazards] --> B[Physical]
    A --> C[Chemical]
    A --> D[Biological]
    A --> E[Ergonomic]
    A --> F[Psychosocial]
    A --> G[Mechanical]
    A --> H[Security Related]

    B --> B1[Noise and Vibration]
    B --> B2[Radiation]
    B --> B3[Extreme Temperatures]

    C --> C1[Toxic Fumes]
    C --> C2[Dust and Particulates]
    C --> C3[Corrosive Substances]

    D --> D1[Pathogens]
    D --> D2[Mold and Fungi]

    E --> E1[Repetitive Strain]
    E --> E2[Awkward Posture]
    E --> E3[Manual Handling Injuries]

    F --> F1[Workplace Stress]
    F --> F2[Harassment and Violence]
    F --> F3[Fatigue and Burnout]

    G --> G1[Unguarded Machinery]
    G --> G2[Pinch Points]
    G --> G3[Slip and Trip Hazards]

    H --> H1[Unauthorized Access]
    H --> H2[Theft and Sabotage]
    H --> H3[Information Breach]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (Modified for Ergonomics)

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[Layer 1: Engineering Controls] --> H1[Hole: Incomplete Guards]
    L2[Layer 2: Administrative Controls] --> H2[Hole: Skipped Procedures]
    L3[Layer 3: Training and Supervision] --> H3[Hole: Inadequate Training]
    L4[Layer 4: PPE] --> H4[Hole: Worker Non Compliance]

    H1 --> ACC[Accident or Incident]
    H2 --> ACC
    H3 --> ACC
    H4 --> ACC

    ACC --> RES[Outcome: Injury or Loss]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Risk Control Hierarchy (Ergonomic Decision Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Identified Hazard] --> Q1{Can we Eliminate It?}

    Q1 -->|Yes| EL[Elimination - Most Effective]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2{Can we Substitute It?}

    Q2 -->|Yes| SB[Substitution with Safer Alternative]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Engineering Controls Feasible?}

    Q3 -->|Yes| EC[Engineering Controls: Guards, Ventilation, Automation]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Administrative Controls Available?}

    Q4 -->|Yes| AC[Administrative: Training, Signage, Rotation]
    Q4 -->|No| PPE[PPE - Last Resort]

    EL --> DONE[Risk Reduced to Acceptable Level]
    SB --> DONE
    EC --> DONE
    AC --> DONE
    PPE --> DONE
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Integrated Safety and Security Management System

```mermaid
graph TD
    POLICY[Safety and Security Policy] --> PLAN[Planning: HIRA and Threat Assessment]
    PLAN --> DO[Implementation: Controls, Training, PPE]
    DO --> CHECK[Monitoring: Audits, TRIR, Incident Logs]
    CHECK --> ACT[Management Review and Continual Improvement]
    ACT --> POLICY
```

> [!NOTE]
> This loop is the foundation of **ISO 45001 (OH&S)** and aligns with the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** cycle demanded by KTU 2024 outcomes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define workplace safety. Mention any two physical hazards. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Workplace safety is the discipline of preserving and protecting human, physical, and environmental resources from accidental harm in the workplace. It involves identifying hazards, assessing risks, and applying controls. (2 marks)

Two physical hazards:
1. **Excessive noise** causing hearing loss
2. **Vibration** leading to Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome (HAVS) (1 mark)

---

### Question 2: What is PPE? Why is it considered the last line of defense? **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
PPE (Personal Protective Equipment) refers to wearable items — gloves, helmets, safety glasses, earplugs, respirators — used to protect the worker from residual hazards. (2 marks)

It is the **last line of defense** because:
- It does not eliminate the hazard at the source
- It relies on the worker wearing it correctly every time
- It is less reliable than elimination, substitution, or engineering controls (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Module-Style Question

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment (HIRA)** process. Discuss the Risk Control Hierarchy with suitable examples. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A manufacturing unit recorded the following data for the year 2024:
- Total recordable injuries: 18
- Total man-hours worked: 3,600,000 hours

Calculate the **TRIR** and interpret the result. List **three engineering controls** and **two PPE** items suitable for a welding workshop. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — HIRA Process and Risk Control Hierarchy [7 marks]

**Step 1: Define HIRA** (1 mark)
HIRA is a systematic procedure to identify hazards, evaluate the associated risks, and implement controls to reduce risk to an acceptable level.

**Step 2: Stages of HIRA** (3 marks)
1. **Hazard Identification** — Walk-through surveys, incident records, worker feedback, checklists
2. **Risk Estimation** — Using $R = S \times P$, mapping to a 5×5 matrix
3. **Risk Evaluation** — Compare against acceptance criteria (Green/Yellow/Red zones)
4. **Control Implementation** — Apply the hierarchy of controls
5. **Review and Monitoring** — Periodic audits, re-assessment

**Step 3: Hierarchy of Controls with Examples** (3 marks)

| Level | Method | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Elimination | Remove hazard | Replace solvent-based paint with water-based |
| Substitution | Use safer alternative | Use quieter machine (< 85 dB) |
| Engineering | Physical modification | Install machine guard, exhaust hood |
| Administrative | Procedures | Job rotation, safety signage |
| PPE | Wearable protection | Ear muffs, gloves |

---

#### Part (b) — TRIR Calculation and Welding Workshop Controls [7 marks]

**Step 1: Write the TRIR formula** (1 mark)

$$ \text{TRIR} = \frac{N \times 200{,}000}{H} $$

**Step 2: Substitute values** (1 mark)

$$ \text{TRIR} = \frac{18 \times 200{,}000}{3{,}600{,}000} $$

**Step 3: Compute numerator** (1 mark)

$$ 18 \times 200{,}000 = 3{,}600{,}000 $$

**Step 4: Divide** (1 mark)

$$ \text{TRIR} = \frac{3{,}600{,}000}{3{,}600{,}000} = 1.0 $$

**Step 5: Interpretation** (1 mark)
A TRIR of **1.0** is **better than the global manufacturing average (~2.8)**. The unit has a strong safety record, but **continual improvement** is essential.

**Step 6: Welding workshop controls** (2 marks)

*Three Engineering Controls:*
1. **Local exhaust ventilation** for fume extraction
2. **Automatic welding screens / curtains** to protect other workers from UV
3. **Flame-resistant welding booths** with proper grounding

*Two PPE items:*
1. **Auto-darkening welding helmet** (shade 10–13)
2. **Leather welding gloves** and **flame-resistant apron**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Discuss **Heinrich's Domino Theory** and **Reason's Swiss Cheese Model**. How do they complement each other in modern workplace safety management? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Workplace Safety and Workplace Security** with a comparative analytical matrix. Explain the **role of ergonomics** in integrating both domains. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution Outline to Question B

#### Part (a) — Domino Theory & Swiss Cheese Model [7 marks]
- **Heinrich's Domino Theory (1931)**: 5 sequential factors; removing one domino breaks the chain. (2 marks)
- **Reason's Swiss Cheese Model (1990)**: Multiple defensive layers; accidents occur when "holes" align across layers. (2 marks)
- **Complementarity**: Domino theory explains the **causal sequence**; Swiss Cheese model explains the **systemic failure** across layers. Modern OH&S uses both — sequence (Domino) within layers (Swiss Cheese). (3 marks)

#### Part (b) — Safety vs. Security & Ergonomic Integration [7 marks]
- Provide a **6-row comparative matrix** (Goal, Trigger, Methodology, Metric, Behavior, Regulation) as given in Section 3.4 above. (4 marks)
- **Ergonomic Integration** (3 marks):
  - Human-centered design addresses both accidental and intentional risks
  - Workstation layout considers emergency exits AND access control
  - Work-rest cycles prevent fatigue-induced security lapses
  - Training programs cover both safe acts and security vigilance

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse TRIR with LTIFR.** TRIR uses 200,000 base hours; LTIFR uses 1,000,000. Mixing these is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Hierarchy order is examinable.** Writing PPE first loses marks — always list in descending effectiveness (Elimination → PPE).
> 3. **In risk matrix questions, state the band (Green/Yellow/Red) explicitly** — examiners allocate marks for the interpretive step, not just the multiplication.
> 4. **Avoid generic "training" answers** without specifying type (e.g., "induction safety training" vs. "toolbox talk") — KTU rewards specificity.
> 5. **Do NOT skip units** in TRIR/LTIFR answers. Always write "per 200,000 man-hours."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Workplace safety** = preventing accidental harm; **Workplace security** = preventing intentional harm.
- The **5 hazard classes**: Physical, Chemical, Biological, Ergonomic, Psychosocial (plus Mechanical & Security-related).
- **HIRA** uses $R = S \times P$; standard 5×5 matrix with Green/Yellow/Red bands.
- **Hierarchy of Controls**: Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- **TRIR** = $\dfrac{N \times 200{,}000}{H}$ — industry benchmark < 3.0.
- **LTIFR** = $\dfrac{L \times 10^6}{H}$ — for serious/lost-time injuries.
- **Heinrich's Domino Theory** — 5 sequential factors; intervention at factor #3.
- **Reason's Swiss Cheese Model** — multiple defensive layers; align holes = accident.
- **Noise Dose (NIOSH)** ≥ 100% triggers mandatory hearing conservation.
- **ISO 45001:2018** — global OH&S standard based on PDCA cycle.
- **PPE is the LAST** line of defense, not the first.
- **Ergonomics** bridges safety and security by designing the human–work system.
- **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** is the backbone of all modern safety management systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assessment of risks in workplaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBIET604 - APPLIED ERGONOMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Assessment of risks in workplaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Workplace Risk Assessment** is the systematic, structured, and documented process of identifying, analyzing, evaluating, and controlling potential hazards in a work environment that could cause harm to human health, safety, or operational efficiency. It is a proactive occupational safety and health (OSH) management tool mandated under frameworks such as the **ILO-OSH 2001 Guidelines**, **ISO 45001:2018**, and the **Indian Factories Act, 1948 (Section 7A)**.

In the context of **Applied Ergonomics**, risk assessment specifically focuses on evaluating the mismatch between a worker and their task, tools, environment, and organizational system — quantifying the probability and severity of musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs), cognitive overload, fatigue, and accidental injuries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"Risk Assessment is the overall process of hazard identification, risk analysis, and risk evaluation, followed by the recommendation of control measures to reduce risk to an acceptable level (ALARP — As Low As Reasonably Practicable)."*

## 1.2 Key Terminology — The Ergonomic Lexicon

| Term | KTU-Standard Definition | Unit/Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Hazard** | Any source, situation, or act with the potential to cause harm | — |
| **Risk** | Combination of the likelihood of an occurrence and the severity of harm | Dimensionless score |
| **Severity (S)** | Magnitude of potential harm (injury/illness) | 1–5 scale |
| **Likelihood (L)** | Probability that the hazard will manifest into actual harm | 1–5 scale |
| **Risk Score (R)** | Quantitative measure of risk = f(S, L) | Number |
| **ALARP** | As Low As Reasonably Practicable — target risk level | Principle |
| **PPE** | Personal Protective Equipment (last line of defense) | Equipment |
| **MSD** | Musculoskeletal Disorder (key ergonomic outcome) | Health effect |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "House Inspection" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are buying a second-hand house. Before signing the deal, you call an inspector who walks through every room with a checklist. They look at the wiring (electrical hazard), check for damp walls (chemical/biological hazard), test the staircase railings (physical/mechanical hazard), and ask about neighborhood noise (psychosocial hazard). For each issue, they ask two questions:
> 1. **How bad could this get?** (Severity)
> 2. **How likely is it to happen?** (Likelihood)
>
> They then write a report ranking each issue and recommending fixes — re-wire the house, fix the damp, install railings. That entire process IS a risk assessment. Now replace the "house" with a "factory/workshop/office" and the inspector with an **ergonomist/safety officer** — you have workplace risk assessment.

## 1.4 The Risk Equation — Foundation Concept

The fundamental relationship in any risk assessment is:

$$
R = f(S, L)
$$

Where the simplest (and most KTU-tested) form is:

$$
R = S \times L
$$

A more refined version (used in **Fine-Kinney Method**, popular in chemical/industrial ergonomics) is:

$$
R = L \times E \times C
$$

Where **E** = Exposure frequency and **C** = Consequence (sometimes equal to S).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Risk Assessment

The entire risk-assessment workflow rests on four sequential pillars:

1. **Hazard Identification (HID)** — *What can go wrong?*
2. **Risk Analysis** — *How bad and how often?*
3. **Risk Evaluation** — *Is it acceptable?*
4. **Risk Control/Treatment** — *What do we do about it?*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Point:** Risk Assessment is a **cyclic, iterative process**, not a one-time event. ISO 45001 mandates re-assessment whenever there is:
> - A change in process/equipment
> - An incident/near-miss
> - New legal/regulatory updates
> - Worker complaints or health surveillance findings

## 2.2 Classification of Workplace Hazards

Workplace hazards — the inputs to risk assessment — are classified into **six major families**:

| Sl. | Hazard Type | Ergonomic Examples | Typical Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Physical** | Noise, vibration, radiation, temperature extremes | Hearing loss, vibration-induced white finger |
| 2 | **Chemical** | Solvents, fumes, dust, acids | Respiratory illness, dermatitis |
| 3 | **Biological** | Bacteria, viruses, moulds | Infections, allergies |
| 4 | **Ergonomic** | Repetitive motion, poor posture, forceful exertion, static load | MSDs, back pain, RSI, CTS |
| 5 | **Psychosocial** | Workload, monotony, harassment, shift work | Stress, burnout, mental fatigue |
| 6 | **Mechanical/Safety** | Unguarded machinery, slippery floors, falling objects | Acute injuries, fatalities |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Risk Quantification

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations for Part-A and Part-B numericals. Each carries 3–7 marks potential.**

| # | Formula / Method | Variables | Output / Range | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = S \times L$ | Severity (1–5), Likelihood (1–5) | Risk Score: 1–25 | Most common in board Qs |
| 2 | $R = L \times E \times C$ | Likelihood, Exposure, Consequence | Score: 1–1000 | Fine-Kinney method |
| 3 | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Severity, Occurrence, Detection | RPN value | FMEA method |
| 4 | $ER = \frac{\text{No. of lost-time injuries}}{\text{Total man-hours worked}} \times 10^6$ | Injury count, hours | Injury Rate | Safety statistics |
| 5 | $FAR = \frac{\text{No. of fatal injuries}}{\text{Total man-hours worked}} \times 10^8$ | Fatalities, hours | Fatal Accident Rate | Safety statistics |
| 6 | $IF = \frac{\text{No. of injuries}}{\text{No. of employees}} \times 1000$ | Injuries, employees | Injury Frequency | Safety statistics |
| 7 | $Severity\ Rate = \frac{\text{No. of days lost}}{\text{No. of injuries}}$ | Days lost, injuries | Days/injury | Safety statistics |
| 8 | $Acceptable\ Risk\ Ratio = \frac{Benefit}{Risk}$ | Benefit, Risk | Ratio | Decision analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> **Vertical Pipe Rule:** KTU examiners strictly prohibit the use of plain `\vert` symbols in markdown tables. When writing absolute values inside tables, ALWAYS use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid`.

## 2.4 The Risk Matrix (5×5) — The KTU Poster Child

This is the **single most-asked diagram** in Module 4. Memorize it perfectly.

| **Likelihood ↓ / Severity →** | **1 — Negligible** | **2 — Minor** | **3 — Moderate** | **4 — Major** | **5 — Catastrophic** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **5 — Almost Certain** | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 |
| **4 — Likely** | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 |
| **3 — Possible** | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 |
| **2 — Unlikely** | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
| **1 — Rare** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |

**Risk Acceptance Bands:**
- **Low (1–4):** GREEN — Acceptable, monitor only.
- **Medium (5–12):** YELLOW — Mitigate with controls within a defined timeline.
- **High (15–25):** RED — Immediate action, stop work if necessary.

## 2.5 Hierarchy of Controls (The "Inverted Pyramid" of Risk Treatment)

Listed from **most effective to least effective**, this is the universally accepted control hierarchy:

1. **Elimination** — Physically remove the hazard (e.g., remove the chemical from the process).
2. **Substitution** — Replace with less hazardous alternative (e.g., manual lifting → mechanical hoist).
3. **Engineering Controls** — Isolate people from the hazard (e.g., machine guarding, ventilation).
4. **Administrative Controls** — Change work procedures (e.g., job rotation, rest breaks, training).
5. **PPE (Personal Protective Equipment)** — Protect the worker with equipment (e.g., gloves, helmets).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "ES-EA-P"** → **E**liminate → **S**ubstitute → **E**ngineering → **A**dministrative → **P**PE. Always work top-down. PPE is the LAST resort, never the first.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Risk Assessment

Risk assessment drives decisions in:
- **Manufacturing:** Designing safer workstations (anthropometry-driven).
- **Construction:** Scaffolding design, fall-protection planning.
- **Healthcare:** Sharps handling, patient-transfer protocols.
- **IT/Software:** Usability risk, eye-strain, sedentary ergonomics.
- **Process Industries:** HAZOP, FMEA for chemical plants.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Risk Score Derivation (Worked Numerical — KTU Board Style)

### **Problem Statement (Model Question Type):**
> In a sheet-metal pressing workshop, an operator must manually lift a **15 kg metal blank 80 times per shift** from floor level to a workbench at waist height. The task involves **bending forward (trunk flexion > 60°)** and is performed **without assistance**. The supervisor wants to calculate the ergonomic risk score using the **NIOSH Lifting Equation (Revised)** and the **5×5 Risk Matrix**.

### **Given Data:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Load weight (L) | **15 kg** |
| Frequency (F) | 80 lifts / shift |
| Horizontal distance (H) | 55 cm |
| Vertical travel (V) | 95 cm (from 25 cm floor to 120 cm bench) |
| Trunk angle | 65° |
| Asymmetry (A) | 30° |
| Coupling | Fair |
| Severity (S) of MSD injury | Major → S = 4 |
| Likelihood (L) of occurrence | Likely → L = 4 |

### **Step 1: Compute the Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)**

The NIOSH Revised Lifting Equation is:

$$
RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM
$$

Where each multiplier is a dimensionless factor and **LC = 23 kg** (Load Constant).

**Identify each multiplier:**

- **LC (Load Constant)** = **23 kg**
- **HM (Horizontal Multiplier)** = $\frac{25}{H}$ where H is in cm.

  Substituting H = 55 cm:

$$
HM = \frac{25}{55} = 0.4545
$$

- **VM (Vertical Multiplier)** = $1 - 0.003 \vert V - 75 \vert$ where V is in cm.

  Substituting V = 95 cm:

$$
VM = 1 - 0.003 \times \vert 95 - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \times 20 = 1 - 0.06 = 0.94
$$

- **DM (Distance Multiplier)** = $0.82 + \frac{4.5}{D}$ where D = V travel in cm = 95 cm.

$$
DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{95} = 0.82 + 0.0474 = 0.8674
$$

- **AM (Asymmetry Multiplier)** = $1 - 0.0032 A$ where A is in degrees.

  Substituting A = 30°:

$$
AM = 1 - 0.0032 \times 30 = 1 - 0.096 = 0.904
$$

- **FM (Frequency Multiplier)** for F = 80 lifts/8 hr and V < 75 cm ≈ 30 cm lift origin. From NIOSH table, FM ≈ **0.40** (multipliers change with vertical origin; for V origin > 75 cm, FM at 80 lifts ≤ 8 hr ≈ 0.57; at ≤ 75 cm = 0.40 — we adopt the more conservative lower vertical origin).

$$
FM = 0.40
$$

- **CM (Coupling Multiplier)** for Fair coupling = **0.95**.

### **Step 2: Substitute All Values into the RWL Equation**

$$
RWL = 23 \times 0.4545 \times 0.94 \times 0.8674 \times 0.904 \times 0.40 \times 0.95
$$

Compute the product step-by-step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
23 \times 0.4545 &= 10.4535 \\
10.4535 \times 0.94 &= 9.8263 \\
9.8263 \times 0.8674 &= 8.5234 \\
8.5234 \times 0.904 &= 7.7052 \\
7.7052 \times 0.40 &= 3.0821 \\
3.0821 \times 0.95 &= 2.9280
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
RWL = 2.93\ \text{kg}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logical interpretation:** The NIOSH safe limit for THIS specific lift geometry and frequency is only **2.93 kg**. The actual load is **15 kg** — vastly exceeding the safe limit.

### **Step 3: Compute the Lifting Index (LI)**

$$
LI = \frac{\text{Actual Load Weight}}{RWL} = \frac{15}{2.93}
$$

$$
LI = 5.12
$$

**Interpretation bands (NIOSH):**
- LI ≤ 1.0 → Safe (most workers can lift without increased risk)
- 1.0 < LI ≤ 3.0 → Increased risk; administrative/engineering controls needed
- LI > 3.0 → **Unacceptable risk**; task redesign mandatory

Since LI = 5.12 ≫ 3.0 → the task is **unacceptable**.

### **Step 4: Compute the Risk Matrix Score**

Using S = 4 (Major MSD — could be disabling back injury) and L = 4 (Likely — given 80 cycles/shift over years):

$$
R = S \times L = 4 \times 4 = 16
$$

From the 5×5 matrix: **R = 16 falls in the RED (High) zone** → Stop work immediately, implement control measures.

### **Step 5: Compare Both Methods — Validate the Conclusion**

| Method | Output | Risk Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| NIOSH Lifting Equation | LI = 5.12 | UNACCEPTABLE |
| 5×5 Risk Matrix | R = 16 | HIGH (RED) |

**Both methods agree → Risk is unacceptable. Action required.**

### **Step 6: Recommend Hierarchy-of-Controls Solutions**

1. **Elimination:** Redesign the process so blanks arrive at bench height via conveyor.
2. **Substitution:** Replace manual lift with pneumatic pick-and-place arm.
3. **Engineering Controls:** Install scissor-lift table to bring blanks to waist height; reduce H from 55 cm to 25 cm.
4. **Administrative:** Introduce job rotation (max 30 lifts/shift per worker), 10-min microbreaks every hour.
5. **PPE:** Provide lumbar support belts only as a temporary supplement.

After intervention, **re-compute** LI and R to confirm acceptable levels.

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Risk Method

| Method | Best For | Output Type | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **5×5 Risk Matrix** | General workplace hazards, quick screening | Score 1–25 | ★★★★★ |
| **NIOSH Lifting Equation** | Manual material handling tasks | Lifting Index (LI) | ★★★★★ |
| **RULA (Rapid Upper Limb Assessment)** | Sedentary/postural tasks (office, assembly) | Grand Score 1–7 | ★★★★ |
| **REBA (Rapid Entire Body Assessment)** | Whole-body postural analysis (healthcare, construction) | Score 1–15 | ★★★★ |
| **FMEA (Failure Mode & Effects Analysis)** | Complex systems, design-stage risks | RPN value | ★★★ |
| **HAZOP** | Chemical/process plants | Deviation scenarios | ★★★ |
| **Bow-tie Analysis** | High-consequence / low-frequency events | Barrier-based | ★★★ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Risk Assessment Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: Identify Hazards] --> B[Step2: Determine Who Might be Harmed]
    B --> C[Step3: Evaluate the Risks S x L]
    C --> D{Risk Acceptable?}
    D -- No --> E[Step4: Record Findings]
    E --> F[Step5: Implement Controls ES-EA-P]
    F --> G[Step6: Review and Monitor]
    G --> H{New Hazard or Change?}
    H -- Yes --> A
    H -- No --> I[Document and Audit]
    D -- Yes --> I
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchy of Controls (Top-Down Effectiveness)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Elimination - Most Effective] --> L2[Substitution]
    L2 --> L3[Engineering Controls]
    L3 --> L4[Administrative Controls]
    L4 --> L5[PPE - Least Effective]
    L5 --> L6[Worker exposed to residual risk]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Risk Matrix Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z1[Low 1-4 GREEN Acceptable] --> Z2[Medium 5-12 YELLOW Mitigate]
    Z2 --> Z3[High 15-25 RED Stop or Redesign]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Inputs to Risk Assessment (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Workplace Observations] --> R[Risk Assessment Engine]
    P2[Worker Feedback] --> R
    P3[Incident Records] --> R
    P4[Legal Standards] --> R
    P5[Anthropometric Data] --> R
    R --> O1[Quantitative Risk Score]
    R --> O2[Qualitative Risk Rating]
    R --> O3[Control Recommendations]
    R --> O4[Documentation Report]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram — Fine-Kinney Risk Method Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Likelihood L] --> M[Multiplication]
    B[Exposure E] --> M
    C[Consequence C] --> M
    M --> R[Risk Score R = L x E x C]
    R --> D{R Score Range}
    D -- 1 to 20 --> D1[Negligible]
    D -- 20 to 70 --> D2[Low]
    D -- 70 to 200 --> D3[Medium]
    D -- 200 to 400 --> D4[High]
    D -- More than 400 --> D5[Very High]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1: Define Risk Assessment. List the four main steps involved in it.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Risk Assessment is the systematic process of identifying hazards in the workplace, analyzing and evaluating the risks associated with those hazards, and determining appropriate control measures to reduce the risk to an acceptable level (ALARP).

**Four Main Steps (2 Marks — ½ Mark Each):**

1. **Hazard Identification** — Recognizing anything that may cause harm.
2. **Risk Analysis & Evaluation** — Calculating $R = S \times L$ and comparing against acceptance criteria.
3. **Risk Control** — Implementing the hierarchy of controls (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE).
4. **Monitoring & Review** — Periodic re-assessment, especially after changes or incidents.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Hazard and Risk with one workplace example each.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Hazard | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition (1 Mark)** | A source or situation with potential to cause harm | The likelihood that harm will actually occur, combined with its severity |
| **Nature** | Intrinsic property (always present) | Function of exposure, probability, and consequence |
| **Workplace Example (1 Mark each)** | A wet floor near a staircase | A worker slipping and fracturing a wrist on the wet floor |

**Key Sentence:** *"The hazard is the wet floor (always dangerous); the risk is the actual chance of someone falling and getting hurt (varies with conditions)."*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — With Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the 5×5 Risk Matrix in detail. Construct the matrix and describe how risk score is calculated and interpreted.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition (2 Marks):**
A Risk Matrix is a graphical tool that combines **Severity (S)** and **Likelihood (L)** of a hazard to produce a numerical **Risk Score (R)**. It is the most widely used qualitative-quantitative technique in workplace safety management and is mandated by ISO 45001 and OHSAS 18001.

**2. The 5×5 Matrix Construction (3 Marks):**

| **Likelihood ↓ / Severity →** | **1 — Negligible** | **2 — Minor** | **3 — Moderate** | **4 — Major** | **5 — Catastrophic** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **5 — Almost Certain** | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 |
| **4 — Likely** | 4 | 8 | 12 | 16 | 20 |
| **3 — Possible** | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | 15 |
| **2 — Unlikely** | 2 | 4 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
| **1 — Rare** | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |

**3. Risk Score Formula (1 Mark):**

$$
R = S \times L
$$

**4. Interpretation Bands (1 Mark):**
- **1–4:** LOW (Green) — Acceptable, monitor.
- **5–12:** MEDIUM (Yellow) — Mitigate within a defined time.
- **15–25:** HIGH (Red) — Stop work, immediate controls.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A factory worker is exposed to a chemical solvent with Severity rating = 4 (Major — chronic liver damage) and Likelihood rating = 3 (Possible — exposure twice a week due to a leaking gasket). Calculate the Risk Score, classify the risk level, and recommend at least three controls from the hierarchy.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Compute Risk Score (2 Marks):**

$$
R = S \times L = 4 \times 3 = 12
$$

**Step 2: Classify Risk (2 Marks):**
R = 12 falls in the **MEDIUM (Yellow)** band → Mitigation required within a specified timeframe (e.g., 30 days). Engineering controls recommended before PPE.

**Step 3: Hierarchy of Controls (3 Marks):**

1. **Engineering Control:** Replace the leaking gasket with a sealed flange system; install local exhaust ventilation (LEV) at the solvent station.
2. **Administrative Control:** Reduce exposure frequency to once a week via batch scheduling; provide training on safe solvent handling; introduce a health surveillance program (annual liver function tests).
3. **PPE (Last Resort):** Provide chemical-resistant nitrile gloves, splash goggles, and organic-vapour respirators until engineering controls are operational.

**Conclusion:** Even though R = 12 is "Medium," the high severity warrants a proactive response to prevent escalation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE ALTERNATIVE** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the NIOSH Revised Lifting Equation. Define Lifting Index and state its interpretation bands.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Background (1 Mark):**
The NIOSH (National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health, USA) Lifting Equation, revised in 1991, evaluates manual lifting tasks. It computes a **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** under given task conditions and compares it with the actual load.

**2. The Equation (3 Marks):**

$$
RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM
$$

Where:
- **LC** = Load Constant = **23 kg**
- **HM** = Horizontal Multiplier = $\frac{25}{H(\text{cm})}$
- **VM** = Vertical Multiplier = $1 - 0.003 \vert V - 75 \vert$
- **DM** = Distance Multiplier = $0.82 + \frac{4.5}{D(\text{cm})}$
- **AM** = Asymmetry Multiplier = $1 - 0.0032 A(\text{degrees})$
- **FM** = Frequency Multiplier (from NIOSH table)
- **CM** = Coupling Multiplier (0.90–1.00 depending on grip quality)

**3. Lifting Index (2 Marks):**

$$
LI = \frac{\text{Actual Load Weight}}{\text{RWL}}
$$

**4. Interpretation Bands (1 Mark):**

| LI Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| LI ≤ 1.0 | Safe — most workers can lift without increased risk |
| 1.0 < LI ≤ 3.0 | Increased risk — controls needed |
| LI > 3.0 | Unacceptable — task redesign mandatory |

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A worker lifts a load of 18 kg with horizontal distance 40 cm, vertical distance 60 cm, asymmetry 20°, frequency 4 lifts/min for 2 hours continuously, and good coupling. Calculate RWL and LI. Comment on the risk and suggest controls.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Identify Multipliers (2 Marks):**

- LC = 23 kg
- HM = 25/40 = 0.625
- VM = $1 - 0.003 \times \vert 60 - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.045 = 0.955$
- DM = $0.82 + \frac{4.5}{60} = 0.82 + 0.075 = 0.895$
- AM = $1 - 0.0032 \times 20 = 1 - 0.064 = 0.936$
- FM (for 4 lifts/min × 120 min = 480 lifts, V < 75 cm, duration 2 hr) ≈ **0.50**
- CM (good coupling) = **1.00**

**Step 2: Compute RWL (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
RWL &= 23 \times 0.625 \times 0.955 \times 0.895 \times 0.936 \times 0.50 \times 1.00 \\
&= 23 \times 0.625 = 14.375 \\
14.375 \times 0.955 &= 13.728 \\
13.728 \times 0.895 &= 12.286 \\
12.286 \times 0.936 &= 11.500 \\
11.500 \times 0.50 &= 5.750 \\
5.750 \times 1.00 &= 5.75
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
RWL = 5.75\ \text{kg}
$$

**Step 3: Compute LI (1 Mark):**

$$
LI = \frac{18}{5.75} = 3.13
$$

**Step 4: Interpretation & Controls (2 Marks):**
LI = 3.13 > 3.0 → **UNACCEPTABLE risk.** Recommended controls:
- Reduce load below 5.75 kg OR introduce two-person lift / mechanical hoist.
- Lower the frequency and introduce 10-min micro-breaks every hour.
- Re-engineer the workstation to reduce horizontal distance below 25 cm.
- Provide manual handling training and lumbar-support PPE as temporary measure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up Hazard vs. Risk** — Marks are deducted if students use them interchangeably.
> 2. **Wrong vertical-multiplier sign** — VM uses absolute value; forgetting $\vert V - 75 \vert$ gives wrong answer.
> 3. **Using PPE as the FIRST control** — Always start with Elimination; PPE is the LAST resort.
> 4. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — Always write "kg," "cm," "lifts/min," etc.
> 5. **Forgetting to re-compute after controls** — A common 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Drawing matrix without interpretation bands** — The bands are mandatory; examiners scan for them.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Risk Assessment in 60 Seconds":**
>
> - ✅ Risk Assessment = HID → Analysis → Evaluation → Control → Review (cyclic).
> - ✅ **Hazard** = potential for harm; **Risk** = probability × consequence of that harm.
> - ✅ Core equation: $R = S \times L$ (5×5 matrix → score 1–25).
> - ✅ Extended equation: $R = L \times E \times C$ (Fine-Kinney).
> - ✅ NIOSH: $RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$; $LI = \frac{Load}{RWL}$.
> - ✅ Hierarchy: **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE** (ES-EA-P).
> - ✅ LI ≤ 1 safe; 1–3 increased risk; > 3 unacceptable.
> - ✅ Risk Matrix: 1–4 Green, 5–12 Yellow, 15–25 Red.
> - ✅ Six hazard families: Physical, Chemical, Biological, Ergonomic, Psychosocial, Mechanical.
> - ✅ Always **re-assess** after controls, incidents, or process changes.
> - ✅ ISO 45001 / Indian Factories Act 7A mandate risk assessment documentation.
> - ✅ Qualitative (Matrix) vs Quantitative (NIOSH, RULA, REBA, FMEA) — choose based on task type.
> - ✅ ALARP principle: reduce risk "As Low As Reasonably Practicable."
